What I wanted to write about is a that the woman who hosted us announced that she likes to ask everyone who attended the birthday to say something nice about the birthday person. It’s my favourite tradition, and it was so nice to see someone do it.

If you’re reading this, I would encourage you to do so at your next get together. This is doubly true if you’re male, it’s so rare to hear compliments or truthful statements from people who care about you. You’ll be amazed by what people say, and in our materialist lives, it’s often the best present you’ll get.

title: Crypto
text:
We’re so back.

Ky and I have been putting together our financial planning for the future, and have decided that we’re going to put 90%+ of our savings into low fuss investments. Savings accounts. ETFs. Boring stuff that works out over time.

But that’s not 100%, which allows us to have a measured dance in the wild roadhouse which is cryptocurrency. I’m slowly refining my strategy, but as for now I’m thinking a 50/30/20 split between Ethereum, Bitcoin, and Solana.

I’m also trying to figure out the best way to manage this situation. Ky and I have a Ledger Cold Wallet (I may write about that tomorrow) to store and stake our coins. But in terms of exchanges, we haven’t nailed one down. The trade offs inherent in all of them are hard to decipher.

Kraken, for example, is low fee, but requires you to wait 7 days until you can withdraw. Coinbase, a competitor, allows quick withdrawal, but higher fee. Binance, perhaps the most famous extant exchange, offers fast withdrawals AND low fees, but doesn’t allow Fiat deposits. It’s also based in China and has some shadowy figures.

One of my favourite idioms is “crypto is astrology for me”, and call me wet laundry because you have me picked. We’re not expecting it to go to the moon, although that would be nice, but it tickles an itch without causing any potential catastrophe.

Are you in crypto or do you just deal in cuck bucks? Let me know, especially if you have a solution for US based exchanges.

title: Miss me?
text:
Yesterday I wrote a post about how Fall was in the air, but forgot to hit publish! Alas, my over 250 day streak was broken.

It is a shame, but one that is rectifiable by the method my friend Dan recommended, which is to always be writing tomorrow’s blog post today. I think it might be time I pursue that, although I do really love just hitting publish and calling it a day, although obviously it isn’t a guaranteed thing!

title: Out of the park
text:
Today was a great day work wise. No promotion, no pay rise, no massive accomplishment. Today will pass like many others.

What was different was I pumped out, what I think, is an awesome PRD for our next feature, and I can’t wait to share it with the team tomorrow. This is the first step of something big, or so I hope.

Fingers crossed.

title: Updates
text:
I write about 80% of my posts on my phone, although 90% of my “effort posts” are typed up on my laptop. No surprises as to why.

Today is a rare example of writing a shorter post on my laptop, and when I do this, I often take the time to update the plugins that keep this website and thus project running. Despite being an incredibly simple website, I am constantly surprised by how many updates I need to do each time; it is often five or six a fortnight. They’re small, with each update usually taking <10 seconds to process, and usually, I never notice a single change.

I am also a big fan of the small, frequent updates that software allows. With each change, the difference is so marginal that I barely notice, but over two or three years, the product is significantly better.

These marginal improvements are going around all around us.

Be grateful.

title: AFL Grand Final
text:
I am not a nostalgic type, but I make an exception for the AFL Grand Final. As I wrtie this, the game has just started, and it looks like a GLORIOUS day in Melbourne. Those early days of Spring are such a special time in the Garden State.

To celebrate, Ky and I have got a little creative. For dinner, we decided to go super kitsch and make ourselves some sausage rolls. They came out really well, although, like all things American, they were HUGE.

Unfortunately, the extensive Denver beer scene hasn’t discovered the joys of Carlton Draught, so I had to make do with the great sin of Fosters. To take the edge off it, it did come in the Super Can (the same size as a wine bottle!), so that was an interesting innovation.

Same as last year, I’ll be supporting the Lions. I’m not against,South Melbourne Sydney Swans, but a successful Brisbane Lions = a healthy competition.

Bring it on!

title: iTalki
text:
After 264 days of studying French on DuoLingo, I realised I wanted to take it up a notch, so today I did my first lesson on iTalki. iTalki is a platform that allows language learners to connect with teachers from across the world, and for $7, I was able to do a 30 minute trial lesson with Chloé, and boy, it was good value!

Duo is a good tool for vocab, and a handy way to improve your reading and writing. However, it’s not great for listening, and useless for talking. Since my goals are more oriented around ordering some nice cheeses than reading Le Petit Prince, it made sense to focus on that.

My lesson with Chloe was very well organised. She asked me to speak as much French as I could, and as I did so, she furiously tapped away at her keyboard. What she was doing was transcribing everything I was saying, and after three minutes of struggling through, we went through the transcript together.

It was great to see the mistakes I had made in real time, and each mistake usually became a mini-lesson. When to use “de”. How to pronounce “un”. What better ways to say I come from Australia.

I have already booked my next lesson.

title: The College Game
text:
Last night, while I was chilling at home, I was flicking through the channels and found a college football game between Alabama and Georgia. Now, for those who are into college sports, this might be one of the biggest games of the season, but for the uninitiated, like yours truly, it was just a distraction on a quiet Saturday evening.

Well, it was until the final quarter, and very quickly I realised I was watching what might not just be the game of the year, but game of the decade.

My American readers may strongly disagree with my following comments about college sports, so if you do, please let me know by replying to this email or leave a comment. I read and reply to them all.

Pro Football can be better described as a chess match between two coaches, who are trying to outsmart and outwit their opponents using brute force and canny plays. At the college level, it just looks like a wild free-for-all.

First off, in college football, there seems like there is next to zero defence, which explains why the average college team scores 30% more than the average NFL team. Mistakes happen more frequently, and younger players are far less likely to have mastered the mental game. Playing on ESPN in front of 80k people at 18 years old will do that for you.

I don’t want to be a spoiler, but I would recommend watching the highlights of the game. Even for someone with no appreciation of the rules, teams, or sport, I still had a good time.

title: Pumpkin Patch
text:
Last Saturday, Ky and I went to a pumpkin patch to pick up a decorative gourd. It wasn’t a half bad experience!

Many people are fascinated by farms. I’m one of them, as are the millions of people who tune into Clarkson’s Farm. Even David Beckham has decided that the best way to plonk his cash post football is into feeding hens, although I’m convinced that while he might have a face for television, he has a voice for newspapers, and thus a media career would never really work.

On our visit, we saw some extremely non-plussed rabbits, some cows, some goats, and a chicken. We then went on a tractor ride, and saw some corn fields. It was ok. Finally, we went into the cornfield maze, which was actually really well done, but the constant twisting and turning made the two of us semi-nauseous, so after 20 minutes, we decided to use a drainage disk to escape. Mission accomplished?

And of course, we ended up picking up a pumpkin to take home. Overall, it was another great day in the Centennial State!

title: Anti-vax
text:
First off, I’m of course not anti-vax when it comes to ALL vaccines, but I tell you what, this latest TDAP has me going down the rabbit hole.

Ok, you’re probably freaking out now, so let me add some context.

To protect our upcoming child, I recently got the TDAP (tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis) vaccine. Oh, and it was $50, thanks Obama. But it’s not the cost that bothers me, it’s how it’s made me feel like I’ve taken a cricket bat to my right shoulder. It’s sore as buggery, and I’ve developed some pretty ratchet symptoms, from poor sleep to an upset stomach.

I’m clearly not against vaccinations, and see them as a modern day miracle. However, I can see why others might not be such a fan. Plus this is the first time I’ve ever felt bad having one, so it must be the microchip.

title: Out from the back
text:
When do you give up on an optimal strategy?

That’s a great question, and one that I have been thinking about quite a bit about recently, especially since learning a little more about soccer and the tactics that seperate the contenders from the pretenders. I am no expert, but the one thing I have half an idea about is “playing out from the back”.

In soccer/football, when an opposing team puts the ball out of play on the goal line, the goal keeper restarts play by kicking it out. Until recently, the most common strategy, whether you’re playing under 8’s or in the Premier League, was to hoof it as far as you can up field. And why not? After all, the odds of you being scored against when the ball is 60 meters from your goal border on zero.

However, in recent years, a balding European who manages an elite Manchester based football club has decided that the best way to play is from “out the back”, or in simple terms, playing a short pass to your nearest defender. The upside is immediate; you control possession. The downside is equally visible; you trade off possession. 13 trophies in 6 years suggests that it may be working though.

It looks like lots of teams have tried to replicate it, but very few have conceded. In fact there are compilation videos made every week of lesser skilled teams trying to execute this tactic, failing, and promptly conceding. In a game where a single goal is enough to win, it’s a massive risk.

So, when do you quit a tactic that works for the best, but might not work for the rest? It doesn’t have to be sporting either, plenty of things that work at McKinsey won’t work at McDonalds.

I’d be interested to hear your thoughts.

title: Terrific
text:
Three weeks ago, our dishwasher started making some strange sounds. We called maintenance, and employing an interesting strategy, they decided to take the misbehaving part. Interesting, how so? Well, they didn’t replace it, so we went from a noisy dishwasher to an ineffective one.

There is something mildly infuriating about playing Russian Roulette with a faulty dishwasher. Somethings come out find, others come out not just dirty, but those stains are now infused thanks to their 90 minute steaming. Terrific….

Three weeks of constant calls and emails, and it turns out the part is no longer made. Again, terrific. But then, the story takes a positive turn, as management decided to just replace our dishwasher with a brand new one. It takes ~20 minutes more per cycle, but it’s silent, and best of all, it is silent.

Terrific.

title: Spring Clean
text:
Our house is probably going to get quite messy in a few short weeks. The optimal strategy would be to relax your standards now, after all, why bother polishing the dunny when there’s going to be a turdinator 3000 in the building?

Instead, Ky and I cashed in our discount voucher and booked the 3 hr deep clean of our place. Being able to come back to a home which is clean, not just tidy, is a pleasure that would have been unrecognisable to a younger me.

Very content.

title: La fin, pour l’instant
text:
Aujourd’hui, je n’étudie pas pour la première fois en 271 jours. Pourquoi? Espagnol est mon parler préféré maintenant.

J’aime français, et j’adore France. Mais, l’opportunité ici aux États-Unis est petite. A l’avenir, nous ne voyageons pas au Le France, et mon esprit de corps pour étudier est une peu.

Tous les jours, je deux parler espagnol ici. L’Etats-Unis est un pays perfect, il y a beacoup hommes et femmes viennent à le Mexique .

Today, I did not study French for the first time in 271 days. Why? Spanish is my preferred language now.

I like French, and I love France. But the opportunity here in the USA is small. In the future, we are not travelling to France, and my ~enthusiasm~ is small.

Every day, I can speak Spanish here. The USA is a perfect country; there are many men and women from Mexico here.

title: Everyone needs a hobby
text:
I have been thinking of getting back into an old hobby for a while, and today, while Ky was at yoga, I decided to make a gentle return to the world of magic. Denver isn’t a well known destination in the conjuring arts, but the store I went to, and the man behind the counter, reminded me why I was so into it when I was younger.

Doug, the man running the store, was super helpful. I explained my situation: how I had once been quite the enthusiast, but it had been almost 10 years since I was regularly practicing and performing. He didn’t blink, asked a few questions, and after about 45 minutes, sent me home with a hardcover book and a few packs of cards.

I haven’t read or used a magic book before, preferring in the past to use YouTube as my main source. However, I am glad I went down this path. Firstly, magic, like any artistic or creative pursuit, has a base layer of technique and theory which are very much worth knowing. Think of the differences between a classically trained pianist vs a self trained guitarist, or someone who has trained in French cooking vs just following a few recipes online. That deeper knowledge of the basic techniques goes a long way, and the book I got, Card College Vol 1, is one of the true classics of the art.

I don’t think I’ll ever make my bread and butter through illusions, but even just spending some time with someone who had a passion for the art was a buzz. We discussed our favourite magicians, and various philosophies on performance. While I’m so rusty even a double-lift might be noticed, but now that I am armed with my book, I think I’ll be back there in no time.

Hopefully I’ll be able to show you something cool when I see you next.

title: One year on
text:
It’s been a year since Hamas invaded Israel, and while I would like to say things have got better, they really haven’t. Despite that, I thought it would be worthwhile exploring the good, the bad, and the ugly.

The Good: Honestly, not much, but Israel has shown itself to be a resilient nation, both militarily and culturally. Prior to the war, Israeli society was a mess. Multiple elections were called, and politics were at a stalemate. Netanyahu, the Prime Minister and Head of State, was looking at corruption charges. The public was being rocked by controversial changes to the judicial system. All was not well in the land of Zion.

But after the invasion, everyone got in line. People enlisted by the tens of thousands, including many overseas volunteers. We see lots of nationalism done badly, the Israelis know how to do it well.

It has also been promising to see the support from Israel’s allies. The US, as always, has been leading the support with logistics, cash, weaponry, and intelligence. I personally believe that troops should be deployed, but acknowledge that is a minority opinion. Other countries have been lacking, the UK, despite having a number of citizens imprisoned as hostages, could do more.

The Bad: My mother’s partner had the best take on this, almost 10 months ago: “No matter what Israel does, they have lost the war”. This was during the ground invasion of Gaza, and today, it feels even more true today. Over the years, the Middle East was becoming more peaceful. Saudi and Qatar were liberalising, Dubai had become the hotspot for young influencers who wanted to party. Syria and Iraq weren’t doing well, but they had kicked out ISIS, and Iran was just sulking in the corner. Now, it’s all out war. Israel has invaded Gaza. It has been sending rockets into Lebanon, and they’ve been sending it back. Iran is also in the rocket game, and they may have nukes.

Regardless of what happens, there has been enough that violence and destruction that victory is impossible. A bomb may kill three terrorists, but it creates 10 more as friends and family are understandably radicalised. Peace is going to be hard to find.

The ugly: Two-fold. You can support Israel’s right to defend itself, and not be overjoyed by mass bombings. There are many Palestinians who just want to live in peace and quiet, and are instead burying those close to them. It isn’t great to see the escalation. Israel has launched a ground invasion on Lebanon. It could get worse, with militia attacks coming from Iraq, Yemen, Syria, and Iran. Rockets are already flying, and there have been rumours of a naval invasion of Iran. At that point, all bets are off, and we’re looking at a WW2 level conflict in the region. Not good.

The other sad thing to see is the rise of anti-semitism. I spend too much time on X, so I see some really nasty stuff, and it has infiltrated the left and the right. It takes different forms, on the left, it is a broader, softer, more elite focussed anti-semitism. It’s protests on campus. It’s wearing the keffir at Columbia. It’s posting stuff on Instagram.

On the right while the leaders have been strong, the rank and file have been nasty. The ancient trope of Jews being global meddlers trying to destroy Western nations is back, and just as awful as ever.

Lots of people will have some hot takes on how we can solve the conflict NEXT WEEK if all goes well, but those people are lying to you, stupid, or both. Things will probably get worse before they get better, but there is still opportunities every day where we can effect change for the better.

title: Personal Brand
text:
It occured to me that while the world of professional sports is so heavily focussed on brands, it is interesting that one area has been largely ignored, the coach, with some exceptions.

This idea came to me while thinking of the University of Colorado head coach, Deion Sanders, who prefers to go by term “Prime Time”. While this is a moniker he picked up while playing professionally (in BOTH the NFL and MLB), but since becoming a coach, he has leant into it even more. The result? People think he’s the second coming of Jesus down at UC, despite a 4-8 record.

Across the pond, the same happened with a well dressed, quick talking Portugese called Jose Mourinho. He dubbed himself “The Special One” in a conference many moons ago, and that has stuck. Yes, he has won multiple trophies, and coached the top teams in the world, but his winning percentage of ~64% is significantly lower than that of current Man City manager Pep Guardiola (74%).

In the eyes of the casual fan, coaches with nicknames are usually massively overrated, producing mountains of articles from more informed fans screaming into the void saying “No, just because you call yourself [insert superlative here], doesn’t make it true [insert wall of text as to why which no one ever reads]”.

So, should managers focus more on personal branding? As usual, the answer is “it depends”. Do you have charisma, aka rizz? Are you comfortable with being the centre of attention? Do you accept the upsides and downsides, knowing that wins will be more celebrated, but at the same time millions of people are licking their lips for the “Special One gets sacked” headlines to be released?

For most people, it is a no. But for some, the Special Ones, the answer is a massive yes.

title: Morning routine
text:
There is a lot of discussion in the productivity world around morning routines. Exercise first thing, or journal? Ice bath? Compulsory. High fat breakfast or intermittent fasting? The comment section becomes a war zone.

There is only one morning routine which I like, have followed, and found useful. Alex Hormozi is a business and productivity “guy”, but I have found a lot of his stuff super useful in the past.

His morning routine is simple: spend as little time as possible between waking up and getting to work. He adds a few extras: black coffee, no food, noise cancelling headphones, and nicotine. I drop the nicotine and make sure I spend 2 hours before caffeine thanks to Huberman’s research, but otherwise, I think it works for me.

It certainly did today.

What’s your go to morning routine?

title: Northern Lights
text:
Tonight was one of the most active Northern Lights events in quite some time. If you found yourself within this area of the map, there was a reasonable chance you could see them.

Unfortunately, despite being in the zone, we didn’t see them. Being downtown didn’t help, but we drove out 40 minutes into the boonies, and unfortunately, still nothing.

It wasn’t all doom and gloom, having a drive is a nice way to spend some time together, but a few colours would have been nice.

Some people were far luckier!

title: Catch me if you can
text:
Ky and I watched the movie Catch Me If You Can this evening. It’s a good one.

While possibly a bit too long, and the plot line with the dad didn’t really fit in, it’s a hell of a story, and Leo DiCaprio does a wonderful job. Is all of it true? Perhaps not, but I wouldn’t let that get in the way of a good yarn, I don’t.

If you’re looking for a good flick, I recommend it.

title: Misplaced Reputation
text:
Today, we ventured out to the notorious Aurora, known for its lawlessness and violent Venezuelan gangs. In the same way Springfield, Ohio is known as the home of cat-eating Haitians (or so they say…), Aurora is the Caracas of the Mountain West.

If that’s the case, the gangs must have taken a long weekend, because we had nothing but a terrific time, loading both ourselves and our car up with wonderfully priced fuel. Fuel is under $3 out here (RIP my NZ readers, as yes, that is the gallon price, and equates to $0.79 a litre), and the place we went for lunch, New Canton, was just the best.

There is something that I love about cheap and cheerful Asian restaurants, and this one was right there. It had everything, the menu on the wall with the new prices stuck up next to them on paper tape. The “Chinese only” menu handwritten in Mandarin. A Polaroid picture of a whole roasted pig with a phone number next to it. A calendar from 2001. A waving mechanical cat. Staff that completely ignore you. A row of caramel roasted ducks hanging in the window. And absolutely delicious food that is served mere moments after ordering.

The only thing missing was the 14 year old kid who was taking orders while doing his homework at the table, butcher paper on the tables, and a bathroom where you have to walk through the owner’s living room to get there.

If you’re in the area, check it out.

https://maps.app.goo.gl/tv8Gu4u4RarShfzD7?g_st=com.google.maps.preview.copy

title: Number 1
text:
Two weeks ago I switched from French to Spanish, and while hesitant, I realised that I had made the right decision.

Firstly, SO much of what I learned in French translates right over, which has been super helpful. Secondly, the structure of Spanish, both in terms of speaking and writing, feels more straightforward. Maybe that statement will come back to bite me, but I’m sticking to it for now.

As for proof, I ended up finishing this week’s DuoLingo league as #1 out of 25, so that is nothing to be sniffed at. Vamos!

title: Welcome to the world!
text:
Yesterday, Ky and I welcomed our first child to the world, Octavia Bari Jones Wetherall. It’s a bit of a mouthful, so we just call her Occi (pronounced Oc-ki) for now. Perhaps she’ll be a Tavie, Ava, O, or Tavs in future. Who knows? A lot of my high school friends call me Menez, and a bunch of my Queensland pals still call me Meaty. Nicknames are great and unpredictable. Maybe I’ll write about them in the future.

You probably want to know how it all went down. Fair enough, it’s quite a story. To do it justice, I think I’ll split it into three parts, the labour, the delivery, and postpartum, starting tomorrow.

Apart from those posts, I’ll try my best to try and avoid writing about her. Mostly to keep it interesting to my readers, and also to keep a part of world seperate from the all consuming nature of fatherhood.

As for now, so far so good. Baby and mum are doing just fine.

There is so much to look forward to.

title: Labour
text:
I guess it started at the H-Mart.

In what feels like a lifetime ago, Ky and I were spending a quiet Sunday in Aurora picking up a few noms and lunch. As we drove back, I distinctly remember us discussing how frustrating it was that baby hadn’t arrived yet, and how Ky had already burned through a week of maternity leave. Little did we know.

Arriving back home, Ky had an inkling her waters may have broken. It’s certainly not like the movies, where a tsunami arrives instantly and without warning, and initially neither of us were convinced. Deciding that we’d rather be safe than sorry, and more importantly, with nothing of note planned in the afternoon, we headed off to the hospital to get a second opinion.

Once we arrived and checked in, the nurses did a quick test to see what the matter was, and within two seconds the results came back. Dark blue. The baby was on its way.

Before all of this happened, I was under the impression that “labour” was the period of time where you were pushing and squeezing. I mean, it does, but it’s like saying that flying in a plane is just the landing sequence.

A few thoughts on this period:

Drugs work: as someone who has had very limited experience of pharmaceuticals beyond what you can buy over the counter, I was AMAZED at both how effective and how quick the drugs at the hospital are. At one point, while Ky’s contractions had begun in earnest, she was in so much pain I thought she was going to pass out. Cue a single shot of fentanyl (provided by the hospital, not the sketchy guys in the car park). Within 30 seconds of it going into her bloodstream, it had completely kicked in. Combine that with an epidural, and Ky was able to sleep overnight with no issues at all!

Hitting the hay was easy for me, and after 7 hours, I woke up the next day for the big event, delivery. You’ll read about that one tomorrow.

title: Delivery
text:
When Ky and I woke up on Monday morning, the first thing I heard was that she was 3cm dilated (the goal is 10) and it was all systems go. Today was the day. We were going to become parents.

It started slowly. Over the next two or three hours, it was simply a waiting game, as Ky’s body prepared for The Big Show. The list things one can do during this time isn’t long, but fortunately, “take a nap” is on it. I took one, and I’m glad I did.

Eventually, the show started, and Ky started to push. It’s hard work, and my only advice is that when they say push, what they really mean is PUSH. It reminds me of my first experience water skiing, where I tried for half an hour, unsuccessfully, to get up. My issue was trying to optimise my technique, when in reality, I just needed to grip that bar like I was the third monkey on Noah’s Ark, and it had just started to rain.

1 hour is a quick delivery. 2 hours is now reaching well past the average, and 3 hours is considered long. As you can imagine, when hour 4 ticked over, things weren’t looking so great. Ky was more tired than a one armed bricklayer in Beirut, and while there was some movement at the station, it wasn’t entirely clear what would unfold next.

Fortunately, it was at this moment when Dr Peterson (the obstetrician, not the psychologist) rolled in, and announced in no uncertain terms that this baby was coming out.

Have you ever watched Pokémon or played a game where the “boss music” plays during a particularly intense scene? Well, that’s what happened here. In the space of five minutes, our two nurses were joined by the doctor, a few back up nurses, a table of sharp implements, some industrial grade lighting, and a can do attitude.

The journey from womb to the outside world isn’t long, and it took over four hours to get halfway through. The last few inches only a few seconds, as Dr Peterson essentially crowbarred the baby out with her two hands; no need for her to do arms day this week.

That 30 second period will be one I won’t forget, and once we announced the gender (it’s a girl!) and cut the umbilical cord (think “calamari”), we had done it. Well, Ky had done it, I was just a semi-helpful spectator.

Would I recommend it? Hard to say. We had great care, but it was still a tough experience, and the downside risk remains very real. However, we do have a kid who is (mostly) lots of fun, so you have to weigh that in as well.

A crazy day.

title: Postpartum
text:
So, we did it. Or at least Ky did it, and I cheered her on. Now, it was time for some well earned rest.

We honestly couldn’t have had a better time at Rose Hospital. After the delivery, we spent two days in the hospital, which originally I thought was overkill. Two days! I want to go back to my bed and have a proper snooze.

Turns out it couldn’t have been a better decision. The combination of having all meal provided, unlimited tea and coffee, and most importantly, medical staff on hand to answer all our questions.

Breast feeding is hard, although it doesn’t look it from the outside. It requires a bit of technique and a willingness to do the “love shove”, when you essentially launch bib’s mouth onto the waiting nip. It isn’t exactly “self-evident”.

Overall, a great experience, from the moment we arrived to the moment we were led out of the hospital.

Also, they cart the babies around in little trolleys, which I like to call Pizza Delivery. It’s very amusing to see.

So ends the baby trilogy, we’re back to regular programming tomorrow.

title: Reverse sear
text:
Cooking a steak is easy, but cooking it well can be a challenge.

The issue lies in the fact that a deliciously cooked steak requires two components; a juicy red interior, cooked to medium rare, and a caramelise crust on the exterior, courtesy of the Maillard reaction. Seems simple enough, but to achieve this, you can easily find yourself in a paradoxical situation. Cook at a high heat, you’ll get the nice crust, but the interior will be too rare, and you’ll have a large grey brand from the high heat. Not the end of the world, but not great.

Alternatively, you can cook it low and slow, and allow the meat to come up to temperature. Great medium rare, but the flavor from the caramelised crust is missing, and the texture is off.

It seems like you can’t win, but you can, if you split the process in half. Introducing the Reverse Sear.

Preheat the oven to 120c/250f, and put your steaks in. For a 0.5lb steak, 30 minutes should be good. You’re aiming for 110-115 internal.

Once they have reached it, pull them from the oven and get your pan hot. I use cast iron, and so should you, but stainless steel works as well. Get it to a cracking heat, put down some oil (people recommend a high smoke point but I just use OO), and sear them for 1 minute each side.

Once you flip once, turn off the heat (the pan will have plenty of residual) and chuck in two garlic cloves and some butter. Once the butter melts, spoon it over the steak and baste it. The garlicky, buttery, nutty addition is well worth it.

After that, you’re done! Pull them, let them rest for 5 minutes (you don’t need as long as you would for other methods) and enjoy.

This evening we paired it with mash and steamed veg, and it was great to get back to date night.

title: Catch up.
text:
For some reasons, the daily emails haven’t been coming through. I’m very sorry, especially as I think there have been a few bangers recently. Here are the latest ones that haven’t gone out, and I’m prioritising tomorrow to get it fixed. My apologies

Article 1: Delivery. Part 2 of my trilogy on the birth. My personal fave.

Article 2: Postpartum. The finale of the trilogy.

Article 3: Reverse Sear. A deep dive into the technique I used to make steak night a success.

title: I’ll have fries with that
text:
Over the weekend, Donald Trump added another career path to his collection, including real estate mogul, tv star, WWE contestant, and of course, president. It was the most American of jobs: working the drive-thru window at McDonalds.

From an optics point of view, I think it looks good. Despite being a billionaire (or so he says), he does a Clintonian way with regular people which drives a lot of his popularity.

Courtesy of the New York Post

Trump seems to be enjoying himself on the train, a sleeper indicator that he might be doing better than the polls suggest. National polls still put the election at a knife edge, but after this move, it certainly hasn’t his hurt his chances.

Harris needs to make her next move; it’ll be interesting to see what she does.

title: Coding away
text:
Looking after a baby means that your list of hobbies can’t involve hiking the Inca Trail on a lark, but that doesn’t mean that I don’t have ANY time to do interesting side projects.

Today, after putting it off for a while, I got back to my software project. It is small scale, and will remain unknown to all but JT (you know who you are), but the outcome isn’t important, the process is.

You don’t have to have cancer to be an oncologist, but I think it’s worthwhile for anyone who works in the software space to be somewhat aware of how the sausage is made. And it is also kind of fun, not in a heli-skiing the backcountry kind of way, but more like solving a jigsaw puzzle, or a massive sudoku.

I look forward to attacking it again tomorrow.

title: Watch this space
text:
My sports watch hit the hay last week, so I was in the market for a new one.

In the past, I have usually gone with the classic black Swatch, but over the years I suspect they have downgraded their quality. They used to be terrific, but now, while still handsome and affordable, I just can’t trust them.

On this occasion, I decided to trial out the Apple Watch. I managed to find one on the Wild West of Capitalism, Facebook Marketplace, and met “Gyzmo”, a former vet turned cross dresser who was about to move to the Arizona desert to meditate for an as yet to be determined time. Not exactly a candidate for Apple, hence the bargain.

We both walked away chuffed.

Do I think the Apple Watch is a game changer? Not really. I feel there is functionality that I’m leaving on the table, and it needs to be charged a LOT. But, it does track my sleep, steps, and movement, and I have noticed it nudging me very slightly towards a more active lifestyle.

It won’t be my next watch, although I have the six and they’re past ten in terms of series, so maybe the newer ones are amazing (big doubts here). However, for the price, I could be doing a lot worse.

title: Bake the cake
text:
Our next door neighbour is making his NBA debut today, and we wanted to celebrate for him! Not knowing what exactly he might want, we decided to go for something everyone like, Chocolate Cake.

Although he’s just a rookie, he has the famous number 23 on his jersey, which, fun fact, was the same number I wore in high school football. It’s a good omen.

I cook all the time, but rarely bake, so it was a good experience. It’s less prone to free styling, which is a shame, but the results speak for themselves. They were delicious (we ate the one on the left with some friends this afternoon).

title: Halloween Party
text:
Our apartment building is hosting the annual Halloween party, and it has been the best one so far. Not that I really know, we haven’t been to many, and this one we only just popped into, mostly to grab a few free snacks and beers.

It would be nice to go down and be social, but after a full week of looking after baby, we think we might just hit the hay.

title: RAM it in
text:
I’ve been seeing a bit of a lag in computer performance recently, which I simply do not have time for. That isn’t controversial; I know you are exactly the same.

The worst thing is that I wasn’t asking the world; all I wanted to do was share my zoom screen while having a few tabs open and maybe Excel. Not exactly 4k editing while running a bitcoin mining operation. Not only was it getting worse, but it was threatening to spill over into my professional work, which was the red line.

I considered upgrading my laptop, especially with the holiest of American holidays, Black Friday, around the corner, but figured I can solve 90% of my problems by just upgrading my RAM. Now, I don’t know the specifics of it, but think of it like an extra engine, except it only costs $30.

Opening up a laptop case to see the components is like writing code into the command prompt; it makes you feel smarter than you are, accompanied with more than a whiff of “if I screw this up, this thing is toast”.

Fortunately, the RAM is in, there has been no explosions or even smoke, and the engine(s!) are now running like a charm.

title: Micro Centre
text:
My friend and I are working on a project together, essentially building a safety deposit box that has a timer attached. In our ideal world, you could set the safe for a few days, months, or even years, and when the timer runs out, the safe opens.

We have some ideas around it which we will let you know at a later date, but for now, the most interesting thing is being able to put the plan into action. To do that, we went down to Micro Centre, the electronics superstore, to find some parts for our prototype.

It’s an interesting experience, and one that may not last long. Buying individual electronics parts, like bread boards, regulators, microprocessors, and more is becoming less common as people shop online. Radio shack has already hit the pavement, and I feel this will too soon.

However, while it lasts, we had a blast. The staff were surprisingly knowledgeable, but it was the other customers in the aisle who were the most helpful. They knew their way around, what parts to buy, and even had some war stories from the hey day of electronics retailing.

We ended up with our loot, and have booked in next weekend for the assembly. We will keep you posted, so watch this space.

title: Back to new
text:
My wife got me a large wooden chopping block a few years ago. Some may think that’s a weird present, but I love it. Already it has become a unique reflection of my chopping style, with the slice marks at a very particular angle, focussed on the right hand side of the board, exactly where I cut most of my ingredients.

Recently, it was looking a little worse for wear. The board had begun to warp slightly, making the experience both unpleasant and unsafe. It looked like I might need to replace it.

Turns out I was wrong; with wood, all you need to do is give it some love. I found a wood oil online, ordered it, and two days later I gave the board a good lathering. I repeated it again the day after, and now the board is good as gold, and straight as an arrow.

Shout out to Clark’s for the oil!

title: Pat on the back
text:
Paternity leave came to an end on Sunday, and I’m back to work.

Some of my non-American readers may be shocked, perhaps even appalled, by the two weeks. I prefer to follow the path of gratitude; at the end of the day, my employer is early stage, lacks funding, and didn’t have to offer me anything. When I ran my own small business, there is no way we could have paid for one of our staff to take two weeks off, so for Innovera to give me the time was nice.

The other reason to be grateful is that until 15 minutes ago, Paternity Leave was like Double Pay Birthday, a perk that was rare, bordering on the mythical. My dad didn’t have paternity leave, neither did Ky’s, and they either worked for massive corps or were self-employed. It just wasn’t the done thing.

It isn’t like things were easier back then, in fact, like much of life, they were harder.

It was nice, scratch that, critical that I had that time to support Ky, bond with Octavia, and get a rhythm going. It would have been nice to still be off (a friend of mine is taking 12 months). But I will also say that it is good to be back, and that no amount of leave trumps an accommodating employer.

It’s a good wicket.

title: Waffle Wednesdays
text:
Like most people, I am in a collection of group chats with friends. It’s a nice, low effort, always on way to stay up to date with mates, even if you’re separated by oceans, responsibilities, and time zones.

They rise and fall in activity, with short bursts of activity, followed by longer periods of dormancy. Friday afternoon is usually a boom time, as is Sunday night. Depending on how many of your friends are unemployed, Tuesday mornings are usually quiet.

The chat is usually memes, updates, some banter around fantasy football every now and then. However, today I was pleasantly surprised by the fact one of the guys wanted to create a “Waffle Wednesday” tradition, where every Wednesday we would just do a short video blog about our life, nothing serious, just a waffle. I think it’s a great idea, and cool to see one another.

Talking into the camera isn’t natural, but hopefully over time it gets better. Waffle on!

title: Sub-zero
text:
After 7 or so months, we are back to where we started. Last night the mercury hit sub-zero (Celsius that is, we’re not that bad yet), and only rose above around 8 this morning.

One thing I have learned from living here is that temperature is only one part of it; equally important is humidity when it comes to feeling the cold.

The best example I can think of is London. I have walked around the UK in 5 degree weather and thought I was going to freeze solid. The wetness and the mizzle causes the cold to really make itself feel present on you. It’s horrible, especially when it’s cloudy and gray (which it almost always is).

Meanwhile, if you have ever been to a place like Zurich, you can be in -5 degrees, and as long as it isn’t windy and the sun is shining, it can be super pleasant. The dryness of the air sort of insulates against the cold, and while you’re not going to go sunbathing, you can sometimes find yourself down to a tshirt after a few minutes of brisk walking.

Luckily, Denver is more of the latter.

title: Finish strong
text:
This was my first week back from Pat Leave, and I came out of the blocks swinging. Unfortunately, a few of them didn’t land, and things weren’t heading the way I was hoping. Never a nice feeling.

Good news is that today, knocked it out of the park, and set up next week nicely.

If you can make any day a winner, why not Friday?

title: Reals over feels
text:
I had the strangest sensation when I woke up, hazily, this morning. It was 7 am ish, and only a few hours since I had been up helping with bub. I wasn’t 100% with it, that’s for sure.

I briefly considered watching my fave Premier League team in action, as they had the early kick off against Arsenal. But when I saw the score was 4-0, in the wrong direction, with 30 minutes to go, the choice to go back to bed was an easy one.

Well, it turned out that 4-0 was simply a figment of my imagination, pulled from dream world, and Newcastle got the chocolates 1-0.

Trust but verify, even in the dream world.

title: Roganaining
text:
With only three days left until the election, things are getting tight. I’ll write a longer pre and post election piece this week, but today I wanted to capture my thoughts on “podcasting as an electoral strategy”. Or in the common tongue, should you go on Joe Rogan if you want to win?

I’m an infrequent listener to Joe, despite what my personal demography may suggest. He is certainly good at what he does, but a lot of guests just don’t really interest me too much, and three hours is a lot to commit to.

However, there is no denying he is good at what he does. Trump went on his show last week, and I listened to the whole thing. It was ok, I guess, and it made me consider the whole thing from an electioneering perspective.

The never trumpers and the ultra MAGA types have already made their mind up, and Joe Rogan isn’t going to change that. But there are still a few undecideds out there, and a good showing can cause a move in the needle.

Personally, I thought it was the right thing for Trump to do, but he didn’t put in a great performance. Age has caught up with him, and what he calls “the weave” is actually just a ton of rambling. I wasn’t super impressed, but hats off to entering the arena, and I wish Harris had done the same.

A few days ago, JD Vance, Trump’s running mate, went on the show. I read Vance’s book, but even so, he is still a bit of an outsider, and the Harris ticket have done a good job as painting him a “weird”.

Well, I’m a little under an hour in, and I’m very impressed. The whole thing about JD is he is super smart, and that comes out in the interview. But he is also very personable, and because of his age (he turned 40 recently), he is quite relatable. Policy wise, he’s not always cup of tea, and I find myself on the left AND the right of many of his core beliefs. However, I think he may be a political star in the not so distant future.

Skip the Trump interview, but give the JD Vance a listen to with an open mind.

title: The case for optimism
text:
Tomorrow is the US Election. Most people, both in America and around the world, are holding their breathe, and not in a good way. Despite the BRAT attempts, the levels of joy in the air are low.

I don’t think that should be the case. Regardless of who wins, there are reasons to be optimistic.

If Harris Wins: the American people will have broken a ceiling that was considered unimaginable even 40 years ago. A half-black, half Indian, raised in Canada, married to a divorced Jew woman is the leader of the Free World. It’s the ultimate victory of Martin Luther King’s dream, of judging people by the contents of their character. It won’t be, nor will it feel, as historic as the Obama election, but it’ll still be an unforgettable day.

A Harris presidency should bring, hopefully, a sense of calm, and banish the worst elements of the Republican Party to the dustbin of history. After failures in 2018, 2020, 2022, and now 2024, it will become clear to the grownups in charge that if the GOP wants to be successful, the Trump approach is not a winning approach. In short, it’s going to have to grow up.

A Harris presidency for us will hopefully mean more compassion for women’s reproductive rights, and ideally some efforts to to lower prices. America’s inflation is the envy of the world right now, but everyone like lower prices. Finally, a world without tariffs, or armed officers going door to door trying to deport families, is a better one.

If Trump wins: it will be a lesson to all about the value of not giving up. Regardless of your thoughts of him, he has spent almost a decade at this point being hounded by the press, the courts, social media, and has survived not one, but two assassination attempts. He still bears the scar of a bullet that almost killed him, and would have sent this nation ablaze. He lost the election of 2020, and has come back stronger. It’s genuinely impressive.

Yes, he has a unique capacity to self-fund, but that wealth also must whisper to him at times; “don’t go on the campaign, spend your final few years on this 200ft yacht, you deserve it”.

A Trump presidency, in my estimation, will lead to a more peaceful world. Feel free to screenshot this and haunt me for years, but with the world seeing ever escalating violence in the Middle East, Ukraine, and perhaps Taiwan, I think Trump’s “art of the deal” approach to foreign policy actually seems to work. I could be eating my hat, but for now, I think he might be the great peacemaker. Who ever would have thought that?

The previous Trump economy roared. My wife and I are financially secure, but a few extra dollars is nothing to be sniffed at, and I like his idea of a government efficiency task force. I don’t mind paying taxes, but there is so, so much waste. Hopefully a Trump presidency would improve that, although I don’t hold my breath.

Finally, immigration. It’s the big one, and as an immigrant (and a serial one at that!) I am obviously never going to endorse a blanket ban on migration. However, a country has the right to decide who comes in, under what conditions, and for how long. It cost my wife and I thousands of dollars to migrate legally to the US, and we came here to contribute, obey the law, and flourish. Not one American have we met have ever, ever, had an issue with us.

I’m naturally pro migration for personal and economic reasons, but I also recognize that privilege insulates me from the downsides of immigration. The residents in my apartment building are not part of Venezuelan cartels (at least not that I’m aware of). My friends and family are not impacted by the metric tons of fentanyl that pour over the border. I don’t see pro-Hamas flags waving in the street.

There is nothing wrong with people wanting an end to ILLEGAL immigration, and as someone who grew up in a country where such migration is virtually zero, I feel that it genuinely improves the conditions of those who are legal migrants.

Will we see the wall? Perhaps. Will it be the worst thing that has ever happened? No.

Regardless of who wins: we will almost certainly see the sun rise on Wednesday, people will go to work, go to school, and spend time with their loved ones. The great American experiment continues to roll on, and anyone who thinks that this is the last election is either lying to you, a fool, or most likely, both.

Who I support: this is super easy; I support the President. Whether it is Harris or Trump, I genuinely wish them the best. It’s a tough job, and by them doing a good job means a better outcome for everyone.

Both are flawed candidates, but in some strange universe, were I ever to get call from the Oval Office requesting my help, I would offer it.

title: Trump Triumps
text:
It is 10pm on Tuesday night, midnight Eastern. ABC, Fox, nor CNN have called it, but I have. It looks like Trump will be the 47th President of the United States.

I picked him as a winner a little while ago, but I didn’t think it would be a done deal before the dawn. To make it even more incredible, it looks like DJT is going to win the popular vote was George W Bush in 2024. It is an impressive victory.

How did this happen? Here are my three thoughts jotted down:

The Never Trumpers cried wolf: Since the election began, I think Trump’s best campaign slogan would have been “you don’t have to like me to vote for me”. It is clear that Donald Trump isn’t a Christ-like figure in character, and he says some pretty wild things from time to time very regularly. However, we have already seen Trump v1, and I think a lot of people thought it wasn’t too bad. In 2016, there was a feel that things would get very dark, but what unfolded, for a lot of people who aren’t political junkies, was actually quite good. Lower prices, higher wages, and no new wars is a pretty good deal for most people.

Harris was not a strong candidate: I follow politics quite closely. I read a lot of articles, and around 70% of my Spotify listens are politically themed. Despite that, I don’t really know what Harris stands for. To be fair, she had a tough run; she only had a short time to campaign after being thrust into the spotlight after Biden defenestrated himself on National Television. My sympathy is limited though; Harris did a poor job in her 2020 Primary Bid, and I find it hard to believe that she would have become a nominee anytime soon.

She played a delicate act trying to present herself as the heir apparent to Biden, while also trying to announce herself as her own person. That was always going to be a tough battle, and her unwillingness to speak off script hurt her. Would a visit to Joe Rogan have paid dividends?

The Issues were on Trump’s side: Each election is about the Economy+. By that I mean the Economy is always the key issue, but there are always a few more. Unfortunately for Harris, they didn’t line up for her.

Immigration and Crime are major motivators for people, and they see it every day. It doesn’t bother me, because I’m insulated from it, but not everyone is. Trump has a lock on these two issues, and that will have helped a lot.

The key issues for Democrats feel like Climate Change, Trans Rights, and Abortion. Abortion is a motivating issue, but Trump was clever enough to take it off the table. Climate Change is a luxury issue. Most people support the environment – no one likes seeing rubbish in the ocean or pandas going extinct – but it usually ranks lower. I’ll expand upon this with a funny story tomorrow. Finally, Trans Rights. I won’t go into it, but the Democrats hoped that it would be the same vote winner that Gay Rights were in the 2000s. It is clearly not.

Finally, it is the Economy Stupid. Inflation really sucks, and people remember the days under Trump when things were cheaper. Yes, the indicators all suggest things are getting a lot better, but the economy for most people aren’t numbers of a screen, it is how they feel at the servo, the supermarket, on payday, and on rent day.

title: A funny political story
text:
Forgive me if I have told this story before, but yesterday I promised to share with you my experience on how electorates see “key” issues.

It was almost 10 years ago, and I was taking a breadth subject in politics; a welcome relief from my micro-economics lectures. Out lectures often had guest speakers, and on this occasion, we had a campaign manager from one of the major parties come in and discuss the strategies of a successful election effort. I really liked him, he was gruff, no nonsense, and a straight shooter, unlike the vast majority of students in the room who probably wished to emulate him.

He started his speech with a command, then a question. “The secret to winning an election is figuring out what people want, and then making them feel like they’ll get it if they vote for you. Now, you’re all smart (doubt), what do youse reckon people care about?”

A irritatingly earnest girl in the front row thrust her arm into the air, mimetically impersonatingg a muggle Hermione Grainger. “The environment!” she gleefully pronounced, blissfully unaware, albeit momentarily, of her naïveté.

“Nope” fired back the manager, “that’s wrong”. The honesty was equal parts refreshing to startling. “Maybe out here in leafy Parkville, but out there in “Punter-Land”, people don’t give a shit about the environment”.

I was awed. Was this guy, in the words of Gen Z, fr? The manager realised he may have gone overboard, and walked back his statement a little. “Well, actually, people do care about their environment, but by the environments, they mean no dog turds down at the local park. I’m sorry, but people might like tigers walking around the forests of Sumatra, but they sure as shit don’t vote for them”.

He was truly a Churchillian figure.

I think there’s wisdom to be mined from this brief but memorable exchange. Punter Land is big, and they decide elections.

title: Where did the policy go?
text:
I was thinking just this afternoon, why didn’t I hear a word about healthcare policy in the recent election?

I heard a lot about the economy, and abortion, and racism, and fascism, and free speech, and tariffs and even whether Haitian migrants were eating “tha dawgs”.

What I didn’t hear, from either candidate, was a word on healthcare, unless RFK jr was in the frame.

It feels strange, but I don’t think anyone has noticed. Perhaps Obamacare worked after all?

title: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times
text:
Ky and I joined a cult today. Like all cults, they don’t describe themselves as one, but it’s definitely a cult. They lure you in with a “too good to be true” message, and boom, you’ve signed your life away, at least for a year.

I’m talking, of course, about CostCo.

I could talk day about first trip there, but instead I wanted to focus on the signup process.

For the uninitiated, CostCo is a members only supermarket. It’s not exclusive, any household that can muster together $65 can join. However, the process of getting said membership, or more specifically, the membership card, is a hassle.

We went in person, having already signed up and paid online. Surely this would only take a few minutes now that we have arrived. No. Very much no.

First off, the line. It moved slowly, which we just put down to incompetence on the side of the individual, not the corporation. How many times have you had to check-in in person at an airport where each person takes 5 minutes+ to be served, and when you turn up, you’ve completed everything within 45 seconds? In our instance, this was not the case.

The lady who eventually served us provided equally the best and worst customer service of our lives. On one hand, she talked us through every deal, and how to maximize our membership. She had recently even acquired a car through CostCo, wouldn’t you know, but her husband wanted a particular car that wasn’t available, so they had to go to Nebraska to pick it up.

Along with explaining what deals were available, she also told us the following:

 * Which church she goes to at 9am on Sunday
 * Which church she goes to at 11am on Sunday
 * How much she dislikes credit scores (while simultaneously trying to sign us up for the store credit card)
 * How she had to supplement her child during breast feeding
 * How the CostCo app works
 * Which aisle the coffee beans are in

Really, she was a wonderful person, but the experience REALLY shouldn’t have taken 45 minutes.

We have the cards now, so it’s one and done, but for those who are considering joining CostCo, perhaps go at 11am on a weekday.

title: Cook book
text:
I have never really fell in love with a cook before. I’ve used some before, but often find that they have a few bangers, and the rest of the book is just a waste of space.

Sometimes the recipes are too complex, or have a style which I don’t really gel with (Jamie Oliver, for example).

But I think I may have found a winner, as this week Ky brought home the official recipe book from the author of RecipeTinEats, and so far we have worked through two meals. Both of them have been easily top 15 we have made this year; one was a hot honey crunch salmon, and the other was a laksa that came together in 30 minutes.

I haven’t been this excited about cooking for a while, and we have challenged ourselves to tick off all 150 recipes within the next two years.

title: Snow day
text:
We had, apparently, one of the biggest snow dumps in 20 years last week, but I have my doubts to its veracity.

I am, however, fully confident in the Coloradans’ capacity to build with snow.

There were a ton of things people had built, but I wanted to highlight the smallest and biggest ones. They are efforts I will remember for a while.

The world’s smallest snowman My first igloo

title: Perspective
text:
I had an early call today to a potential client in Tel Aviv.

“How was your day”, I asked casually as we broke the ice.

“Great”, he replied, “only two bombings this week”.

Everything really is a matter of perspective.

title: DOGE
text:
As someone with an above average (not difficult) interest in fiscal policy, in the peak Elon fanboy demographic, and an enjoyer of big bets, I was pleased to see Trump’s appointment of Elon Musk and shit-stirrer extraordinaire Vivek Ramashwamy to the newly created Department of Government Efficiency, aka DOGE.

Doge, for those who don’t know, is also the name of a meme-coin, which is a cryptocurrency with no underlying value bar the fact that it is connect to a meme. It should be worthless, but in the past month has soared over 250%. This is going to be difficult to explain to the grand kids.

Back to the department, I think it is excellent that a government is putting together a taskforce to address waste and inefficiency. Listening to Elon speak about it earlier, it was reassuring to hear that this wasn’t some arch conservative “slash and burn” approach; Mr Musk was a big believer that regulations and certain social programs were fundamental to a healthy, thriving society. What is anathema to this is the time, money, effort and possibility that is wasted by inefficiency and mismanagement.

There are over 450 agencies in the US Federal Government, meaning they’re growing at almost 2 a year. More agencies means more complexity, which slows things down. As an example, Elon said that the first thing they do when they build a new rocket is ask for a permit from the government, and by the time they design, test, build, and prepare the rocket for takeoff, they then have to wait sometimes months for the paperwork to be filed. That isn’t great.

DOGE could be a complete mess, or it could be forgotten about by Thanksgiving next year. But I like people who are brave enough to try something new, even if it sounds crazy.

title: Date night
text:
Ky and I had our first date night this evening since Occi was born. It was two hours, but it was great.

Kylene’s old company runs an alumni event every year in each city they operate out of. It’s a pretty sweet gig to be invited to; technically they’re looking for clients, but we’re just there for the free drinks.

It was so nice to get out, dressed up, and have some adult conversation. Highly recommended!

title: A good week
text:
How do measure if you had a good week or not? Do you use a checklist or quantitative approach, idealized as “my goals are to do X, Y., and Z; if I do, that’s a success”.

Or is it a feeling you have on Friday evening where you think, well, that was time well spent, even if you didn’t knock off every goal.

I really screwed up going to the gym, but I got a lot of work done, and as someone who really enjoys it, that’s a positive. I’ll make up for the skipped sessions over the weekend.

title: Duolingo Max
text:
I have been gifted three days worth of DuoLingo Max, which is the AI powered version of DuoLingo.

It’s a great addition. It unlocks features like video chat, where I can chat with an avatar in Spanish, both verbally and written. It is certainly a step up from what they already have.

However, this benefit comes at a cost, and right now, the additional price isn’t worth it. If it comes down, maybe, but regular Duo is enough for me at the moment.

title: Lark at the park
text:
We have had two family outings to the park this weekend, and each time we saw some rather peculiar things.

On Saturday, we saw an orthodox Jewish couple walking the lake in the opposite direction to us, an entirely unremarkable occasion. Or at least it was until a runner, who for reasons unknown, deemed it necessary to scream “Shabbat Shalom” to the couple as he jogged by. It was very unusual.

Today we went to a different park, and within 200 yards of one another we saw a Furry convention and a particularly heated game of quidditch. Again, very unusual, but thus is life living in a free country.

I think it’s great.

title: Politics on the Edge
text:
That’s the name of the book I have just started reading, by former British MP Rory Stewart. I’m only a few pages in, but so far, it looks great.

It comes highly recommended, so I will get back to you when I am finished.

title: Done is better than perfect
text:
It’s late.

I’ve been working for hours on trying to do something which I thought would be relatively straightforward, but turned out to be anything but.

I looked on forums, asked two different LLMs, and spent hours seeing if I could knit something together quite nicely. Turns out, I should have just made something ugly, something that was clunky, but something that worked.

title: 600 Club
text:
The St Catz Rugby Club just ticked over 600 followers on social media, which is clearly cause for a celebration. Whether they’re university clubs, or sporting clubs, and especially university sporting clubs, that usually includes having a pint.

Or in this case, 600. Despite not pulling on the blue and claret jersey for a while, I’m still part of the group chat, and I’m proud to say that the boys pulled it off. 600 pints is quite the achievement to complete in 24 hours, but determination, grit, and the average student’s iron cased liver is not to be underestimated.

Well done lads!

title: Day One
text:
It’s the start of the Australian Test Summer, and I’m pumped! It’s not only on at the perfect time (7:30pm start) but it’s against one of my favourite teams to see, India.

It’s just hit lunch, and it looks like the Indians have really struggled. The pitch isn’t doing a huge amount, but the bowling from Australia just looks sensational. Regardless, the maxim “never judge a pitch until both sides have batted” is one you should ignore at your peril.

The other thing I like about the game is how it doesn’t monopolize my attention. Test Cricket is a slow game that unfolds in chapters, so looking away, writing this blog, crushing some Duo etc doesn’t take away from anything major. In fact, I often prefer listening to the radio vs TV, with so much dead time to cover, the commentators have to fill it with waffling on about the “good old times”.

Octavia has been passed out on me the entire time, so I don’t know if we have a fan yet, but hopefully the subconscious mind is picking it all up!

title: GitHub
text:
I don’t fully understand, but now I have a GitHub, and not only that, I use it!

For those who’d don’t know what GitHub is, it’s a repository for code. It’s got a few purposes; it’s a portfolio, you can see what other people have done, and you can store the various iterations of your code without fear of it being lost.

This comes in super handy when you have built something that works, but you want to tinker with it to make it better. However, all it takes is a rogue semi-colon for the whole thing to break. By saving (or pushing) it to GitHub, then even if you completely screw it up, you can always return to the original, working code. It’s helpful.

Feel free to star me (is that correct) at https://github.com/hwetherall

title: Sensory overload
text:
Ky and I went to a 40th birthday today, our first. It was at a place called Pindustry, which was, by far, one of the most unusual venues I have ever been in my life.

It was out in the boonies, where there isn’t as much of a premium on being hip, nor being economical with space. With both of those restrictions relaxed, Pindustry was free to provide its guests a bar, a bowling alley, a mechanical bull, a darts alley, an arcade, and a live music stage as well, where a 10 piece band pumped out a bunch of dance floor classics.

Perhaps even more unusual was the clientele. There were three types of people; 23 year olds on their 2nd dates, 60 year old boomers getting absolutely turnt on the dance floor, and our group.

A wonderful experience!

title: Never judge a pitch until both sides have batted
text:
Two days I wrote about how India was collapsing under a relentless Australian bowling attack, and thought that the game would be over before it really started. However, I did leave a caveat; “never judge a pitch until both sides have batted”.

To my American readers, the pitch makes a huge difference when it comes to the game. Unlike baseball, where the ball passes through the air, in cricket, the ball (almost) always has to bounce on the pitch before reaching the batsman. Every pitch is different, and they change over the game as well. Hot weather in particular can have a major effect, as the heat dries out the wicket and can cause cracks that can cause the ball to jerk off course. When the ball is travelling at almost 100 miles an hour, even a tiny diversion can be deadly.

It turns out I shouldn’t have been so quick to judge. While India was bowled out for 150, Australia barely got over 100, which are both very low scores. What’s more, each team has two innings a piece, and most of the time, they score far more in the first attempt than the second. Not so in this instance, India piled on 450+ (a huge score), and have set Australia a target of 500+.

Since the first game in 1877, there have been over 2500 tests, and only 13 times has a score of 300 been chased down. 3 times a score of 400 has been achieved, and 500 has never been achieved.

It would be a genuine miracle.

title: Adobe
text:
You may know the company from Acrobat, their PDF viewer which was essential downloading until Google Chrome cucked them. Or if you’re more creative, you may be familiar with their software like Premiere or Auditon. Either way, they’re kinda famous, and also very poorly received. In a world where people want to surprise and delight their customers, it feels like they do the opposite.

Perhaps there is a new sheriff in town, but today I trialled their free AI sound enhancer. It. Is. Awesome. It sounded like I recorded it in a studio, a studio without a crying baby and a beeping washing machine. It’s all part of their new plans to enter the podcasting space, and honestly, they might be on a winner.

You can check it out HERE.

And for those wondering, no, there no affiliate links here, just spreading the word.

title: Stone’s Ginger Wine
text:
We recently picked up some drinks for Thanksgiving/Birthday/Christmas, and in the mix was Stone’s Ginger Wine.

If you haven’t had it before, it’s hard to describe. It is like the essence of an aloholic ginger beer, as though someone had reduced it over the stove for 20 minutes. I first had it many moons ago at my old sharehouse, and for the life of me I couldn’t remember what it was called. Describing it to ChatGPT was a challenge, but finally, FINALLY, we got there.

Hopefully it is as good as I remember, and for 8.99 a bottle (wine bottle, mind you), the risk I screw this one up is low.

title: Demo Day
text:
I don’t like being bored at work, so while I have a bit of down time, I have been fiddling around with a few side quests. Thanks to AI, building has never been easier.

When you’re unsure of what you’re doing, it can be difficult demoing to your peers, especially if they’re experts. So it’s nice to be an org that not only allows it, but celebrates it.

title: Book club
text:
I’m part of the world’s smallest book club; right now it’s just two people, our Chief Product Officer, and me. We meet every two weeks to discuss a book on product, management, sales, or something else useful to the business. Friday is our first session.

The first book is called “The inmates are running the asylum”, and I have come across my first banger quote.

We have all heard the expression “you get what you pay for”, but in software businesses, and perhaps all businesses, a better maxim is “you don’t get what you don’t pay for”.

Something to chew on.

title: Thanksgiving
text:
It’s my favourite US holiday, and this year we got two for the price of one.

Yesterday, Thanksgiving proper was had at our friend’s place. It was exactly what you’d expect expect; turkey (which wasn’t dry!), stuffing (or dressing as our neighbors call it), mash, bread, gravy, and lashing of wine. We didn’t pass into a food coma, we didn’t have a screaming match over politics, and we didn’t watch any football, but otherwise it was a quintessential day.

Today was Aussie Thanksgiving with another group of friends. There is a small but vibrant Australian community in Denver, including the Denver Bulldogs, the local Australian Rules Football Club, which are the Real Madrid of US Aussie Rules. It was a fun, casual affair, which was completely dedicated to eating desserts and drinking sparkling wine. I’m usually not a fan of either, but on this occasion, it was a proper treat.

Thanksgiving always reminds me of Easter back home; all the fun of Christmas, none of the pressure, and four days to focus on eating and spending time with friends and family.

title: Breakdown
text:
We’ll be back to regular programming tomorrow, but alas, the schedule post feature on WordPress just does. Not. Work.

It should in practice, but I’ve been completely stitched up in this regard.

Feel free to read my posts on Thanksgiving and Book Club at the attached links, but in the meantime, let me entertain you with an extremely short story about how Octavia unleashed such a hellish turbo-turd that it broke through her nappy, outfit, and almost my clothing as well.

Shit happens.

title: Birthday
text:
Today I celebrated my 33rd birthday, and it was a blast.

Ky had put together an absolute showstopper of an occasion, mixing together thoughtful gifts, fun activities, and a deliciously decadent dinner. As I write this, about to hit the hay, I feel very lucky indeed.

There are many wonderful things about being married, but one of them is having someone who both knows you, loves you, and wants the best for you in your life.

It really is a blessing.

P.S Thank you to everyone who wrote me the last few days, it means a lot.

title: Calling in the cavalry
text:
My mother has arrived in town for two weeks, which is equal parts excellent and exciting news.

As I write this, she is having some very special one on one time with Octavia, and you can tell this isn’t her first rodeo. The way she holds her and cuddles her suggests that she is a real expert. Three kids will get you there.

We don’t have a huge amount planned for her time over here; more than anything we want to maximize the time we can spend together as a family. With borders, mountains, and oceans separating us, every moment counts.

title: Self Custody
text:
A few years ago, I went through a bit of a crypto-phase. How much of a phase? It’s hard to say, I didn’t liquidate any assets to get all in on Cuck-Coin or something foolish, but I did buy myself a Ledger.

For the uninitiated, a Ledger is a type of Cold Wallet, which probably doesn’t illuminate anything. A better way to describe it is that I have a little USB stick which has two buttons on it which keeps my crypto safe from hackers.

It’s mind-bending, and the UX could be improved, but it’s cool to be able to “be the bank”.

Should you buy one? Probably not, especially as Black Friday has passed, and if you were going to get one, you probably already have one. I like mine though.

title: Greedy Defence
text:
I have been doing a fair bit of rapid prototyping at work recently, which is both enjoyable and, I hope, valuable. A lesson I learned today was that when you’re trying to build something new, don’t forget to hold onto what has already worked.

Software can get complex, fast. It only takes a little bit of tinkering and before you know it, what was working well is completely broken. There is no inbetween; changing up a recipe might make it worse, but you still have a meal. With software, a misplaced ; means the entire program fails to function.

Moving fast is fine, but it pays to bring a life raft. In this instance, saving your files online using a repository are a life saver.

title: Video tells a million words
text:
I have found making short videos to be an incredible work tool recently. I’m not talking TikToks, I’m talking make a quick vid that demonstrates a process, a bug, a demo, a whatever.

Describing the world around us is difficult; there is a reason why there are so many people on Twitter and so few celebrated novelists.

I use Kapwing, which is a bit of an upstart, and I can use it on my underpowered laptop. I’m open to alternatives, so let me know.

Not loom.

title: Birthday Dinner
text:
I had my birthday dinner party last night, and had a wonderful time.

I’m a fan of the dinner party. It’s grown up, and gives you a chance to cook something which usually isn’t appropriate for midweek. As someone who really enjoys cooking, what for many part is the second worst past of hosting (everyone hates tidying up), it’s a nice bonus.

The highlight of the evening was, by far, the chocolate cake that Ky made. I’m not really a cake, nor sweet, person, but that went down an absolute treat.

Thanks for everyone who came and made it a wonderful evening, I know you read this!

title: Fast news week
text:
Some days, the news is slow. This is when we read the “Man bites dog” articles, or the “What your choice in Garlic Bread says about your career prospects” clog up the feed.

Not this week. There has been martial law in South Korea. Syria went from zero to 100 real quick. Someone murdered a CEO in NYC in broad daylight, who is currently also being voted as one of the “sexiest men in America”. Talking about murders in New York, Daniel Penny was found not guilty. Oh, and there seems to be a full on descent into chaos in Haiti.

I don’t often watch the news, but I did this evening, and it was very much a packed schedule. Sadly, this is rarely a good thing.

title: No Rush
text:
There are somethings you can’t rush. You can’t speed up the slow cooking of a lamb leg, even if your guests are due to arrive in the next 20 minutes. You can’t get to the airport a little bit faster if you’re on public transport. You’re simply constrained.

This can be the same with some work as well, particularly software development. If you don’t know what you’re doing (i.e. me ), then there is no way you can accelerate the process, because you don’t even know where you are.

Plan accordingly.

title: Into the wild
text:
I have been practicing my Spanish solely on DuoLingo for the past two months or so; today, it was time to take it to the calles.

Luckily, I knew the perfect place to go; lunch at Tacqueria La Familia. It’s the closest I can find to immersion; none of the staff really speak English, and ordering a burrito isn’t exactly outlining the finer points of quantum computing.

With one error; I made it through. The reason I switched to Spanish is a chance to practice regularly, and now it’s time to honour that.

title: Labels. Fables?
text:
I had a meeting with my employer today to discuss my goals and role for 2025. It was a success.

The most interesting conversation we had was around my job title. I’m (almost) completely indifferent to it, and came to this conclusion many years ago when I was running the Flying Zucchinis. As a very small company, it felt ridiculous to call myself CEO. Furthermore, when one of our staff members left and asked for his title, I realised we didn’t have one, so I assigned him the “Director of Warehouse Operations” role, which is exactly what he did. He needed it while he was applying for a different position, and I’m glad to say he got it.

I think I have found my title for LinkedIn, but if anyone asks, the most accurate title stay’s the same “I work for Innovera”.

title: Bas and Pete
text:
I came across the most unlikely hip hop duo today at work, Bas and Pete. Hailing from South East London, they originally got into making music after Pete would listen to his grand daughter playing music in the car. No, that isn’t a typo; you see, Bas and Pete are both in their 60s.

Being a rapper is largely a young man’s game. 30 is most certainly old, 40 is completely geriatric. So to be laying down sick lines while also collecting a pension is nothing short of inspiring.

The UK rap scene isn’t everyone’s cup of tea, but I would recommend giving these two gents 3 minutes of your time.

title: Signatures
text:
