We had an early Christmas dinner this evening, as we have both Ky’s sister, her fiancé, and mum in town. We decided to go all out and do the Beef Wellington, the world’s bougiest sausage roll.

I like cooking the Beef Wellington. It’s a masterpiece, but the dish is made up of a series of simple tasks; make the duxelle, make the crepes, sear the beef, combine, wrap, cook. There is a lot to it, but in terms of technique, it is fairly straightforward.

Nevertheless, it’s still a massive relief when it comes out perfect. The number of times you cook “showstopper” dishes is limited to a handful a year; it is dreadful when it doesn’t work out.

title: Get it India
text:
When entering India, one of the most difficult things is filling out the visa application. The UI/UX on the site is appalling, and regularly crashes. Putting your payment details in rarely works, and requires you to attempt the process multiple times. If it there way of keeping the weakwilled out, then it’s very effective.

As a needle in the haystack, there is ONE highlight that keeps you going, and that is selecting your occupation. Today, my boss shared it with me.

There are some excellent items that always get a giggle out of me.

Sea man, is one, a job that I am fairly sure doesn’t exist.

Having Reporter/Journalist/Press/News Broadcaster/TV Producer/Media as options feels like overkill, when Teacher is absent.

But my all time favourite occupation has to be Un Official. I would love to meet someone one day whose job is this.

title: Top Golf
text:
Today, Ky and I, along with her sister Dee and fiance Ben, went to Top Golf. For the uninitiated, Top Golf is what you get when you take the humble driving range, and throw a ton of Private Equity cash at it.

The premise is simple: hit golf balls into an empty field, as many times as you like. But at Top Golf, they have pursued some giga-brain strategies:

 1. Most Driving Ranges (henceforth known as Bottom Golf, or BG) charge per the ball. This makes sense, but once you’re out of balls, you’re probably going to head off. Once you’re gone, so is your wallet. At Top Golf, you pay for TIME, with unlimited balls. This not only feels like a better deal, but it means you’re likely to stay longer, and thus the opportunity to be upsold on food or beverages massively increases. There is no marginal cost to a golf ball being hit, so why not encourage people to hit as many as possible?
 2. Bottom Golf only appeals to Golfers. Of the four of us (excluding Occi), only Ben knew anything about hitting a golf ball. Playing a game you suck at – unsurprisingly – usually sucks. Golf isn’t table tennis, the learning curve is brutal. However, Top Golf has created various “games” that mean that you can play against one another in such a way that an agricultural hack can score just as many points as a PGA-perfect drive. The “inclusiveness” means that even punters like myself, who know nothing about Golf, will be coming back for more.
 3. Nail the basics. When going to a driving range, you don’t expect a Michellin Star experience. However, some places either way overcharge, or just provide the absolute worst possible product. The difference between a garbage pile of chips/fries is probably a few cents and a half inch of effort in the kitchen, but the outcome is significant.

Overall, a great experience!

title: Breather
text:
It’s been pretty full on the past few weeks at work, so it was nice that today was an easier day.

Being able to finish everything early, take time for a proper lunch, go for a stroll, and play with Occi was a much needed breather.

title: Dark Side
text:
I am ashamed to admit it, but I have embraced the Darkness, and decided to add a Pop-Up to my Home Page. I know they’re not always the most popular, but I realised that I have been getting a few people message me about how they went to my blog, liked it, read a few articles, and then LEFT, unsubscribed.

Perhaps they think it’s lame, or they don’t need ANOTHER newsletter clogging their inbox, but it also may be because I don’t make it easy to subscribe. There is a CAPTCHA, you have to navigate to the Subscribe Page, the UX is horrible, and then you have to answer the confirmation email. Hardly straightforward.

I might not see any uptick, and fair enough. If that’s the case, the Pop Up goes, and I’ll try and think of something else to get the punters lining up.

title: Prince of Dune
text:
It’s not really a Christmas Movie, but we watched Prince of Egypt this evening. What struck me was how much it reminded me of another movie I had seen, 10 months earlier.

Dune is a good movie, and there are obviously a lot of differences. But beyond the desert backdrop, the idea of an enslaved people (Hebrews/Fremen) who rely on a prophet to guide them, who comes from the enemy itself.

They’re both great stories, told in interesting and powerful ways. PoE has the better soundtrack, Dune the better visuals.

Both are worth a watch .

title: All American
text:
Today my future brother in law and I went for an All American afternoon. It was a wild ride.

It all started at lunchtime, where we dined out at apparently one of Denver’s premier Mexican restaurants. They say “location, location, location” when it comes to the Real Estate industry, but when it comes to dining out, a restaurant connected to a gas station in a rougher part of town can be slinging some of the best burritos around. In a word, authentic.

From there, it was off to the Tesla Dealership to test drive the Cyber Truck. It was both the best and worst driving experience of my entire life.

The good news? This car is unbelievably quick, by far the fastest vehicle I have ever driven. Its acceleration from 0-100 kms is only a few seconds, and you can REALLY feel it. At the same time, the manoeuvrability is top notch. A super fun drive, and I’m not someone who loves cars.

The bad? At the red light, we decided to play around with a few features, and ended up logging ourselves out of the car! So imagine this, we’re at a red light, we can’t get out of park, and all we can hear is honking from irate drivers behind us. This went for 10 minutes until we could get through to HQ and they could remotely restart it. A terrible experience.

After that, we were a little flustered, so we decided to destress and check out the range. Yep, we went down to the gun store, saw their range, then went to the range.

I’ve never fired a pistol before, and it took me a little while to get the hang of it, and I probably won’t do it again any time soon. Regardless, a fun, very American day.

title: Footballer, Teacher, Soldier, Prisoner?
text:
I had a friend at primary and middle school called Oscar Jenkins. When I moved for high school, I never really saw him again, although he did leave quite an impression. He was a terrific footballer and cricketer, and there were quite a few to pick from in my year level. Not only was he talented, but he was the type who you would naturally pick as captain; while not always loud, he was very reliable and well liked by all.

As you can imagine, it was the most terrible of surprises to see that the kid I played school yard cricket with 20 years prior had recently been captured by Russian forces in Ukraine. According to news reports, Oscar decided to join the resistance, and is fighting on the front.

The videos and images are shocking. Handcuffed, dirty, thin and sullen eyed, Oscar is a shadow of the bright futured boy I remember back in Melbourne.

His fate is no longer his own. The best case scenario is the Aus Government will be able to organise his release, perhaps with a prisoner swap. There is reason to hope that this may occur; after all, they were able to free Kylie Moore-Gilbert from Iran a few years ago.

There are stark differences though: Dr Moore-Gilbert was an academic falsely accused of espionage, appeared completely normal, and is very photogenic. The last two points shouldn’t matter, but sadly, it does.

In comparison, Mr Jenkins is clearly fighting as a mercenary, has made some unusual comments in the past about converting Chinese people to veganism, and doesn’t have the million dollar smile. The sympathy from the public just won’t be the same; and the fact remains, he chose to join the front, knowing full well this might happen.

I hope Oscar is freed. He is a good person, and was always kind to me at school. While I pray for the best, there is every chance that the worst may happen, and the Russians will execute him.

He is just 32 years old.

title: Pre-game
text:
We went to the basketball this evening, all five of us, including Occi. She did great considering it’s a loud environment, and definitely performed better than the visiting Phoenix Suns (they got blown out by 20+ points).

Usually we turn up just as the game is about to start, despite only living a 12 minute walk away. It’s the damn queues; we always forget about them.

On this occasion we turned up early, and it was a great experience. No stress, we got to hear the anthem, and the stadium always does their darnedest to get the crowd up and about.

title: Final time trying
text:
There are books we have all tried, and failed, to get through. There is often a sense of frustration attached, particularly if they’re well regarded, or worse, recommended.

There are a few on my list, but the one that sticks out like a sore thumb is Meditations, by Marcus Aurelius.

There really is no book like it, and there probably never will be. The private writings of a general, philosophy, and emperor, written with no expectation of ever being read. In some ways, it’s the opposite of this project!

I recently got a paperback copy off Amazon, and have attacked this version with a little more vigor. I’ll white knuckle it to Chapter 5, and if it’s not working, it’s not working.

I think Marcus would approve of this strategy.

title: Merry Christmas!
text:
Wherever you are in the world, I wish you a very merry Christmas.

For the religious, Christ is Born! As a father myself, I only now realise that there is a sense of both unmitigated and unlimited hope channeled into a new born. They really do have limitless potential, although saviour of all mankind might be a little optimistic!

For my more secular friends, Christmas remains a true cultural touchstone. You can bitch and moan about the creeping commercialism of the holiday, but most people tunnel through that to enjoy the company of their friends and family, and take some much needed time off.

For us, we had a wonderful day. A pancake breakfast, a ham ‘n’ lamb dinner, and then one of my favourite movies to finish off the day.

Merry Christmas!

title: Crowd pleaser
text:
It’s hard to pick a meal that everyone enjoys, that can be made easily, cheaply, and at scale. The ones that can tick off all four boxes are what I call School Camp Classics.

The list is very limited, but top of the list would have to be the Sticky Date Pudding. We made it for dessert tonight, and while I hadn’t spent the night sleeping in a tent or trying to read a map, it still hit the spot.

title: The Nut Cracker
text:
Ky and I went to the ballet this evening to watch the Nut Cracker. It was a lovely chance to soak up some high culture and do something christmassy.

I’m a fan of the ballet, particularly the Tchaikovsky numbers. While Swan Lake remains number 1, I think the NC is a lot of fun.

What I found interesting was the second act was FAR better than the first; which is a rarity for me. As someone who only goes once or so a year, I am by no means an expert, but I usually prefer Act I, usually as there is a bit of plot movement. Act II often feels like a dance fest.

With the Nutcracker, the dance fest IS the best bit. From the internationally themed dances, to the Grand Pas De Deux, it is just a feast for the eyes.

The Colorado Ballet put on a good show. They’re not the NYCB or the Bolshoi, but they had a gorgeous set and a terrific orchestra. We’ll be back!

title: Invention
text:
Walmart is an American icon. Love it or hate it, you’d be hard pressed to find someone who hasn’t bought something there.

We went there today to show Dee and Ben around, and we thought it was a scream. In particular, Ben and I were captivated by the fishing aisle, where they had a rod which you could easily fit into your briefcase. A far cry from the surf rod, but if you’re hiking out there and would love to see what’s out there nibbling, you could go a lot worse than that.

It was also cool to have Ky, loss prevention extraordinaire, point out the visible artificers of her hidden world. More about that tomorrow.

title: Pre-Cap
text:
Tomorrow is the last day of the year, and I was wondering if you, dear reader, have any questions you’d like answered!

I am planning on doing a recap of the year, and my predictions for 2025. However, I’m open to suggestions, and would love to hear what you like, what you dislike, and what you think is missing.

Replying to this email comes straight to my inbox (and I reply to each one!) but feel free to message me over the medium you most prefer. Take care!

title: A year of writing
text:
It’s almost been an entire year since I decided to start writing this blog, and while I haven’t written every day, I did so for 97.5% of them. Not shabby.

When I look back at what I think went right, I think what stood out was starting slow. For the first month, all my blog posts I wrote on my Notes app. I didn’t want to tell people that I was embarking on this grand project, only to discard it a few months in. Instead, I set myself small milestones, and once I built up the momentum, it became hard to stop.

Writing everyday isn’t difficult. The world is so interesting, and there have been some (very average) posts I have written in under a minute as I was falling asleep. But just because it isn’t difficult doesn’t mean it’s hard; losing weight requires one to just stop stuffing their damper hole with treat, but again easier said than done.

What have I got out of this? The biggest one has been a stronger connection with friends and family abroad. Quite a few times I have met up with a subscriber and they’ll mention something I have written months earlier. It’s nice, and it means we can spend less time “catching up” and more time deepening our relationship.

In terms of becoming a better writer, I’m not sure if there has been any significant change. I think I’m better at writing “ok” text faster, but I certainly haven’t become Dostoevsky in the past year. Part of this is that I rarely write at my best – it’s often the last thing I do before bed – so perhaps I can change that next year.

Finally, it’s been a great opportunity to create a body of work. I never go back to read past pieces, but next week I might get ChatGPT to read the corpus and share their thoughts. Who knows what they’ll uncover!

title: Happy New Year!
text:
I was planning on doing a full recap, but time has escaped me, so I’ll do it tomorrow.

Stay safe, be bold, have fun, and I’ll see y’all in 2025!

title: Goals
text:
Ky and I had a great walk today, where we discussed our goals for the year ahead.

We split them into four parts: Relationships, Health, Financial, Career, and Personal Development. I have debated whether to share them on this, and for now, I think they’ll stay under lock and key. Perhaps the fitness goals might get a mention, but we shall see.

The conversation finished unfinished. While we were very clear on the way we wanted in some areas, there are other challenges which remain relatively undefined. Hopefully over the coming days we will figure them out, and maybe I will be more bold with sharing our hopes for 2025.

There is at least one reader in this group who I’ll have a chat with about goal setting, so stay tuned for that message #elephants.

title: A day in the life of…
text:
The past few days, I have been living the VC life. And not the glamorous side of it either, ripping my Juul pod while rocking my Patagonia vest and All-Birds while offering generic advice to anyone who will listen. No, the past 48 hours has been deep in DD, Due Diligence.

Venture Capital is misunderstood, similar to the law. I use this example as most people have no idea what Private Equity does, so they reserve their opinions on the matter. However, thanks to TV, people seem to have a misguided view of what being a lawyer looks like, distorted entirely by a singular show, Suits. My lawyer friends laugh painfully when I point out that their career path rarely features wise-cracking in court, expensive dinners, or model-esque paralegals.

Venture Capital is similar. You may think that there aren’t any tv shows that hit mass appeal that present a glorified view of this industry, but you’d be wrong. Shark Tank (or Dragon’s Den if you’re in the UK) is essentially a hyperspeed version of early stage equity investing; instead of software companies, they’re looking at pet toys and ice creams, but otherwise the process feels about right. Come up with a cool idea, pitch it to fools, and secure the bag.

Both these shows have a skerrick of truth to them. Some lawyers really do go to court and passionately persuade a jury to free an innocent person. But a lot (and I mean a LOT) more spend their time hunched over documents.

In the VC space, it’s the same. Yes, there is the “fun” part where the founders come in and show you the demo/deck, but then you have an associate trawl through tens, if not hundreds of documents to find out what really is going on under the hood.

I’ve been doing this recently for work, not as an investor, but it has given me a more honest view of what a career in VC might look like.

It would serve a lot of people if this was more common; seeing what life is really like, not just what you see on TV.



P.S: 2024 recap is coming, I promise!

title: Goal #1: Cold Showers
text:
I’ll throw you a bone, Goal Seekers.

On the list of the things I am trying to achieve this year, one of them is to do series of monthly challenges. Like in the software world, it is a wise idea to iterate quickly, seeing what works and what doesn’t. Trying a new habit, lifestyle, or behaviour for 30 days is a nice balance between spending enough time figuring out what the benefits (and costs!) are of pursuing the change, while also being free to try 12 different modifications over the year.

January is dedicated to Cold Showers, one per day. So far, I have always gone for the morning shower; it means it is out of the way, plus, now that I give myself a 2 hour wake window before consuming caffeine (and you should too), it is an effective if slightly barbaric way of rousing from slumber.

We’re on Day 3, and it’s already getting easier. Have I seen the wonderful effects that Wim Hof, the Dutch guru/cold enthusiast/nutty squirrel professes? Not yet, but I don’t really do the breathing routine to match, so that might explain it.

If you haven’t heard of the Wim Hof method, I have included a short video, featuring a dear friend of mine, explaining not only the method, but the benefits of his approach to breathing, pushups, and the cold.

I’ll report back in 28 days.

title: Year 2
text:
Today is my second wedding anniversary with Ky, so a very special day.

It was also freezing, and Occi has been a bit of a handful recently, so it was a quiet affair. We went to our favourite cafe for brunch, and had a lovely dinner as well. In between we went to Costco.

The highlight was, without a doubt, watching our wedding video. It’s a tad over 8 minutes long, and while I can’t share it here, let me know if you’d like a copy. Otherwise, I’ll leave one of my favourite photos below.

I’m thrilled to be married to Ky, and two years on, things are better than ever!

title: Ups and Downs
text:
Today I had a bad call. Google called me up and said that my account had been compromised, and that a hacker had tried to change my password. Weird, sure, but to make it worse, they had used my Social Security Number to try and break through. So it looks like my identity is at least somewhat compromised.

I’ve taken some precautions, including contacting the credit agencies, but it does feel strangely violating. The whole experience unnerved me.

On the flip side, Occi started laughing today, which is such a delight.

Ups and downs.

title: True-done
text:
Justin Trudeau stepped down today after 10 years in charge. It’s been coming.

I can’t say I know a lot about him, but it was definitely the right decision. Like many democracies, governments are rarely voted in; rather, they’re voted out, and Mr Trudeaus’ had overstayed his visit.

It takes a long time for historians to accurately remember someone. Jimmy Carter was considered a dreadful president when he got booted in 1981; when he passed away last week, most people mourned a great American.

For Trudeau, I don’t think it will go down well. Not since Blair have we seen someone soar so high, and fall so low, within their reign.

Back when Justin first came to power, he was considered by many, particularly college-educated, left-leaning women, to be the best thing since sliced bread. Politics is Hollywood for ugly people, and he really does have a great head of hair.

But things are rotten in the Far North. Housing prices are high, wages are low, and lots of people feel despair about the future. I hear similar echoes in parts of Australia and New Zealand, but at least the weather is nice.

Anyway, there is going to be a lot written about this, both now and in the future. I still think it’s rough to have a crack at a bloke having a crack, and putting your hand up to lead is a tough job that’s poorly paid and receives little thanks.

Enjoy retirement Mr Trudeau.

title: A different distraction
text:
My phone recently told me my average view time for the past week. Usually I avoid looking at it, as it can be a sobering experience.

Not this time. It hasn’t been under four hours for probably years, but it’s now closer to three.

I was pleasantly surprised, but I shouldn’t have been. An infant is far more interesting than an iPhone.

title: Icy Slip
text:
The snow has fallen, and melted, but it’s still icy out here. Icy enough to stack it while walking home tonight from the theatre to see Douglas Murray speak.

No harm, no damage, but ice on concrete is hard.

title: Douglas Murray
text:
I went to see esteemed writer and journalist, Douglas Murray, speak at the Paramount Theatre last night. It was an engaging affair.

He spent a lot of time talking about Israel, and the conflict that he’s been covering since October 7. By integrating footage he took with anecdotes, both light and dark, he was able to build a very persuasive case. The audience, clearly on his side, loved it.

One comment I took away from the affair came towards the end, when he gave the advice that he usually gives to young people, which is to seek truth. Imagine two paths: one path involved going with the flow, agreeing with what others say, and being true to your contemporaries’ shibboleths. The good thing about this path is that it’s predictable; others have done the same thing, and you’ll end up where they are if you follow the path.

The other path is to seek and say what you think to be true. The downside of this is that life is unpredictable, you could be cancelled, or in more tyrannical parts of the world, imprisoned or killed. The advantage of this is that you are living your own life, rather than a copy of a copy of a copy.

Ky and I spoke about this today, and I think the conversation will continue onwards.

title: Flavour Nap
text:
We’re seeing friends for dinner tomorrow night, and we’re bringing a raging. I pride myself on making a good one, and firmly believe the secret ingredient is time. No, I haven’t misspelled the herb (although it feature), but taking the time both to cook it down (~5 hours) and then for the flavour nap (~24 hours).

It’s worth the wait.

title: Arranged
text:
Note: Yesterday I referred to a meal as raging. What I meant to say was ragù, and for those who wonder, yes, it was delicious.

A writer I follow on Substack wrote about how he predicted an increased trend of “arranged marriages” in the US over the coming years.

He was careful to define what he meant; “arranged marriages” weren’t forced marriages, and certainly weren’t consanguineous. Rather, the idea was that parents, who knew their child well, and wanted the best for them, would nudge them in the right direction, from introductions to encouragement.

Choosing a partner is an extremely important decision, probably THE most important. Yet his argument, and fair enough, is that leaving young people alone to make this decision solo, aided only by alcohol and apps, does very little to maximise the odds of a successful, long term partnership.

I’m not fully convinced. I met my wife on an app, so did both my sisters, and my sister in law. What was once fringe has become very much mainstream. I understand there are downsides to them, but they DO offer people a chance to find partners outside their circles, which is great at many levels, including social mobility.

However, I do see the logic of my friend’s piece, and for those who are looking for a relationship, but struggling to do so, it could be another avenue to wander down.

Like everything, I guess we will just have to wait and see.

title: Home or Away?
text:
It’s FA Cup weekend, and while I didn’t catch any games, I did enjoy a few highlights in the late afternoon.

For those who don’t know, the FA Cup is an English soccer/football knockout tournament open to every club in the country. This week was the Third Round, the round when the Big Dogs enter the comp, and there are always massive mismatches.

I was thinking this afternoon, if you were a massive underdog, would you prefer to play Home or Away?

If you play at Home, it’s a special experience. I watched the highlights of Tamworth (stadium capacity 4k, 16th in League 5) play against Tottenham (stadium capacity 62k, 12th Premier League). Tottenham’s captain earns 190k pounds a week, Tamworth’s captain works as an Insurance Appraiser for one of the local construction companies. But for 90 minutes, you get to welcome them to your backyard, and everyone in the town is rooting for you to cause an upset. Home ground advantage extends beyond the field as well, as the photos below demonstrate:

Tamworth’s Medical Facilities

Compared to:

Tottenham’s Change Room

However, there is also a very special experience to being the away team. Accrington Stanley (currently 19th in the 4th Division, town population 34k) played Liverpool (1st in Premier League, stadium capacity 61k), and while they lost 5-nil, the chance to go to Anfield, go to the change rooms, be on TV, play in front of more than your parents and a few seagulls, and compete next to global superstars is such a wonderful thing.

The magic of the Cup is still alive and well in this household.

title: Personal Sayings
text:
Most idioms are common, largely because language needs to be shared to be valuable. We all understand the saying “shag on a rock”, whereas “pidgeon on a boulder” would only be greeted with some confused stares.

However, we all have personal sayings that we think are clever or useful, but just haven’t caught on, yet. For me, there are two I often use at work:

An octopus picked every winner of the World Cup.

Context: In 2010, Paul, an octopus, picked the successful outcome of all of Germany’s wins in the Fifa World Cup, as well as Spain’s eventual triumph. Octopodes are clever, but to our knowledge aren’t able to pick the future. Paul was, in a word, lucky. Actually, he was VERY lucky, as the odds of this occurring a 1/256, or just under 0.4%.

Usage: This idiom captures the idea that in any environment where multiple decisions or events occur, there will always be outliers that exists, at least partially due to luck, and that there will ALWAYS be someone who arrives at those destinations.

This is particularly helpful in the VC world, where a particular fund may invest in winner after winner. Perhaps they DO have some secret sauce that allows them to keep discovering the next Uber, or perhaps they are just enjoying a period of luck. This saying reminds us that when it comes to picking the future, luck plays a role, and that highly successful (or disastrous!) outcomes could be a result of a short-term hot streak.

Why I like it: There are many alternatives to that adequately capture this idea. Flipping a coin to heads eight times in a row doesn’t mean you’re a master coin flipper, but I think the Octopus reference is pithier and most importantly, draws a wry smile semi-regularly. The downsides is that Paul was doing his auguring 15 years ago, and while he may be a Julius Caesar figure amongst his eight-legged brethren, he is slowly edging towards irrelevancy, particularly among the youths.

Tomorrow I’ll write about Saying #2, but in the meantime, feel free to reply to this email and let me know what YOUR favourite personal saying is, and why it should catch on.

title: Sayings #2
text:
Thank you to everyone who shared their favourite sayings, it was great to hear from you all, and I’ll get back to you all by tomorrow if I haven’t already. In fact, I might feature a few of them tomorrow.

My second saying that I have created, and would love to see catch on, is “an animal is a cat”. Confused? Let me explain.

This saying is an answer to an unstated question, although it can be inferred quite easily. That question is “what is an animal?” and the answer is “an animal is a cat”.

Explanation: I use this when someone tries to explain something by using an example, rather than a definition. Sometimes you can get away with it, but sometimes it’s lazy, ineffective, or confusing.

What is an animal? A cat is an animal, but so is a frog, and so is a fish, and so is a person. Knowing a single example doesn’t help; a cat has four legs, is a table an animal?

Usage: I use it occasionally at work, but more frequently when discussing or thinking about politics. A classic example may be someone describing themselves as a conservative, and when you ask why, they might say something like “I support Trump”. But that’s not a definition, that’s an instance. Often, people haven’t really thought about it, which is fair enough, there are lots of immediate things that need attending to.

title: Your Sayings
text:
Thank you for those who wrote in to share some of their favourite sayings. Here are three that I thought were worth sharing:

Blind Freddy could have told you that: Apparently this is a fairly common idiom in Australia, but I don’t think I have ever heard it used bar the person who messaged me, who uses it pretty much daily. It is used when something is blindingly obvious, for example, the Hawk Tuah Coin was so obviously a scam, even Blind Freddy could have told you that. Interestingly, Freddy is a fictional character, although some say it refers to an 1860s Sydney Policeman Sir Frederick Pottinger.

Is the juice worth the squeeze: This one came from a friend who is completing his medical studies, looking after a one year old, a dog, has another on the way, all while playing Gaelic football for Australia. With quite a busy itinerary, asking “is the juice worth the squeeze” a great way to determine whether the outcome is worth effort/investment, and to focus on prioritization. A great one!

Massive Win Today: This is very much an inside joke, created way back when I was working at Kiwibank. The details are slightly murky, but the gist of it was that I had volunteered to run an internal coaching event on a Friday afternoon, and completely forgot about it until 30 minutes to go. I ended up doing the most appallingly hamfisted job, and completely stunk.

I went back to my desk and had a few beers with two workmates to commiserate about the disaster. While slurping back a few Genghis Cans, I tried to rectify the situation by writing an email to the team wishing them a good weekend and thanking them for their participation. However, the subject was MASSIVE WIN TODAY, as I had originally written an over the top email talking about how the whole thing was indeed successful as a bit of a joke, with no intention to send. I cleaned up the message, but forgot the subject, and by the time I hit SEND, it was too late.

In a twist of fate, the leadership team couldn’t make the session, and only saw the email referring to the MASSIVE WIN TODAY, and concluded it was a resounding success!

MASSIVE WIN TODAY is used ironically, and is usually shortened to MASSIVE WIN. Will it catch on? Not at all, but for the three of us that experienced it, it will remain a regular staple on our group chat.

I called a friend up the other day, and he had set himself the challenge of reading 24 books in a year.

I think it’s an excellent challenge, as it is neither daunting, nor unaspiring. I’ll be following his progress on Good Reads, and wish him luck.

title: Sora
text:
I finally tried Sora, ChatGPTs AI video generator.

It’s ok. Despite being on the premium plan, I burnt through all my credits in less than 10 minutes, and what I created weren’t great by any means. Here are the pros and cons:

Pros: I mean, despite all my complains, this thing is still incredible. In under a minute I can imagine an idea, type it into the prompt, and have a short video to download. It’s pretty close to magic, even if my expectations were not met.

Cons: Creating video is incredibly complex. Making an image is difficult. Making a 5 second video at 30FPS means you need to make 150. While I understand that from a technical point of view, I still feel like it could be better.

There are also some issues with text on screen, with multiple spelling errors, which I never really understood.

Final thoughts: It’s not where you start, it’s where you finish. Below is the progress of how AI art improved within 12 months, and I think Video will be something similar.

Anyway, I’ll end with my video I made.

title: Amateur Hour
text:
I watched the last quarter of the Divisional Playoffs between the Washington and Detroit. The Detroit Lions came in strong favourites, but managed to completely implode.

One memorable example was seeing them penalised for the the most comical of reasons; too many players on the field. I played a lot of low level sport, and I can tell you that this NEVER happened. The fact that this could occur on the biggest stage, with the season on the line, is incredible.

There is a lot of wisdom in succeeding simply by reducing unforced errors.

title: VPN
text:
I have been a happy customer of the Swedish VPN company, Mullvad, for two years. However, today was my last day of my contract, and I chose to go with a competitor. I know a few of my readers are in the technology/product space, and you may be interested/frustrated to hear why a user could simultaneously like your offering and still leave. Here’s three reasons why I did, perhaps they apply to you:

 1. Underperform in instances that matter: one of the most crucial features of a VPN is that they’re undetectable. With Mullvad, I never had any issues, apart for with one app – ChatGPT. Everytime I wanted to use it on my phone (it was fine on laptop), I had to disconnect. Since I use ChatGPT all the time, this was an instance that mattered, and made the other 99% of successful instances immaterial.
 2. Overperform in instances that don’t matter: Mullvad prides itself on a number of features which I frankly don’t care about. Their ultra high levels of security, such as crypto payments and zero trust logins, are critical if you’re living in an authoritarian regime and need anonymity. I don’t, so crowing on about these features is only ever going to be a cherry on top. It’s not something I would pay extra for, which leads me to point three:
 3. Price: I respect Mullvad’s pricing model, €5 a month. Doesn’t matter if you buy 1 month or 1 years worth, or buy it today or Black Friday. The price is the price. Respect is one thing, but when other companies offer massive discounts to new users willing to take on long contracts, you’ll always get some churn.

I have gone with SurfShark, which for a few dollars more than 12 months with Mullvad has given me 2.5 years. It works perfectly with ChatGPT, and I don’t care if they made me login with an email when signing up. In the past few hours I have been using it, I’ve been happy with my choice.

For those who are considering a VPN, my referral link is HERE. Alternatively SurfShark offers unlimited devices, so if you’d like access to it, gratis, please let me know. Consider it a subscriber bonus.

title: Inauguration
text:
Today was Grocery Price Dropping Inauguration Day for Donald Trump. I didn’t catch much of it, but I saw a few of the highlights. It’s certainly an interesting time to be living here in America.

A few people from abroad have asked me about how I feel about it all, and particularly about the man of the moment, DJT. My position has always been consistent: I support the President. Whether it is Sleepy Joe or Don the Con, the fact remains that it is a difficult job, and everyone should be hoping they do a good job.

That doesn’t mean I don’t have opinions on those in office. It is completely rational to say that Joe Biden did lots of great things over his career, while simultaneously being too old for the job. Similarly, Donald Trump is a rather odious individual who says some pretty wild things, but there were certainly lots of bright spots in his previous presidency that I hope he can replicate this time round.

As with so many things, it is a matter of “wait and see”. The Hamas ceasefire is a good start. The support for going to Mars is one I can get behind. I think pardoning the Jan 7 Rioters is stupid, and probably a non-starter.

On a final note, it was nice to see the other characters up on stage. Barron Trump really is a giant, even among some tall people. It was nice seeing JD’s family on stage as well, but seeing Melania in her skin walker outfit was very, very strange.

Day 1 starts tomorrow Mr President, good luck!

title: An answer is not a solution
text:
We have had a fair few issues recently at our apartment, some of which have extremely annoying, and borderline unsafe.

Today maintenance came around, and while they were extremely eager to help and always friendly, we didn’t get what we were looking for.

For example, we have been having an infuriating issue since Day 1 where our toaster always seems to cause the fuse to break. This means that every time we want some toast, we either have to move the toaster or flick the switchboard a few times. It’s infuriating.

The answer to our troubles was that our apartment simply didn’t have enough wattage (or was it amperage?) to keep all our appliances running, and perhaps if we moved the toaster to a different socket, things would be better?

It was an answer, but we wanted a solution.

Something to think about.

title: Sleep on it
text:
I had a late one last night , trying to figure out a particular knotty problem. Being tired didn’t help, and after a few hours I had barely made any progress. It was frustrating, but also slightly concerning, as I had planned to present to the team in the morning.

The mind does wonderful things while you’re sleeping. When my alarm went off, I knew exactly what to do, and was able to deliver the goods. Today was just the dress rehearsal, tomorrow is the big show.

If all works out, I’ll let you know how it goes.

title: Basketball
text:
I went with a friend to the basketball tonight, and due to the team (Sacramento) and time (Thursday night), there were plenty of tickets available.

In the US, unless something has gone terribly wrong, every game sells out. This is due to their dynamic pricing strategy; I’ve seen tickets for baseball games as low as $4. So for my pal and I, it was our lucky day.

Every time I have been to the basketball prior to this I have sat right up in the gods. On at least one occasion there row behind me was literally a concrete wall. It’s a fun experience, but the game becomes a bit of a sideshow.

Sitting in Row 14 is another matter. The fans are more intense, the action is more visceral. I’m still price sensitive, so I won’t break the bank, but it was a great experience.

Our seats Previously best seats

title: Packing
text:
Ky and I are moving out of our apartment next week, and so the packing has begun.

It’s a horrible task. I joked with Ky today that we have been “reverse robbed” over the past two years; at night we have thieves breaking into our place, but instead of pilfering our possessions, they’re instead storing random things in our cupboards. These things tend to multiply.

The worst thing so far has been the bikes. There is something about bike bags that just frazzle me to no end, but fortunately, that’s now behind us.

Tomorrow is the big packing day, wish us luck!

title: Save the Date
text:
One of my sisters is due to be wed this November, and she sent out the Save the Date today.

It’s probably the only event in one’s life where you get two invitations, the pre-invite and the actual invite.

Thanks to the wonders of technology, the Save the Date is usually accompanied with a website bearing all the relevant information, and this one was no exception. With the price of digital real estate pretty much zero, you can answer all those pesky questions that could never fit on a paper invite.

What’s the dress code? When does it start? What will the weather be like? When does the bus leave? All vital questions that can be answered once, not multiple times as people call and ask the same thing again and again.

It’s a great system.

The actual invitation, delivered by post, might strike some as a mere formality, but it’s certainly something nice to hang on the fridge. I’m looking forward to it!

title: Chaos chasing?
text:
I’m about to hit the hay after a week focussed solely on packing, working, and planning. It’s been tiring, to say the least.

I was chatting about it today with Ky just then; is all this crazy planning just chao seeking? Moving with a small child to NZ, then to Aus, then back to NZ, then back to Aus, then back to the US, and then (possibly) to digital nomad in Mexico for a month sounds like an itinerary dreamt up in an insane asylum.

I don’t think we’re chaos chasing, but rather opportunity taking. The chance to do a lot of these things will only be harder in the future.

title: Never in doubt
text:
I’m usually a bit of an underdog supporter when it comes to sports; a rather common disposition. However, there are a few exceptions that I have recently been thinking about.

First off, I’m a fan of Patrick Mahones, the QB for the Kansas City Chiefs. They have won the past two Super Bowls, and booked themselves into the Big Dance last Sunday, winning by just a few points.

I don’t know much about American Football, and really only enjoy the last quarter. However, when Mr Mahomes has the ball, I have a sense of calm that it’s all going to work out well for him and his team. I have seen it too many times for me to think otherwise.

If the Australian guy watching it on TV with only the most cursory understanding of the rules feels this way, I imagine it has a major effect on his team mates.

I’ll report back in 2 weeks time!

title: Birthday among boxes
text:
It is my wife’s birthday today.

We celebrated by getting up very early to get a jump start on work before the movers arrive, heaving boxes around, having a nightmare at the storage unit, and it isn’t even 3:30. Not exactly glamourous or romantic. Such is life when you’re moving, even temporarily, across the ocean.

What has been a decidedly burdensome day is an outlier though, because for the past 366 days, every moment has been nothing short of a delight.

People talk poorly of marriage, or how it’s a scam simultaneously oppressing women and/or robbing men. That hasn’t been my experience in the slightest, because I am married to the most wonderful person I have ever met.

This has been true for a while. What has changed is that Ky has since become a mother, and she has done an incredible job. Seeing her and Occi together never fails to make me realise how lucky I am.

So while today may not have the glamour a birthday deserves, it perfectly captures what makes Ky extraordinary – her resilience, her ability to find joy in chaos, and her unwavering dedication to our family’s journey.

Happy birthday my love.

title: Takeoff
text:
I write this on the tarmac of SFO, steeling ourselves for our first long haul flight with our three month old daughter.

Our first short haul flight was an 7/10, but averages can be misleading. The process at the airport was a solid 3/10, as we hauled 5 bags of luggage around. The flight itself was a 9.5/10, as she was a delight from the moment we took off. In fact, she was a minor celebrity!

However, it was the final 20 minutes that brought the average down. Way down.

Sadly, being able to equalize yourself on the descent isn’t genetically coded, and needs to be learned. When you’re barely past “holding head up”, it’s a tall order, and thus it was a loud, painful, and unpleasant experience for all.

Fingers crossed it’s better this time!

title: Explanation
text:
A few of you have reached out to wonder if Ky and I have put an end to our US adventure. I appreciate the concern!

The answer is no. Ky and I have decided to make the most of maternity leave and spend two months in NZ, with a few side trips to Aus for my sister’s engagement party and a friend’s wedding.

So no, we’ll be back in the US, but for now we’re going to enjoy our winter escape!

title: CNY
text:
Gong Xi Fa Cai!

Happy Chinese New Year to everyone. It’s one of my favourite holidays of the year, and a chance to eat great food with loved ones.

Chinese New Year wasn’t something I grew up with, but I have really taken to it since being with Ky.

Enjoy the year of the snake!

title: Reset
text:
A buddy and I have been running a fantasy premier league football tournament, but the last two months it’s been a pretty low priority.

Hardly surprising.

With only a few more weeks to go, I decided to do a reset. The rules are that you can only change one player per week, but once a season you can revamp your whole team with no penalty. There was never going to be a better time than now.

My big move was dropping Salah, a regular top scorer. I thought that dropping him would all me some extra cash to have a more balanced team.

Sadly, he decided to become a points monster over the weekend, so the gamble didn’t pay off. Or at least, not yet.

As always, we shall see

title: Sun up to sun downing
text:
It’s been a while since I went to bed earlier than the sun. In fact, I can’t really remember the last time that happened, maybe when I was sick?

But after two long days, and very early starts, it’s a not just the best option, it’s a luxury.

title: Affordable luxury
text:
I love the idea of affordable luxuries. One could argue it’s a fallacy; how could anything affordable, or even free, be a luxury good?

To those people, try some of the French butter, Lescure, on a slice of quality bread, with an optional sprinkle of Maldon salt. A single slice probably costs you a dollar or two, max, but it’s the most perfect bite. It’s 10x the price of margarine on WonderWhite, but doesn’t break the bank.

Even better are free luxuries, and I was reminded of one today. Eating outside on a beautiful day doesn’t cost a red cent, but it always makes me feel as though I’m on the very best of holidays.

If you have an affordable luxury that you would recommend, please let me know!

title: One of many
text:
If you had to name the most famous painting in the world, you’d probably land on the Mona Lisa. However, if you asked for the second one, you’d probably end up with either The Scream or The Wave.

It’s an image I have seen countless times, but today, there were three things that stuck out to me.

 1. Mt Fuji is in the background. The print is clearly Japanese, but this was the first time I had ever seen the famous peak.
 2. There are people within the boats. They’re small, but they’re definitely there!
 3. The image was one of 36. All the others have faded away to obscurity, or near to it, but this one stood the test of time. This is what they call in the VC game a “grand slam”

The more you know eh!

title: Core memories
text:
I spoke with two friends today over Zoom, one of whom is training to be a psychiatrist. As our chat waxed and waned, we ended up on the topic of core memories, a term borrowed from the Pixar (?) classic, Inside Out.

Core Memories are memories that don’t just stick; they shape. Good, bad, ugly, we all have them, but we don’t always share them.

My psychiatrist friend said that one thing she does during her sessions is ask people what the top 3-5 most defining events of their life are, and she finds this to be a very effective way of cutting through the clutter.

That surprised me, because at first glance I imagine people would be slippery and evasive when asked directly. But perhaps not, sometimes the direct approach really is the best one, especially when you’re in a confidential environment.

I’ll think more about it, and let you know what conclusions I come to.

title: Supply and Demand
text:
The final of the Carabao Cup, the little brother of the FA Cup, is due to be held on March 16th at Wembley. It’s a date pencilled into my calendar, for as a long suffering Newcastle United fan, they have somehow, against the odds, made it to the big dance.

Out of curiosity, I went online to check out the ticket prices (cheeky long weekend away, anyone?!) and quickly realised I would be watching it on TV. At £800 minimum to be up in the gods, it wasn’t going to happen.

What I did find interesting was that there was a significant price difference between seats on the Newcastle side compared to those on the Liverpool side (European sports divide the supporters). And no, it’s not what you think; the Liverpool tickets were significantly cheaper.

On the surface, this makes no sense. Liverpool are one of the biggest clubs in not just England, but the world. Newcastle, on the other hand, is a local club; I am a real rarity in that I support them with no connection to the area (Mum is a West Ham supporter, my uncle is a QPR fan, and that’s it really).

My theory is that he Liverpool tickets are cheaper not despite their size, but DUE to it. Let me explain:

Currently, Liverpool are top of Premier League table, favourites for the Champions League, and until this morning, favourites for the FA Cup. Last year they won the Carabao Cup, by far the “4th” trophy in any season. So if you’re a ‘Pool fan, then you have plenty of “big” games to go to. You look at an event like this, and think, “hey, it’s only the Carabao, I think I’ll save my cash and instead spend it on a Champions League match in Munich or Barcelona instead”.

For the Newcastle fans, who haven’t won a major trophy since 1955, this is a big as it gets, and they must be feeling confident that this really could be “their year”.

After a 70 year wait, can you blame them?

title: Branche, bébé, branche!
text:
There are many Trump quotes, some of which I like. One I am not so keen on is “drill, baby, drill”, referring to his love of expanding oil exploration in the US, particularly in protected areas. While I am a big fan of fracking, I’m not fully onboard with this.

Regardless of my own views, it has become common parlance, even used semi-frequently in some dentist clinics. But you know things have really taken off when world leaders begin remixing it.

This is precisely what happened when Emmanuel Macron, president of France, did recently, when he announced that he wanted to “Branche, bébé, branche!”, or “Plug, baby, plug!”

In the AI race, people assumed it was just going to be a battle between the US and China. However, Europe, and in particular France, has entered the chat. Thanks to their enormous nuclear power plants, as well as elite level talent, France has everything it needs to be the third player in the game.

To test this hypothesis, I did what everyone should do, downloaded Le Chat onto my phone. Bromidically named, but apparently both cheaper and faster than ChatGPT, I’ll get back to you with my findings.

title: Distraction List
text:
Don’t you hate it when you’re trying to focus on some work, and the merest of distractions shanghaies your attention (‘just for a second’, you tell yourself)? I know it’s slurped an impossibly large amount of my time over the past three and a bit decades.

Discipline works, but it’s hard, and fallible, so we all need a system. For me, the one thing that has worked for me in the past is having a note-book where I jot down intrusive thoughts. Combined with the pomodoro timer (25 mins on, 5 mins off), it works well.

I don’t always use it, but I should.

It has at the very least allowed me the time to write this post!

title: Masculinity
text:
I quite like the academic Scott Galloway, better know as Professor G. A marketing professor at NYU, he has gained far more reach with his commentary on the world at large, particularly its issues. I subscribe to his weekly newsletter (you can do so HERE), which is always worth reading.

I recently heard him on the Theo Von podcast, where he outlined what he believes is the three-legged stool of masculinity in the 21st century, and because he works in marketing, of course it has to be alliterative:

 * Provider: Prof G believes a good man provides, and in a capitalistic society, men who are unable to provide will always struggle
 * Protector: this ranges from the local to the global. He thinks it’s far better to stop bar fights than start them, and it’s hard to argue with that
 * Procreator: his most contentious claim, but it certainly has merit. Most men love their children, I know I do, and that it helps them feel they are living a good life

Obviously it’s not exhaustive, and it’s not true for all people at all times. But I thought it was really well put, and I have thought about it a bit the last day and a bit.

title: 18 holes
text:
I played my first game of golf today.

No wonder it’s popular.

As I sit here writing this, a bit sunburnt and a little worse for wear, I look back on today as a good one.

What surprised me most was that the barrier to entry was far lower than I thought. Having barely done more than a mini-golf, I thought would be a major hindrance on the team. This fear actualised quite viscerally on the first hole, where I hit the ball so poorly I could have caught it.

This is a scene you seldom see on the pro tour

But over time, and thanks to a few tips, I acutely had a few good shots, and certainly had a ripper time.

I’ll be back.

title: Cora
text:
I used to practice Inbox Zero, or at least my version of it. To do this, I more or less opened every email, with the occasional “mark all as read” cheat.

It worked, until it didn’t. The rate I signed up for things exceeded the rate I unsubscribed, and over the years, it has grown to the point where I’m averaging, probably, 500 emails a day. It’s absurd, and it’s way, way too much.

Gmail does some of the heavy lifting by triaging the spam and promotions (“Someone you haven’t talked to in 17 years just got promoted!”) but that still leaves a lot of fluff.

As I write this, I have 3000 unread emails. 98% of them have very little use to me.

A few days ago, a solution arrived. Cora is a free service that reads your emails, and then twice a day, gives you a brief on what was in there. For emails it feels are important, they go straight to your inbox, but for the rest, you get a scrollable summary.

From the creator

So far, I’m loving it, and is has solved a problem I was aware of, but hadn’t fully articulated.

As an early beta user, I have a link that allows you to skip the waiting list, which can be accessed HERE.

I’m not remunerated in any way, I just wanted to share something I have found super useful.

title: Alive
text:
Oscar Jenkins, my former school buddy, has been found alive in Russia. For those who don’t remember, Oscar has enlisted to fight for the Ukrainian Foreign Legion, and had been captured two months ago by Russian forces.

I wrote about it HERE.

My fear was that once captured, the chance of Mr Jenkins staying alive were slim. But today, there is proof out that while he is still in a tricky situation, he is, most importantly, alive.

title: R1-1776
text:
Perplexity is one of my favourite Large Language, but today, I thought they did something really cool.

Many of you may have heard about DeepSeek, a Chinese model that isn’t only incredibly powerful, but startlingly cheap. It only had one downside; it was clearly a propaganda tool.

For weeks, there were endless memes in the AI space showcasing people’s attempts trying crack the code, but the AI stood firm. Props, I guess.

To counter this, Perplexity have created R1 1776, a a replica of DeepSeek, with all its reasoning capability, but trained to remove any propaganda or CCP influence.

Three cheers!

title: Prompting
text:
I have been a fan of language learning recently, and today I think I added a new one; prompt engineering. Not fluent, by any means, but at least I have started.

Prompt engineering is a lot of things, but at its most basic, it’s talking to LLMs in a way that maximises efficiency. Think of it like direct instructions; instead of asking ChatGPT to give you a recipe, you ask it to give it you step by step, using Celsius, and in a way that someone who is inexperienced in cooking might best understand. In both scenarios you get the recipe, but one is clearly better.

The proof will be in the metaphorical pudding tomorrow, when I present my findings to our AI squad. Fingers crossed.

title: Stung
text:
I was bitten by a wasp today. It’s not something I think I have ever experienced, and unsurprisingly, not one I will be running back to repeat.

Pain is a hard thing to describe. This particular sting felt like a hot wire poked onto the back of my hand, which is unusual in two ways: everyone describes it the same way, yet almost no one has experienced that sensation themselves.

The imagination is strange and wonderful.

title: The White Lotus
text:
Season three is back, baby!

I’m not much of a tv guy. While I don’t shy away from YouTube, it’s rare that I will indulge in a series. That might change with the White Lotus, one of my favourite shows of the past few years.

Despite missing the first half, I was quickly thrilled that all the main characters were deliciously unhinged, and in an environment that is both humid and alcohol drenched, that usually leads to explosive situations.

I was pondering what exactly I like about White Lotus, and I think it’s the sweet spot between easy watching and clever writing. Head too much in one direction, and you have Emily in Paris; lovely, but you can skip entire seasons and still catch up on what’s going on in two minutes. Alternatively, I have watched the first few episodes of Season 1 of the Wire at least three times, and I struggle with figuring it all out.

Here’s to a good season.

title: Three years
text:
This is how long the war in Ukraine has raged.

I remember the day it happened. I was at my old job at Kiwibank, and our entire team had a live stream of Kiev in the corner of our screen, waiting for the tanks to roll in. Everyone and their dog expected it to happen in just a matter of hours.

But it didn’t. Zelenskyy proved himself to be an incredibly effective leader, both at motivating his people, defending their land, and perhaps most importantly, raising funds to keep the effort going.

There were moments of optimism along the way – for example, the much vaunted “Spring Offensive” – but it seems that the odds of victory for Ukraine now seem slim. There are three paths ahead: Russia succeeds in taking over Ukraine, the West fully supports Ukraine with their own troops and pushes Russia out, or they make a deal. The last option is both the most likely, and probably the best.

Worryingly, the only person who can realistically make this happen is the former star of the Apprentice. The good news is that the Abraham Accords were a masterstroke in foreign policy, the bad news is that the current signs don’t look nearly as promising.

As we speak, the G7 are all meeting to discuss a plan. I expect this week to be a memorable one.

title: Chart of the week
text:
I don’t know if this will be a regular feature, but I saw this chart recently, and just had to share it.

This is number of Software Jobs posted on Indeed in the past four years. It’s a bleak picture if this is the game you’re in, and an even worse one if you’re trying to join the party.

Smart people, like yourself, might point out that this is just a return to normal, and they’d be right. On the demand side of things, the index is around 78 at the start of 2024, so lower than it was pre covid, but not by much.

What this picture doesn’t capture is that the supply side has exploded. CS majors have increased by 30%. The amount of people doing boot camps, or learning online, has grown substantially as well. Three or so years ago, doing a 12-week bootcamp could land you a job at Microsoft. Now, you won’t even get a call back.

Labour market corrections are the most painful, and sadly, the tools we have to counter it are limited. There WILL be millions of aspiring software engineers who will have to look, like journalists, switchboard operators, and fletchers in times past, for different lines of work.

The good news is that Software Engineering allows you to build cool things, and with AI and plenty of cash floating around, there is no reason why many of these young men and women can’t create their own jobs.

title: Happy Families
text:
Tolstoy famously wrote that “all happy families are alike, each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.” Known as the Anna Karenina Principle, this observation has been applied to everything from equities to microbiomes.

How? The basic idea is that there are countless ways to fail, but to succeed, you need a common set of requirements that you fulfill or exceed. A championship team must have strengths across the board—offense, defense, midfield, coaching, etc.—whereas a team with just one weakness (like a poor goalkeeper) will struggle.

I was thinking about this today at my sister-in-law’s birthday celebration. It was a lovely day, with three generations of Pattisons enjoying each other’s company, sharing food, and having fun. Even the “outsiders” like myself felt right at home.

It’s always a pleasure to see a happy family, and as someone who also had the great fortune to grow up in one, I can confirm that they really are very much alike.

title: Keep it fun
text:
I have been spending the afternoon coding on Cursor, an AI-powered code editor. It’s had a few updates, which are really, really awesome.

The one that grabbed my attention, and that I just had to share, came from the Composer. Here, you can write commands for what you want coded, and the AI will write the script for you.

The default setting requires you to approve any modifications made in the terminal. If you want to do download a new package, or install an update, you have to manually click “accept” each time. That is, unless you choose the auto-accept mode.

But they don’t call it auto-accept, they call it Yolo mode. I like that a lot.

Keep it fun.

title: Do or die
text:
ChatGPT is launching their latest model, 4.5, next month. It’s an important milestone for two reasons.

One, despite what the bots on X say, OpenAI and ChatGPT are the flagship of the industry. There are plenty of ride-share companies, but only one Uber. Being in that position means there will always be a LOT of attention on you, so missteps are painful.

At the same time, a successful launch could be another step forward in solidifying that position as leader. Rewards flow to the top; being there creates a flywheel that brings in more money, which brings more talent, which brings a better product, which brings more money.

The other milestone is more personal. I haven’t been having the best experience with OpenAi, and after being a member for well over a year, I’m thinking of dropping it. My membership with Claude, particularly after they dropped their latest model, continue to amaze me, and provides everything I need.

My membership just ticked over today, so Sam Altman has 30 days to prove it’s worthwhile.

We shall see.

title: Same time, but different
text:
After a wonderful three weeks up in Northland, we are back in Auckland for the week.

Ultimately, we decided to fly. It’s a short one, only 40 minutes, and amazingly, required no ID check or security. Clearly the number of radicalised terrorists in the semi-tropical regions of the South Pacific are either low in number or appallingly organised.

We could just as easily have driven, and it would have taken about the same amount of time, door to door. But petrol is expensive, and a young baby isn’t the best road trip companion. It was certainly the right decision, and there is something fun about going up in the twin prop.

title: Broken Hill, Broken Clock
text:
I found out yesterday a strange geographic fact.

Working and traveling abroad requires me to be up to date with time zones; which always gets me to thinking about the curious case of South Australia, which has a 30 minute difference to the Eastern Seaboard. I don’t know anywhere else in the world that has gone for the half hour time zone.

But that’s not the strange thing. What had me scratching noggin is how there is town within New South Wales, which has Sydney as the capital and is on the east coast, that goes by the South Australian time zone, not the time zone of the state it is in. It would be the equivalent of San Fran being on Mountain time.

This town has the romantic name of Broken Hill, and fun fact, was where the huge mining company, BHP, was founded (Broken Hill Proprietary).

This quirk is due to Broken Hill’s closeness to the SA border, and how the only train line connected them to South Australia, not their home state. Thanks to the train line, and then tradition, Broken Hill continues to be 30 minutes behind the rest of the state.

title: Helpful ears
text:
We all have friends that we can lean on when we need advice, but we often don’t reach out as much as we should.

You’d be surprised by their advice and insights, and how much people really are willing to help.

title: Video Editing
text:
Perhaps you’ve made a video before, perhaps you haven’t. Either way, I can guarantee you that it takes longer than you imagine.

Perhaps that is true for all creative endeavors. As I reflect on it, I think there might be something to that.

Videos, like paintings or gardens, are never really “finished”. There is always something you could add, or change, or improve; meaning the creator has to be the one who decides “enough!”.

Secondly, each production is different. I have made the same recipe multiple times, and it’s largely autopilot. But each video is unique, and while the skills improve, the content changes.

If you’re making a video on a deadline, good luck!

title: What are the odds?
text:
We all have places where we know we’re almost certainly going to bump into someone we know. For some readers, it’s the MCC, or Collin’s Street, or Central Park.

For Ky, and to a lesser me, Auckland Airport is a happy hunting ground. While we had no luck this time, by God’s good graces we sat in front of an old school friend, Rhys D.

We swapped stories about our lives – Rhys has just had his first child – and it made the experience on my least favourite airplane a whole lot more pleasant.

Like all working dads with a free moment, he swiftly nodded off, but a serendipitous moment nonetheless.

title: A job well done
text:
Ky, Occi, and I are in Melbourne for the weekend. We’re having a wonderful time, but only thanks to a lot of people doing their jobs well.

Ky, as always, is doing an incredible job planning and executing the next few days. It’s incredible how well she pulls things together, and I’m constantly amazed at how she does it. What often goes unnoticed is the level of self-sacrifice attached, and how she will go home early to care for bubs if she’s getting tired.

Mum, aka Nabs, did a great job this evening while Occi was unsettled. It was a tough gif; Occi usually hits the hay at 8pm NZ time, the exact time our Aus friends arrived to say hi this evening. Having someone do the hard work – hugging and soothing – allowed me to spend time with friends which is apportioned out once or twice a year.

Finally, James, the owner of the establishment we visited, did a stand-up job. Operating a hospitality venue in Melbourne is tough, but James was able to deliver a wonderful evening for all; from drinks, to nibbles, to a late dinner for all.

I write this feeling very blessed.

title: Big day
text:
Not just a big day, but a great day. We went to a family trip to the art gallery to see an incredible exhibit, then finished with the best dinner celebrating my sister’s engagement.

More to come tomorrow!

title: Disappointed
text:
I was due to write about my time in Melbourne, but honestly, the time has escaped me, and I am in no position to write the things I mean to say.

Instead, I’ll pick some low hanging fruit and say how saddened I was by the inflationary curse that has hit Australia’s liquor stores.

However, not all is lost. Wine in particular seems particularly good value, and I have had the good fortune to try many of them this long weekend.

title: Australia
text:
I’m writing this on my way from a whirlwind 5 days in Australia. It’s been great.

You probably have noticed that the most recent posts have been rather shabby, if not downright absent of any valuable content. Well, consider this post a proper catchup.

We flew in on Wednesday night, and besides sitting behind good friend Rhys, it was largely uneventful. Occi had a plane party, which was better than a meltdown, but tiring nonetheless. Little did we know, things would only get more exhausting.

Halfway!

Thursday evening was at a close school friend’s wine bar, Gilbee’s. James did a wonderful job hosting us, and at the 11th hour able to conjure up a delicious and outrageously good value dinner for 12. It really was the most wonderful way to kick things off and catch up with friends.

Friday was getting on top of a bit of work, then heading to the National Gallery to see the Yayoi Kasuma exhibition. I didn’t know much about her, but at 94 years of old, she keeps creating. I’m not always the greatest connoisseur of art exhibitions, but this one was excellent, enjoyable, and the perfect length of time. If you’re in town, do check it out, and absolutely book tickets ahead of time!

That night we celebrated the engagement of my sister and her fiancé, Ben. It was a special evening in every way, from the food, to the wine, to the speeches. Most of all, the company shone through, as we had residents from 4 different states converge for the night. Magic.

Saturday was another family affair, this time at my sister’s place who host a BBQ. It was casual dining at its finest; think long tables, lots of fresh salad, juicy meat, tasty vino and of course, the cheese. It will be hard to beat as meal of the year.

Finally, we got to Sunday, which featured a visit from one of my closest friends and his wife. This meal was in many ways similar to the one previous; tasty, relaxed, and sadly, way too brief.

Today was the most relaxed day by far, where we able to visit some work friends, pack our bags, and fly back to NZ.

It’s been a lot, and I was hoping Occi could catch some zzz on this flight, but she’s still soaking up every moment. Needless to say, this occasional would never have worked without the magnificent generosity of my two sisters, who fed and housed us, our friends who came to say hi, partially Lachy and Hanna who flew in from Tasmania for the occasion, Daniel and Felipe, my team at Innovera for their understanding, Tracy and Matt for DRIVING from Adelaide for the weekend, Deb and Johno for more sensibly flying, and above all, my darling wife for everything she does for this family.

All around, marvelous.

title: Back to it
text:
It’s back to work, after a few days off. Our trip to Australia was flat chat, and arriving at 1 am didn’t make things any easier.

Today wasn’t the most productive day I have ever had, but work is a marathon, not a sprint, and burning yourself out on a Monday is a fool’s strategy.

title: Envy & Similarity
text:
I read something very briefly in my inbox this afternoon about whether Social Media was driving a rise in envy. The case was persuasive.

The author, Rob Henderson, discussed how when he was a child, a popular tv show was MTV Crips. The premise was simple; people like Jay Z and Ludacris would bring TV crews in to film their often outrageous homes.

Mr Henderson, who grew up in poverty and passed between foster homes, never felt jealous or envious of how these rappers lived. After all, Monsieur Ludacris was not only a global star, he also lived in the “big city” and was, of course, an adult. They do things differently.

What really got Rob’s blood pressure climbing was seeing the kid next to him in class with a pair of new sneakers. They may have even been the same brand and type as the one’s Rod wore, only these were white and crisp, or at least whiter and crispier.

This whole story tried to capture an idea that I think is quite illuminating, envy is a local issue. Kids get peeved that they didn’t make it into their accelerated maths class in Year 9 while their friend did; they’re not at all concerned who won the Field’s Medal or got accepted into MIT. Teenagers aren’t heartbroken because Ana de Armas isn’t in their DMs, it’s because Sofia is going to the dance with Timmy instead. The examples are endless.

The issue with Social Media is that suddenly the world between Ludacris and Timmy is a lot smaller. There are kids – same age, same crummy haircut, same lame dance moves – who is famous across town for, well, something. They’re not just more famous than you, they’re 9123% more famous than you, based on views and likes. Their potential for envy has now risen swiftly!

I thought this was a pretty novel take, and seems to be true. I’ll give it more thought, but would love to hear what you think!

title: Double-dip
text:
We went into town today to see Ky’s old work, and when we arrived, Occi had done a remarkably creamy turd. The combination of the roadtrip, introduction to solids, and a three-day brew made it particularly pongy.

We (almost) came prepared; and car boots really do make great change tables in a rush. Sadly, we only brought one outfit which sadly got a dash of the Coleman Mustard, but again, nothing a pub bathroom sink and air dryer can’t fix when combined with a touch of inventiveness.

Our parenting strategy

We thought the shit was literally and metaphorically behind us, until on the way home from the event Occi decided to go full meltdown. We tried everything, from songs, to feeding, to moving to the backseat, to wild dancing. None of it worked.

Turns out that losing what felt like 1/3 of your body weight in Coogee Bay Icecream doesn’t prevent you for going back for seconds a few hours later.

A lesson learned.

title: Magic, again
text:
I recently heard some feedback about how they hate it when I write about AI, so I’m going to try and limit it going forward. However, this one I could NOT pass up on.

NotebookLM have come up with a novel way of sharing information. In this instance, our company was able to upload a few documents, and in a few minutes a 20 minute podcast is made, with two guys and a girl, sitting around a table discussing Innovera. It’s super natural; largely due to their errors. They cut each other off, then apologize. They correct one another when they go off track. They make jokes.

It’s better than 50% of podcasts out there, and it’s only just the beginning. If you’re as big as Joe Rogan, you’re fine, but for those with smaller audiences trying to something fairly basic, things got a whole lot tougher.

You can listen to the full interview below; trust me, it’s magic.

title: Swimming lessons
text:
We took Miss Octavia to her first swim lesson today, which was just absolutely lovely.

I haven’t asked her, but I think she would score it a 6/10. Not exactly thrilled, but didn’t seem to mind at all. More than anything, she seemed a little surprised what all the fuss was about.

I don’t have many hard and fast rules about what I want my daughter to do in life, but one thing which is non-negotiable is being able to swim.

In Australia, it’s inconceivable that you don’t know how to swim. I joke that I don’t know how to, but that’s because my butterfly sucks, and I’m overall rather slow. However, I love having a splash around in the ocean, and hopefully Occi will too one day.

title: How to optimize
text:
We had catch up drinks with a few pals today, and it had Ky and I wondering, how do you optimise these things?

On one hand, having a gang of people round can be helpful to catch up with a lot in a limited amount of time. Travelling sucks, and a small child and a long commute make it even worse.

At the same time, seeing 15 people across a few hours means you barely scrape the surface, and again, a child steals attention every which way.

There probably isn’t a solution, at least not in the short run, and might just be the cost of doing business when it comes to maintaining friendships across continents.

Regardless, it was a very pleasant afternoon.

title: At last!
text:
I try to get a sleep in on my days off, but today was an exception. As a long suffering Newcastle United supporter, there was no chance I was going to miss my chance to watch history unfold. You see, long suffering is an understatement, the last time the Magpies won ANYTHING was the 1969 Inter-Cities Fair (A now defunct competition designed to bring increased interest to trade fairs), and the last time they won a domestic trophy was the 1955 FA Cup. 70 years is a long time to wait, and at 33, I didn’t dare gamble another multi-decade wait just for a few extra moments of shut-eye.

Spoiler alert, despite the pundits, bookies, and online chatter, the team from Newcastle got the chocolates. And the best thing about it was they well and truly deserved it.

Knockout competitions have an element of luck to them. A lottery decides whether you get to play home or away, against Premier League giants or League 2 minnows. Getting a “lucky” draw means that you can overperform, but for NUFC, their path to Wembley went through the likes of Nottingham Forest (currently 3rd), Chelsea (currently 4th) and Arsenal (currently 2nd). An easy draw it was not.

Secondly, and I know I am biased, but Newcastle really were the better team on the day. There is no denying that their opponents, Liverpool, have played some scintillating football this year, and will deservedly be league champions for 2025. Nevertheless, this was not their day.

Warning: you are now about to read some bottom-tier football wonkery based off half-truths, vibes, and misremembering. You have been warned.

It is common to blame the coach when things go wrong, but in this instance, the criticism is fair. I haven’t watched many Liverpool games, but even Blind Freddy could tell you the following:

 * Salty Salah: Mo Salah, the best player at Liverpool, the league, and arguably the world, barely made an impact across the entire game.
