We have all had a situation where we wished we had the words to stand up to a bully, a bore, or a belligerent. Sadly, the words come too late, and you end the conversation silent, looking like a stunned mullet.

Bad outcome.

I’ll let you know my approach and the outcome.

title: Big day
text:
What a great day today was! There was so much on, I don’t really know what to focus on.

Let me give you a sampling.

It was an early start. 5:45. We all had to rise, even Occi, to have a photoshoot to capture those precious moments of her still being a little croissant. We won’t know until the photos come back, but it was a gorgeous morning, and if we get only one photo, it’ll be worth it.

After we got back, we had another thing I planned to write about occur, the fire alarms! One decided to wander off the reservation, and caused the whole suite of them to malfunction! They were very loud, and it gave us the opportunity to test how fast the Denver Fire Brigade would reach our home if an emergency were to occur (answer: very fast).

This interruption caused me to be late to my first touch rugby training session, which deserves its own article; expect it tomorrow.

Finally this evening was a dinner party with friends and a chance to celebrate the start of summer. Another article in the making.

Overall, it was a big day, and a great day.

title: Watch list
text:
I dropped my watch on the concrete outside this morning, and while it still works perfectly, I have chipped some of the glass. Perhaps the end days are coming soon.

If my watch was to die, I’m not sure where to go next. I wore swatch watches for years, but I have had a few issues with them lately, and fear their quality has gone downhill, particularly with the straps. My current watch, a many generations out of date Apple Watch, is fine, but I hate having to charge it all the time.

Any recommendations would be greatly appreciated!

title: Chess, not checkers
text:
Last Sunday, I went to my first Touch Rugby training session. I mentioned it a few days ago, but now is the time to explain how it went.

To put it mildly, it was completely discombobulating. I had played casual games of touch in the past at school and with friends, and liked how it was very much “pick up and play”. Success was largely down to avoiding mistakes, exploiting your opponents mistakes, fancy footwork, and most importantly, speed. That is NOT what I experienced down at the park last Sunday.

There were a few major changes I noticed between what I was used to, and what the current USA National Champions did.

 1. It was VERY tactical, and with those tactics came a ton of lexicon. While the overall strategy was fairly straightforward (use your first five touch to move the ball up the field and encourage the opposition to bunch up, then spread it wide into space), it was the tactics to get you there that were complex. The team had about five or six set plays that they would run, all with completely unobvious names such as Smash or Colours.
 2. The best tactic is to exploit instincts: While there were a lot of tactics, they all revolved around luring your opponents into making a mistake by letting them play naturally. There is a natural pull towards the ball, this causes space out wide. Even though everyone knows this, it still keeps working
 3. Subs are critical. In some sports, a substitution can take minute or two to do. In touch, it’s more like ice hockey, where players run off and on super quickly to try and get an advantage of freshness. When done well, it is magic, but you have to be alert.

I’m already looking forward to the next session.

title: We’re back
text:
After what feels like a month long break, test match cricket is back. Australia is playing South Africa in England (very much a Commonwealth affair) and while Aus is on top, they haven’t run away with it. South Africa does need a big morning though, but with Bavuma at the crease, I wouldn’t write them off.

It starts at 3:30 every morning, which is too rich for my blood, but finishes just before 11:30. If I’m working on something that doesn’t require full attention, it’s a nice thing to have on in the background. Sadly, that isn’t often!

title: Ordinary Ordinances
text:
Our main road is being repaved.

How do I know this?

Well, to begin with, our car was towed this morning.

It’s an absolute disgrace. There has been no warning, no letters, no one telling us what is going on. Fortunately, towing just meant round the corner, literally, but they still whacked us with a $175 fine.

I would pay it unhappily but without anger if it was my genuine mistake, but it wasn’t. We’ve clapped back, and hopefully we can have the ticket dismissed.

title: Is the world getting more dangerous, Part 2
text:
14 months ago, I wrote this piece wondering if the world is getting more dangerous. You can read it HERE.

I thought I would revisit it, in the wake of the Israeli attack on Iran. It feels like this will be a short conflict, given how devastated Iran was with this attack, but I can’t imagine they’ll give up without a fight. Rockets must be coming soon, but the Iron Dome is a hell of a technological marvel.

It really does feel like October 7th was a turning point for Israel, and they’re full “gloves off” when it comes to their foreign policy. It isn’t always pretty, but they live in a dangerous neighborhood, and you gotta be tough.

Ukraine was the other conflict I talked about, and after Operation Spider Web, which pretty much crippled half of the Russian Airforce. After more than three years of conflict, this presents as a major psychological and military victory for the Ukrainians. I work with a lot of them. They’re great, and I do hope their country overcomes.

Finally, I discussed three more countries: Haiti, Somalia, and Taiwan. They have all fallen off the map; I couldn’t tell you the last time I heard them discussed. This is unusual for Taiwan, but sadly typical for the other two. A cursory read over suggests that both Haiti and Somalia are still on the brink of collapse, if they haven’t fallen over completely already.

Is the world getting more dangerous? Not where I sit, but we certainly live in interesting times.

title: Moving day
text:
It’s a tough day. A long day. And a day you can’t skip.

It is, of course, the day you move house.

After 3 months living at our friend’s place, it’s finally time for us to move into our own home.

The real MVPs were the guys who did the moving, but there is still a ton of work that needs to be done.

Moving is always tough, but we’ll figure it out. We always do.

title: Father’s Day
text:
Today was my first Father’s Day. It was very special.

It might not look like that from an outsider’s point of view. We spent 90% of the time unpacking boxes as we slowly but surely move house.

But for me, it won’t be a day I forget. There were a number of highlights.

 1. Having breakfast at Denver’s premier bakery is always a treat. As the kids say, Denver food is decidedly mid, buy Bakery Four do a wonderful job. Whenever someone asks for my weaknesses, cheese and ham croissants are right up there
 2. BBQ with friends: Almost every culture celebrates socially by cooking meat with fire, but for some reason, you always think your particularly civilisation did it either better, or first. Being invited around to a friend’s place for a BBQ dinner, was a blast and a ton of fun.
 3. Occi: You don’t really get to celebrate Father’s day without being a father. And in many ways, I really couldn’t do it without Octavia. Seeing her smile and light up brings me so much joy, and it really is every day. I get to be silly with her. I read her books in french that neither of us understand. I cook omelets that neither of us are super thrilled about but chow down on anyway, hoping for better next time. It really is the best.

I know I have a few days who read this, so Happy Father’s day, even if you are in Aus/NZ, and this doesn’t apply. For those on the fence, and have the right partner and position, can I full throatily endorse hitting it raw.

title: Prompting
text:
I used to scoff at the idea of being a prompt engineer. What an absurd job, writing prompts for AI. Anyone could do that.

Well, as of this week, I’m the company’s chief prompt engineer. It’s a temporary, made up, and largely useless title, but the irony hasn’t escaped me.

title: Trust lost
text:
We all have heard that expression that trust takes a lifetime to build, and an instant to shatter. Often the examples we give are hypothetical or happened to a friend of a friend. If that’s you, I have another example for you to use.

I’m thinking about expanding my cooking repertoire. For the last few years I have developed a style based on YouTube videos, feel, and trial and error. But that only gets you so far, and I felt I had plateaued. So I borrowed two French cookbooks from the library, and decided to start working through them.

By work through, I don’t mean starting at Aardvark Stew and going down the list, I mean starting with the classics, and nothing sings French cuisine than Onion Soup. I had two books, picked one, and read the recipe.

I was appalled.

Without knowing anything about French Onion soup, I can still tell you that it requires:

 * More than one onion
 * Said onions need to be cooked for more than 10 minutes
 * The onions should not be strained from the broth before serving

I consulted 3 Michelin Star Chef, ChatGPT, and they were equally shocked.

No soup was made that night, and the book has promptly been returned to the library, never to be opened again.

title: Almost there
text:
We are almost 80% there with unpacking all our stuff.

It’s taken ages, but finally, FINALLY, we are starting to see some progress.

Having a useable dining table makes a world of difference.

title: DoD
text:
Aka Dad’s off Duty. That’s the name of our group (?), or at least that’s what I call it.

It’s pretty simple; a bunch of guys who catch up and do something together sans kids and partners. In fact, you don’t need to be a dad OR in a relationship, but it tilts that way.

This evening, we went to the baseball. Or at least we went to the ball park; these things revolve almost entirely around shit talking and drinking beer, and 5% around the game itself. Fortunately, tickets are VERY cheap and the Rockies are historically bad, so we don’t miss out on anything.

I’m already looking forward to the next one!

title: Lee was right
text:
One of my heroes of the 20th century was Lee Kuan Yew, the founding father of Singapore. His vision and leadership turned a disease ridden fishing village into a futuristic metropolis. I dare anyone to go to Changi Airport and not be amazed.

I’ll write about his second big idea tomorrow, but today, I would like to focus on one of his galaxy brain ideas; the importance of air-conditioning. Lee had studied at Cambridge University, and he quickly realised that doing knowledge work in a chilly King’s College library was FAR easier than doing so in a sweltering hut. So his first order of business: investing in air con.

Humans CAN live in a range of climates, but they do best around 20C/70F. Not great if you’re trying to catch some rays at the beach, but whether you’re manufacturing products or writing code, it’s hard to beat.

By investing heavily in AC, Singapore was able to become the knowledge capital of Asia, and become fabulously rich in the process. San Fran just happens to be in the perfect part of the world to achieve the same effect.

The reason this story is important, at least today, is that yesterday OUR AC went out. For 24 hours we were left to a couple of fans, and the temp was high 30s/90s. I mean, really, really hot.

Our main concern was looking after Swocky, and we did. But it would have got UGLY if we hadn’t fixed it. When you cannot sleep, work, focus, or relax, you really aren’t optimised.

Long story short, air conditioning is a modern miracle that builds nations, and most importantly, keeps me comfortable.

title: Put it on the Weber
text:
We bought a second hand Weber today for $80. Honestly, it might be one of the best deals I have ever found, and the burgers we grilled on it were juicy and delicious.

Years ago when I was rowing, we spent so much time together talking trash (or “talking rot” as we called it), that we were always coming up with new phrases and sayings.

Sometimes it was Cockney Slang (we used the word “rhyming” to mean keen, after some guy on the walls who was called Simon Keenan: Keen > Keenan > Simon > Rhymin’. It made sense to us).

Often it was more straightforward. One of the classics was replacing the phrase “getting grilled”, i.e being spoken to in a harsh or critical manner, with “being put on the Weber”. It could be a coach, an employer, a friend’s furious date; anyone really. If you told another athlete about how you were late to training and the coach “put me on the Weber for 20 minutes”, it would make perfect sense to everyone listening.

Hopefully 2025 will be more focussed on grilling meats, and less time of being verbally accosted.

title: Driving lessons
text:
One thing most parents dread is teaching their children to drive. Fortunately, we probably won’t have to.

Seeing videos of the Waymos, Robotaxis, and Tesla’s full self driving cars is crazy. They’re already safer than a human, and will only keep improving. One person described driving three hours from SF to Tahoe and didn’t touch the wheel once; they described it as “flying business class, but on the road”.

I can’t wait for my first autonomous taxi. It’s going to be similar to Uber when it first came out; lots of people suspicious at the start, but never going back to the old ways after trying it just once.

title: Half-bag Harry
text:
I fly off to SF tomorrow for two days. It’s a work trip, and hopefully will be quite productive.

Last time I went, it was just for the day, and I only brought a backpack. With all my work gear, spare shirt, toiletries etc., it was almost bursting.

This time, I am bringing a small carry on suitcase as well. It’s not even 50% filled, despite overpacking shirts, undies, and socks.

It’s a Scylla and Charybdis moment, but at the most minor of levels.

title: SF Trip
text:
I’m at the airport, waiting to fly back to Denver. The past two days in San Francisco have been great.

Travelling for work isn’t always glamorous, and being away from Octavia and Ky is horrible. But we had a very productive 48 hours, I was able to see one of my best buds from Oxford, enjoyed a succulent Chinese meal, and officially launched the website I had been working on for the past two months. It’s live and being used by our sales team, and I can’t wait to see how it works for them.

Going for two days means I don’t have to catch the 5am flight. It also means that tomorrow isn’t hell. But it does mean, at least on this occassion, that my flight has been delayed an hour, and I won’t get home until 2am.

Grim.

title: Date night
text:
Occi’s grandparents are in town, which gives us some flexibility. We have taken full advantage.

Or at least a decent amount.

This evening, we were afforded the opportunity to not only go visit friends for a appertivo, but follow it up with a trip out for dinner. It was Japanese, which never disappoints.

Date night is something Ky and I have always cherished and thus prioritised; and we know it’s critical for our relationship across every time horizon.

Plus it’s a lot of fun.

title: Hike + Feast
text:
With Ky going back to work tomorrow, the three of us took a much needed break to spend time together as a family.

First off, we went hiking. It was magical to be able to put Occi in our hiking backpack and show her something that the two of us love; getting out into nature.

She was usually happier than this, promise

Of course, halfway through she decided to unleash 72 hours worth of well marinated turd into her nappy, and naturally, we hadn’t brought a spare. Bear Grylls, the survival expert, was famous for a quote “Adapt. Improvise. Overcome”, and that is exactly what we did.

With Occi now feeling a LOT lighter, we were able to enjoy the rest of our walk.

Post shid bliss

When we got back, I was straight on the tools, making my favourite dish, Beef Wellington. It’s a labour of love, and I think I was cooking for 3.5 hours to bring it all together. It isn’t necessarily complex, but there are a LOT of moving parts, and at one point we had all four burners going for the carrots, the mash, the milk, and the red wine reduction. It turned out a treat; it really is as good as it gets.

A wonderful day.

title: Latency
text:
For a while, I have been thinking about this concept of “friendship latency”, or in non technical terms, how quickly two people become friends with one another.

There are a few people I have met where I hit it off straight away. Others have been a slower burn.

What is interesting is that low latency (you become buddies fast) doesn’t have as much explaining power as you’d imagine when it comes to depth of friendship; some buddies I have were pretty surface level until the 4-5 meet up, or longer.

I don’t have a reason as to why some connections happen faster than others, beyond the trivial and the obvious. If you have a better idea, let me know!

title: Dream Team
text:
AI Twitter has been a flutter ever since Meta, the disaster formally known as The Facebook, has decided to go ALL IN on their AI team, and poach the hottest talent in the market.

The story has been rehashed a thousand times, from a million angles, but the only two that matter are this:

 1. The Ambition
 2. The Pay

Meta already has an AI function, but it is widely considered second rate. That said, it IS free to use, and open source, so you can run it on Groq chips for lightening fast speeds (think of the Nos cylinders in Fast and Furious, but for computers). I often API it in when I need to trade off quality for speed and cost.

That all changes with the Dream Team. Clearly Zuck isn’t interested in helping people update their Facebook Status more succinctly, they want to be the leaders of the revolution. And while Facebook has taken a prestige hit, Instagram and Whatsapp are still massively profitable. They have the funds to do it.

Which brings me to the next point, the pay.

Tech salaries have been high for a while. Obviously there are the billionaire founders, but it isn’t uncommon for people to be making 250k a few years out of college, and it isn’t super rare for talented people in the Valley to be making 2-4x that (full disclosure: I am not one of these people).

But the new pay packages, if true, are wild. One AI researcher was offered $18m to join Meta, they turned it down to join a buzzier, cooler startup. Someone has accepted a $100m four year deal, but no one (well, someone, but not me) knows who. And Alexandr (not a typo) Wang sold 49% of his company, Scale AI, to become Zuck’s minion. Without knowing the cap table, it is likely that Mr Wang is now part of the tres commas club. Not bad for a 24 year old.

title: How do they do it
text:
I called my mother this evening, and due to a full house, decided to go down to the Curry Grill, a Nepalese themed sports bar.

As you can imagine, I was the only customer.

It was nice to be able to have a beer and chat away, but it did have me thinking, how the hell do they do it. A front? Cheap labour? An illegal poker game downstairs?

I’ll never know.

title: Christmas Flights
text:
It’s either very hot or miserably cold in your part of the world right now, and perhaps you’re thinking about some respite over the holiday period. Or perhaps you’re like most people, and haven’t really thought about it at all.

I thought I’d get a jump on it this year, and beat the rush. While it’s still early days, it’s nice to know at least my flights are locked in, and I’m being only mildly extorted.

title: Happy Fourth of July!
text:
A short one today, Happy Birthday to America!

We live in abundant times!

title: Date night
text:
Ky and I try to make a priority of date night, so it was magnificent to leave a sleeping Occi with the grandparents, and head off to our local cocktail bar.

They do a great job, but the real fun is spending some time together to chat and focus on being husband and wife, not just mum and dad.

title: 13 years
text:
I received an email today from LinkedIn, saying I had just clocked up thirteen years on the platform. It certainly hasn’t been memorable.

LinkedIn gets a lot of hate, and understandably so. Some of the most inane, masturbatory, and try-hard behaviour bubbles up on the site far too often, see Exhibit A below.

But some of the hate is unfounded. Yes, there is a bunch of low quality material. Yes, there are a ton of people who are congratulating themselves on new jobs. And yes, there are far, far too many people who treat the comments section like LinkedIn.

But it serves its purpose. Almost everyone I know is on it. It’s a great way to find clients, customers, and ultimately hirers. It was how I ended up with my job at Kiwibank, so I can’t be one to judge too harshly.

If I had to guess, most people spend very little time on LinkedIn, and when they do venture on, it is ONLY when they are looking for a new job, sometimes desperately. In the same way lawyers are often poorly perceived; the only time people require their services are when poor fortunate has befallen them.

title: Applying Lessons
text:
I am a third of the way through Nate Silver’s latest book, and it has had me thinking about risk-taking. The biggest thing I have taken from it so far is that people are not taking enough risk, or more accurately, they’re not taking appropriate risks.

What is a good risk? Well, at its most basic, it’s one with a positive Expected Value, or EV. EV is basically the likelihood of the positive outcome, multiplied by the payout of the outcome, minus the cost. Lottery tickets have negative expected value, even when they have enormous jackpots; the minuscule odds of winning make it worth less than the cost of a ticket.

The first thing to avoid when dealing with risk is limiting downside, aka the worst thing that can happen. Train surfing is probably fun, but the downside, death, makes the activity, at least in my eyes, EV negative.

The ideal bet, then, is one which has minimal downside, very large upside, and ideally, an edge. They’re hard to find, as you can imagine, but I think I have found one that is close.

Next season, my favourite football club, Newcastle United, is playing in the Champions League, where they’ll play against the elite European clubs. In their 133 year history, they’ve only played in the competition three times.

It would be great to see them play under the lights against one of the top clubs, but alas, it is VERY expensive. And of course, with a child, even more so, because we’d all want to go. I frankly can’t afford it.

But what if I could make a bet on it? A $100 bet (plus the new user bonuses that they’re always giving out) would yield a payout of over $5k, enough to pay for the entire trip. The upside is a once in a lifetime experience. The downside is $100.

It feels like the right strategy, but I’ll sleep on it. The odds aren’t moving anytime soon.

title: Getting Started
text:
My parents are/were keen bushwalkers. One of Dad’s favorite sayings was “90% of people who get lost bushwalking do so from the carpark”. I don’t know how true that is, but it seems directionally correct, and applies to more than just hiking.

After spending three hours trying to get a new project setup in my codebase, I have a new found respect for it.

title: Upgrading Work
text:
Today was a GREAT day at work. Which is a very good thing, because the last two weeks I have been feeling a little flat about it. Not sad, not willing to quit. Just not that excited, a bit hom hum. It was leading to a poor effort, which meant my work got a little sloppy, which I wasn’t happy about, which lead us back to a lack of motivation. Not a great place to be.

One of the issues I identified is that I wasn’t getting the most socially out of my work. Being fully remote has its perks, but it has been hard for me to build some of the friendships that got me through the trenches in previous jobs. So today, I made sure to spend some quality time with Felipe, my associate, as we built out our latest tool.

It was a blast. While we designed, coded, and deployed our work together, we cracked jokes, talked about ideas we had, and just genuinely had fun. He lives in Brazil, which means it is difficult for us to meet up, but I hope we will sometime either this year, or the next.

title: Blocked up
text:
Occi is unwell.

Not badly, but she has a bad case of the sniffles, and while it hasn’t dampened her spirits, it has made life a little bit tougher.

We’ve been lucky so far in avoiding illness, but it was only a matter of time. The little girl darts around the floor, army crawl style, more than our Roomba.

title: End of the week
text:
It has been a big week, and I’m absolutely stuffed. Time to take it easy.

A sick child and a couple of huge deadlines sure are demanding!

title: Over Promised
text:
We went as a family to the Denver Bastille Festival, which lasts for three days.

It was terrible. Strikingly so. There was the French Chamber of Commerce, a Crepes store, and a few empanada (?) stores as well.

Very disappointed, because nothing gets me more excited than a French Market!

title: No going back
text:
I learnt the hard way today that while you can always add salt, you can never really remove it.

Act accordingly.

title: ProxyCurl
text:
Scraping LinkedIn for info is really useful.

It’s also really difficult.

The company knows that they need to walk a fine balance between showing people information about Jobs, Businesses, and People, and making sure people don’t just replicate all that information themselves. They do this by focusing probably 80% of their dev team on this singular problem.

The result is that a cottage industry of digital mice, escaping the allegorical LinkedIn Cat, have sprung up, offering their services to scrape LinkedIn. The best of these is a Singaporean company called ProxyCurl.

Or should I say WAS. After being threated with a game-ending lawsuit from LinkedIn, the small band of merry scrapers decided to pack up shop, turn off the lights, and call it a day. It’s a shame, because they were very effective for our use case, and now we’re looking for another mouse.

title: Antiquated
text:
I read today that apart from the ultra high end stuff, antiques have essentially collapsed in value. One person estimated it at a 90% drop, which seems high, but it could be true. It certainly feels directionally true.

I can’t remember the last time I went to a peer’s home and saw some serious, made to last furniture. Probably when I went to a BBQ at Ellie Golding’s now ex-husband’s house in Gloucester. But for everyone else, it’s mostly just IKEA and FB marketplace. I include myself in that category.

I always assumed it was just really, really expensive. But perhaps not.

I’ll have to investigate.

title: Broken down
text:
To celebrate Bastille Day, I made Coq au Vin for dinner. It’s a classic for a reason.

It isn’t a particularly difficult dish, it just has a lot of parts, and takes a while. The result is spectacular.

It’s probably like most things in life; a combination of time, consistency, and getting the small things right.

title: So much fun
text:
Our dear friend Sydney had a surprise birthday party this evening. Her grandmother, who is now a great-grandmother, had made the trip from Austin to be there.

I used to think older people were gross, but these days, you realize they’re living treasures. What they have seen in their lifetime, and the changes that have occurred, are well worth hearing.

For Syd’s grandmother, her biggest comment was how much fun she’s had. Despite losing her husband recently, and a number of friends, she really felt as those she had lived a fortunate life.

It was cool to hear.

title: Wills
text:
Finally, we have finished writing our wills. Perhaps, dear reader, you’re in them, but you’ll have to wait and see.

It’s a strange experience, thinking about what happens are you wander off the mortal coil. For me, the hardest bits were figuring out the edge cases, such as if we both die, and their godparents are also dead, what happens to Octavia? At the point it’s probably world war 3, so our will probably won’t we worth the paper it’s written on, but we still needed a plan.

What made it a LOT easier was having two trustees of the will that we really trust. No matter what happens, minus global thermonuclear warfare, it feels like things will be ok once we’re gone.

title: Snowball
text:
My Apple Watch tracks my steps, minutes exercised, hours standing, and calories burnt.

I used to ignore all of it.

But now I have a bit of a streak going, and man, the snowball effect is REAL. To the point where I’m walking round the block at 10pm trying to knock off all my goals.

This is what they mean when they say “tech enabled”.

title: The Defenders of the Byline
text:
I don’t think I ever remember a time when there were journalists who garnered respect directly from the byline. By that, I mean people would read an article and say “Oh, that’s by Ms X, it must be good”. Most trustworthiness and prestige was held by the publication, not the individuals. Some organisations, such as The Economist, don’t even have bylines, although rumour has it that you can guess who write what quite easily (I’m not too sure).

There are, however, two holdouts though. Fabrizio Romano and David Ornstein are either two people you have never heard of, or you refresh their feed every 20 minutes. It really is one or the other.

If you’re in the former category, messrs Romano and Ornstein are football journalists who specialise in transfers. Through connections, dark arts, and a bit of luck, they are the ones that people seem to trust to break the news.

There are plenty of people in this space, ranging from BBC pundits to internet anons. Everyone knows that salacious stories such as “Will Ronaldo go to Auckland City this summer?” (the answer is, unsurprisingly, no) will generate clicks. But only a few are trustworthy, and that is a reputation to hold on to for dear life. Once gone, it’s never coming back, and when you have it, there is a virtuous cycle.

title: Citizen O
text:
Octavia received her Australian Certificate in the mail today, so she’s officially “on the team”.

Having dual citizenship is awesome.

Kylene and I have both benefited from our British passports when we worked and studied abroad (Ky studied in Germany and worked in England, I did it the other way round), and hopefully Octavia will have opportunities open up for in the future.

More importantly, it means she has another home. She will always be an American, and I am bullish that this is, and will continue to be, a good thing. But neither Ky or I are, and our paths are likely to lead us back to the South Pacific.

Now we just need the passport to save on those pesky NZ tourist fees!

title: A very useful tool
text:
If there is one tool in the kitchen which never ceases to impress me, it’s the thermometer.

You don’t NEED one, but I highly recommend it. The digital ones in particular are both affordable and effective.

The idea that you can use your fingers to test the doneness of steaks is completely codswallop, and a medium rare chicken is in nobodies bucket list.

Do yourself a favour.

title: The three Bs
text:
When I worked in banking back in NZ, I remember my boss explaining why our employer didn’t have a Private Banking arm. For those who aren’t familiar with the term, Private Banking is essentially a more hands-on, bespoke service that is offered to high net worth individuals. It’s very profitable.

The issue, or so he claimed, was that there simply weren’t enough candidates in NZ to make it worthwhile. This wasn’t just due to the low population (more people live in Colorado than in New Zealand), but also due to priorities. According to him, most New Zealanders were very motivated to get the 3Bs, but not much past there.

The three Bs are the Boat, the Bach (the Kiwi term for beach house), and the BMW. Once those are achieved, the desire for a lot of New Zealanders is to take their foot off the pedal, and go enjoy the good life. From the bank’s perspective, this is a tragedy.

In a place like America, and ESPECIALLY in places like the Bay Area, we always hear stories of people doubling down. The serial entrepreneur. The Elon Musk and Jamie Dimond types. Those who read Marc Andreesen’s manifesto and decided that yes, “it’s time to build”.

Is one way better? Depends on who you are. As someone who is a long way away from the 3Bs, I get the appeal!

title: Pulling the boots on
text:
Today I joined the Denver Bulldogs, arguably the Real Madrid of USA based Australian Rules Football clubs.

It was a blast, but boy, I’m sore writing this now. Hopefully I’ll toughen back up quick smart.

Playing Australian Rules Football internationally is an interesting experience. The main difference is that the teams often have huge deltas between player experience and ability, ranging from people who had played professionally in the AFL, to people who have literally no idea what the rules are.

It’s a lot of fun, and I’m looking forward to spending more time down at the club.

title: A night at the circus
text:
With the grandparents looking after Octavia, Ky and I have been taking the opportunity to enjoy some dates together. Last night, we went to Cirque du Soleil.

If you haven’t been, I HIGHLY recommend it. Whether you’re young or old, there is a sense of wonder, magic, and amazement seeing acrobats and circus performers hurl themselves in the air. The gasps from the crowd aren’t forced, they’re real.

This was the second time I have been to Cirque, and I would go again. It’s intimate, and because of the ring shape stage, there are no bad seats in the tent. Even in the peanut gallery where Ky and I sat, we were only 7 rows away from the action.

So, you may be wondering, what made the performance memorable. Two things:

 1. There was a guy that Ky and I called Slinky Malinky, who was, by far, the most flexible person I have ever seen. His performance was something out of the exorcist, as he twisted his head 180 degrees, used his arms as jump ropes, and twisted himself into various knots. It was hard to watch, and impossible to look away. A real circus freak!
 2. The other thing I’ll remember is how nice it was to spend an evening together with my wife. Occi is a delight and a gift, but it’s also nice to go out enjoy the company of my dearest.

title: Demi-glacé
text:
I have been making my own chicken stocks for a while; they’re super easy to do if you have ever roast chicken (which we do regularly!).

This was, however, the first time I ever did her big, beefier, brother; the Demi-glacé.

It took two days, but I have finally reduced almost 5 litres of water into 200ml of pure beef essence. When it’s that concentrated, it becomes a jelly.

I can’t wait to try it out tomorrow for a steak sauce. Everything I read about making a great sauce (which really can be the best part of a meal) hinges on a top quality base.

Fingers crossed I have one.

title: Planning
text:
August is a busy month for me. I’m back in Aus for a week, then off to Europe two weeks afterwards for a friend’s weddings. It’s a lot to plan for.

One thing that has added to the puzzle is planning work for my team while away. I manage a (very) small team at the moment, only one person, but that will grow to at least three by the end of the month.

In the past, I just set my OOO and was out the door. Now, I’m still holding the bag.

Any advice or thoughts, much appreciated!

title: Stages
text:
It’s a silly thing, but the last two weeks I feel as though I have been going through the five stages of grief over a football transfer.

Long story short, it looks like Newcastle’s best player is going to move to Liverpool. It is pretty disastrous for the club.

Originally I was in denial, surely these were all just rumours that get passed around. But the rumours didn’t stop. They got worse.

This lead to anger, and a bit of bargaining. Perhaps this will work out after all? Perhaps this is just 4d chess. When it didn’t play look like that, our comes stage for, depression.

Now that’s too strong a word, I’m certainly not depressed. But every single story being fed your way is just a reiteration of a rather negative situation. It isn’t pleasant.

The good news is I’m now at the point of acceptance. At the end of the day; it’s a pastime and a distraction. Win, lose or draw, 99% of the time it makes no impact on me, my family, my friends, my wife, job, child, and health.

Plus, the Chinese has a saying about events; “good luck, bad luck, who knows?”

I wrote about it a while ago, you can read that article here: https://harrywetherall.com/perhaps-we-will-see/

title: Mouthbreathing
text:
I spoke with a good friend yesterday. He’s one of life’s great conversationalists and adventurers, so I am always thrilled to hear what he is up to.

This time, the topic steered towards his latest fascination with breathing through one’s nose, and his disdain for “mouth-breathers”. Fascinating stuff. No, really.

The term “mouth breather” feels like ancient lore; the kind of insult we know means something (dim-witted, knuckle-dragger, neanderthal etc), but have forgotten where it comes from. Don’t we all, at least some of the time, breathe through our mouth? And is it really that bad?

Apparently so.

As my friend explained it; 60% of our face is dedicated to cleaning the air we breathe, but ONLY if we use our nose. Our mouth, due to it’s multiple functions of speaking, tasting and eating, just isn’t built for sucking in air day in, day out.

But facts are not what kept me interested, it was the anecdotes. According to my friend, were he to go to sleep without taping his mouth shut, he would wake up feeling as though he had crushed 19 pints the night before. Anyone can wax lyrical on the anatomical features and how they may or may not affect lifestyle, but a story like that is hard to dismiss.

Am I going to tape my mouth from now on at night? Yes, at least for a month.

I’ll report back my findings.

title: Take-off
text:
I’m writing this from Denver International Depatures, about to head back to Australia for a week. My mother is currently going through chemotherapy, and while it looks like she’ll be 100% fine, it’s still a tough experience for her, so I want to be back to see her.

My plans in Sydney are simple; spend time with family. I can’t wait.

title: Saturday the Missing
text:
I took off at 7pm Friday night, and landed at 7am Sunday morning.

If you asked me what I did on Saturday 2nd of August, 2025, the answer is absolutely nothing.

It just disappeared.

title: Luck of the draw
text:
I sent off Octavia’s Australian passport application today, after being told I didn’t have all the documents yesterday.

After spending a few hours picking up the required pieces of paper, I went to a different post office and tried again.

This time, zero issues.

The annoying thing was that it wasn’t the documents, it was the person behind the counter, that made all the difference.

title: I might be wrong
text:
For three years, I have been a firm supporter of Israel’s occupation of Gaza.

I have defended them, to friends and foe. I went to see Douglas Murray, and would send my Jewish friends messages of support and comfort. I drank the Kool Aid.

I’m certainly no Hamas supporter. I still the society that Palestinians have built is evil, backwards, cruel, and barbaric. I still think the Israelis who were massacred on Oct 7 were the victims of unthinkable malevolence.

But now, I think, is time for something to change. Being a father has given me a lot of empathy for other parents, and even if their desires, wishes, and even actions are evil, I want the suffering to end.

It’s a complex situation, and I think I might be wrong. But that is the first step towards being right.

title: Beef welly
text:
It’s my favourite thing to cook, and tonight I made it for my sisters.

Another success

title: The end of the beginning
text:
One of my favourite quotes comes from Winston Churchill.

“This is not the end. It is not even the beginning of the end. But it is perhaps the end of the beginning”.

That is how I feel right now. The whole tech world is poised for what could be a MOMENTOUS day tomorrow, when OpenAI releases their long awaited GPT-5. Or so the rumour goes.

I watched the 90 minute demo on the flight, and was floored. The improvements, particularly to coding, look like a significant improvement. I can’t want to check them out.

title: Reunion
text:
On Saturday, I attended my 15 year school reunion. I’m glad I went.

There are quite a few people I have kept in touch with since finishing up at Corio, but quite a lot of the people I hadn’t seen since year 12. Fortunately, most people looked exactly like they did last time I saw them.

It was a wonderful evening, and everyone was in good cheer. My personal highlight was chatting with a friend who was on the debating team with me, who went on to be our Dux and is now a doctor. A very impressive guy. He reminded me about the fun we had debating, and how much he enjoyed our time together. I

t was really nice to hear.

title: Gone
text:
Skype was one of the first technology companies I used which felt like magic. Calling people with clear audio and video, for just a few cents a minute, wherever they may be in the world. This was at a time where people were still going wild over 10 cent texts.

It’s now gone. And not because of competition, but because morons at Microsoft wanted to roll it into Teams. Luckily there are alternatives like Viber (which I hadn’t thought of for 14 years), but still, it seems like all good things come to an end eventually.

title: Credit
text:
We have some airline credit from a cancelled trip. It is a curse in disguise.

It’s enough that we can go to a lot of places, but not to a lot of places we WANT to go to (for example, back to Aus/NZ). It is also too much that if we were to NOT use it, we would be kinda pissed. To make it worse, we have to use it in the coming months, and with an airline we rarely use (albeit not North Korea Air or anything of that nature).

It feels like a nice problem to have, and we WILL get to a solution, but it isn’t super clear right now.

title: Date night
text:
Tonight, Ky and I claimed our date night dinner voucher (thanks Liv) and went to a cooking class. Honestly, it was a lot better than I had expected.

The theme was Argentinian, which apparently includes a giant wheel of fried cheese, but this time with chimmichurri! Ay caramba!

That said, we had a blast. The two couples we were foisted with were great craic, and one of them even invited us round for board games night.

It might be the gift that keeps on giving.

title: Traffic
text:
This evening, the whole family went off to absolute whoop whoop to get some snaps. It was almost a three hour expedition.

Leaving at 5:30 on a Friday night was probably not the smartest idea. What should have taken us 20 minutes took us closer to 50, and man, it was terrible.

I have been fortunate. For most of my life, dealing with traffic hasn’t been a regular part of my life. There was a bit when I went to primary school, but for middle school I could take the tram, and for senior school I walked to class. Both took under 30 minutes.

Then for uni, it was either walk, ride my bike, or take a tram. There was one year where the trip was 45 minutes plus (although on public transport) and it was probably the worst year of my life.

And for work, it’s been somewhat similar. Kiwibank was 20 minutes on a scooter (my favourite commute). I lived on-site while mining in Western Australia and selling industrial screwdrivers in Germany. My desk is 2 meters from my bed.

As I sat in 40 minutes of traffic (there AND back) I realised how #blessed I have been, and how avoiding this is in the future is a Tier 1 priority. Either that, or invest in a Self-Driving Car.

title: Preseason
text:
In a couple of weeks, the NFL kicks off. I don’t mind watching the last quarter on a Sunday, huddled up while the snow falls. Honestly, I quite like it.

But at ~$300 a ticket, I can’t see myself going to a game anytime soon.

The exception is preseason, where tickets are 90% off. Graham, Kim and I went this evening to see Denver crush Arizona, but honestly, I’m just there for the fireworks, promotions, music, lights, and the horse that runs on the field after every major event.

A great night.

Prematch – Preseason

title: Up and Away
text:
It has been almost 5 months, but today, we finally, FINALLY, unpacked our last packing box. It was, unsurprisingly, our art.

Now, art is probably too strong a word. Most of it is really just framed photos of our wedding, family, and holidays, but there are a few pieces that deserve to see the light of day.

My personal favourite is a David Shrigley piece, which I got for Ky a little while ago.

It is simple, fun, and clean. It doesn’t try to be anything it isn’t, but every time I walk past it, I am glad it is there. Not a bad pick!

I don’t know if there are any more pieces that we will add. I am a fan of indigineous Australian art, southern African minimalist art, and think it might be nice to have a Norman Rockwell print at some point, the classic American vernacular painter.

It’s a low priority for now, but we will get there.

title: Outage
text:
Our internet decided to shit the bed today, around 1:30 pm. It’s been very annoying.

There is a technician coming round tomorrow, but for now, we’re going to just muddle on through.

title: We’re back!
text:
People love to complain about their telecom provider. Who wouldn’t? After all, you don’t think about them 99% of the time, and then 1% of the time, either due to poor connection, an unexpected bill, or an outage, it is all consuming.

As readers of this series will know, yesterday our internet went out. Today, our technician came out, free of charge, and fixed it within an hour. It turns out there was an issue connecting the cable to the street, so we never would have been able to solve it ourselves.

So perhaps, these days, my relationship with telecoms is 98% ignore, 1% hate, and 1% gratitude. Quite the improvement!

title: Sad goodbyes
text:
After two wonderful months, Occi’s grandparents, Kim and Graham, have flown back to New Zealand. They have been a godsend.

It certainly wasn’t a holiday for them. With Ky back at work, they were on full time Occi duty. This meant trips to the library, swimming lessons, music lessons, and most of all, time spent playing in the living room. All that 2-on-1 attention has paid dividends; when they first came, Occi was barely army crawling; now she is racing around the house on all fours, standing almost unassisted, and maybe (maybe…) saying her first words (we don’t know if the dadas are a word, or just noise. I’m unconvinced … so far).

It hasn’t just been looking after Ock-Star though; they have helped us out a ton too. From cooking, to cleaning (they’re VERY neat), to doing the laundry, it has made the last few months a dream. Having a kid isn’t always HARD work, but there is a lot of it, so having a second pair of hands allows us to go to the gym, or have a nap, or see a friend, or just do a thousand other things. It allowed me to go to SF for a few days for work, and a week to Aus to see family. I know Ky feels the same.

They’re very sad to say goodbye, and so are we.

title: Rough start
text:
Ky and I are about to begin our summer vacation. It’s something we have been quite looking forward to, but are slightly daunted by the long flight ahead. 9 hours is a long journey, especially with an infant.

It turns out that the flight was the least of our worries, as 20 minutes into our drive to the airport, Occi decided to unleash a massive vomcano into her lap! All those raviolis, lightly digested, were dripping down her front in a languid manner.

We’re about to take off now, so hopefully the worst is behind us.

title: Fair enough
text:
One of my favourite Paul Graham (founder of YC) quotes is “in February, you wonder why ANYONE would live in London. In June, you wonder why anyone would live anywhere else”.

While it’s not June, it is a beautiful time to be in the English capital for a long weekend.

title: A sequence of events
text:
Today will be a series of vignettes throughout the day, as my friend Joseph and I go to see Crystal Palace take on Nottingham Forest at Selhurst Park.

11:51: boarded the ThamesLink train at Kings Cross (Aka St Pancreas in our household) en route to East Croydon. The weather is beautiful, there are a few fans about, and the mood across the city is lifted.

12:15: Arrive at East Croydon. People say it’s a bit of a dive, but it’s fine. Off to the Builder’s Arms for a roast and prematch pint. Duolingo is done for the day

2:37: Crystal palace score. 1-0

3:05: Second half begins. Mood is bright

3:15: Nottingham Forest equalise. 1-1. Crowd, bar the away end, are silent.

3:23: Nottingham Forest player injured. Crowd finds their voice again. Next goal looks like it’ll be palace, but it’s close

4:52: Arrive back at King’s Cross. Train is both comfy and airconditioned. A first!

title: Getting out of here alive
text:
We flew into Florence today, ready to spend a week or so in Italy before our friend’s wedding. Tuscany is a beautiful part of the world.

The hardest part of the journey hasn’t been from our accom to the airport (seamless, thanks to the public holiday induced traffic absence), boarding our plane (London City truly is the best airport in the UK), or our flight across (seeing the Alps from the air is well worth it).

The hard part was getting out of Florence! It took us about 40 minutes to just get out of the city, which is impressive, as our destination was only 50 minutes away. A lot of missed turnoffs, Italian traffic, and a lack of aggressive honking on my behalf was entirely to blame.

It’s all part of the adventure.

title: Foreign Technology
text:
I was reading an essay by Noah Smith, an American blogger and economist, about a long held memory he had of watching a documentary set on a remote island. The locals were explaining to the host what they got up to on the island; fishing, building shelter, hunting with spears, tending the fire. It was a pretty basic way of life.

At no point did it occur to the chief strange that this entire conversation didn’t only include himself, a translator, and the host, but also a fourth person, the camera man. Surely, one would think, that someone who grew up in a world without electricity would be fascinated by a man carrying a very large, very electrical, very unusual camera on their shoulder.

You would think that, but you’re wrong. The chief had simply no interest in adopting a foreign technology.

Sadly, that trend continues to this day.

Italy, which is very hot at this time of year, as it has been every summer since the last ice age. This isn’t surprising.

But the absence of aircon remains a tradition. Whether the average Italian is incurious about it, fears it, or fails to recognise the value, remains a mystery to me. But as we all sit here running fans and feeling slightly sticky, it would be well worth considering.

Other than that, today has been another great day!

title: Pizza Party
text:
They have a poor reputation, largely due to memories of out of touch executives trying to motivate the troops with a few slices of lukewarm dough.

However, for a chance to get together the day before a wedding, it’s a great and easy mixer.

Especially in Italy.

title: Pisa Party
text:
Last night I wrote about our Pizza Party (although I fear the dodgy wifi prevented it from posting). If you missed it, you can read all about it HERE.

Today is the day of Charles’ wedding, a very old school friend of mine. He was one of just three people I went to school more or less all the way through with; primary, middle (he left a year earlier) and then high school. I’m fortunate that we still maintain a bond long after.

I’ll give you the full run down tomorrow, but for now, I thought I would share a classic snap from our experience yesterday in Pisa. Honestly, I wasn’t the biggest fan of the city, it felt a bit low rent, but at least we got this photo.

title: Finally!
text:
I have always been a little underwhelmed by Italian food. When I was 19, I spent a week here, and all I wanted was a big tasty pasta. I never got it.

On this trip, there has been a lot of very good food, including risotto, pizza, and Jim’s sensational chicken cacciatore. But I still hadn’t had my big tasty pasta.

That changed this evening. It was a gorgeous evening, so we went into town and sat at a tiny, homely, beautiful restaurant. It was quiet, so we let Occi play at our feet while we dined on Salami, Tiramisu, and finally, at last, a big tasty bowl of pasta.

It was everything a good neighbourhood restaurant should be: tasty, good value, comfortable, friendly.

It was one of the highlights of the trip, and we will be back, at least one day.

https://maps.app.goo.gl/Uj5vEhkxoN4gAehT7?g_st=ipc

title: Jet lag
text: It’s never fun, but it doesn’t last forever. Occi decided that 3am was time to party, which was fine, but here’s hoping we make it to past 6 tomorrow morning.

title: Vested
text:
Something cool happened yesterday, some of my shares at Innovera vested. It’s worth $0 at the moment, and possibly might stay that way for a while, but most people’s work anniversaries come and go without much fanfare.

Not this time.

title: Ciao 🇮🇹
text:
Note: this post should have gone out last week, but it got stuck in drafts.

We are on the plane home. Occi is asleep, so is Ky. It’s been a busy holiday.

I’m glad we came. London, despite its many faults, is a second home to me. Every time I touch down, it feels comfortable. Familiar. And exciting. Unlike a friend, I don’t get teary eyed when I touchdown at Heathrow, but I always enjoy my time in the capital.

Having a weekend of near perfect weather didn’t hurt.

Italy was different. I rarely get to see my sister, so having 4 days with Victoria and her fiance Jim was very special. In just under three months, they’re going to get married in Australia, and I can’t wait for that. It will be a very special day.

Talking about weddings, seeing Charles and Elisa wed was one of the highlights of my year. I have known Charles for many, many years, and it was great to see him put together an unforgettable few days. Like many people, I’m a big fan of cross-cultural weddings, they are always exciting and fresh.

Overall, it was a perfect mid year break.

title: Up in flames!
text:
I had a small kitchen fire today.

While searing some prawns, the oil in the pan caught alight and before you know it, I had a mini flamethrower in my hand. Crazy!

Fortunately, I knew what to do, and most importantly, not WHAT to do. For those unsure, whatever you do, don’t use water to out out the flame, you’re going to burn your house down. Just take it off the heat, and if you don’t have much oil, it will quickly burn itself out. If you have a lot, just blow on it, or cover it with a fire blanket.

I still haven’t figured out a way to avoid the fire alarms going off!

title: Gout gout
text:
99% of people only pay attention to track and field during the Olympics, and I’m one of them. Today, however, I make an exception.

I sat next to a rather odd person at dinner a little while ago, and he told me about this running phenomenon out of Australia called Gout Gout, who may very well be the next Usain Bolt.

Apart from the memorable name, this kid is rapid. At the schoolboy championships, with a slight tailwind, he ran 10.04 seconds. At 16.

As someone who enjoyed track at high school, the magic number was sub 12. If you were sub 13, you were certainly considered fast. So to be 20-30% faster than that is really, really, really impressive.

Something to look forward to in LA 2028

title: 1st Birthday
text:
Our good friends’ daughter just had her first birthday. It was fun.

I have been to first birthdays before, but as a non-parent, they’re often very loud and not much fun. Usually they don’t have a crate of beers you can delete either.

As a parent, today was a blast. Fun in the park, with our daughters playing together, while late summer wraps Denver in a blanket.

A special day.

title: New Book
text:
I recently went past the the library, and picked up two books. One was on Deep Learning (the machine version), the other was on cooking. I’ll focus on the second.

I never really watched any of Anthony Bourdain’s many shows, but I did read his best seller, Kitchen Confidential. Now, I have his second best book; his recipes from Les Halles. Les Halles was easily Bourdain’s most famous project; a classic French brassiere based in the heart of NYC. It was packed night after night for decades.

It’s my kind of food, though I’ve never been and it has long since shut down (I think?). Simple French cooking, lots of flavour, unpretentious. All the reviews about the venue was that it had a buzz, was always filled with groups, couples, friends and families having a good time, and the food was very good. Not great. But very good.

Bourdain is very honest about this. He never was awarded any Michelin Stars. He never worked in any of the top restaurants in America or Europe, and I don’t even think he spent that long working in Paris. But as someone with a full time day job and a young family, I’m not making any Michelin starred dishes anytime soon.

It’s part cookbook, part guide. It doesn’t just state the ingredients and steps, it gives advice, tips, primers, and history. It’s written conversationally, with a sense of humor. When was the last time you read an instruction manual like that!

I don’t know what I’ll cook first, but there look like some crackers. I’ll keep you posted.

We often hear about the concept of going from 0 to 1, which comes from Peter Thiel’s excellent book of the same name. It’s all about creating something new.

However much I enjoy that kind of work, what feel more common, and probably more useful, is going from 1 to 1.1, or having a marginal improvement. It’s what I am focussing on right now as I focus on enriching datasets, and while building semantic searches around it is sexy, building a slightly more robust set is where the magic happens.

title: No Spend September
text:
Ky and I are trying to have a No Spend September, which is really just a hard curtail on as much discretionary spending as possible.

This is things like Amazon, coffees or meals out, events. It’s certainly not dumpster diving.

It hasn’t been too hard so far, but conversely, we haven’t seen the benefits of it either. It feels like dieting, you need to stick with it until you a) get used to it and b) see the results compound.

And like dieting, I feel that smarter choices always beats strict rules. Starving yourself is always going to be a short term solution.

title: Assassinated
text:
I was reading X while waiting for some data to upload this afternoon, and found out that a mere 3 minutes prior, and ~500 miles away, Charlie Kirk had been assassinated at a rally.

It’s a terrible thing to happen.

I don’t share Mr Kirk’s politics, or at least not most of them. But there is no denying that he made an impact, built something, stood up for what he believed in, and was committed to the cause. More importantly, he was a husband and father.

Many lives have been ruined today. His. His family. The person who did it, who is at large, and if caught, might be executed. Their family. The list goes on.

Charlie Kirk was 31 years old.

title: Boys night
text:
Ky and Occi are off to Vancouver today, enjoying sometime with a few good friends of ours. Due to work, I’m flying out tomorrow afternoon. It should be fun.

With an empty house, I invited three other dads around for some steak and potatoes. We talked about everything and nothing; from supersonic flight, to how fatherhood has changed us.

I really enjoy having guy friends, which can be hard and takes work. I’m glad this evening worked out.

title: En route
text:
Due to a screw up with flights, we ended up with a fast expiring credit with United. Not wanting to waste it, we decided to go visit Vancouver for the weekend.

Ky has already headed off with Swock, and I’m writing this from the tarmac, about to take off. I’m looking forward to it, and am committed to giving Van an honest appraisal. The last time I was hear I thought the place was a complete dump; hopefully this time I’m more impressed. The fact I’m not going in the depths of winter should help.

title: Vancouver
text:
Honestly, not too bad, especially in the summer.

We had a lovely day, going to a children’s farm, including peacocks, enjoying some paddling pool fun, and finishing off with a trip to a cosy wine bar.

I can see the appeal. The closeness to nature, both beach and snow, is a MASSIVE WIN TODAY. The summers are lovely, albeit a little temperamental, but nothing that London forces you to deal with. Being relatively far north during summertime means that mean you are bathed in sunlight in the evenings; obviously, one must pay for this privilege in six months time.

Honestly, thus far we have had a great time. Vancouver has come up a notch in my book.

title: Chicken Dinner
text:
I love making roast chicken. It is, by far, the hardest dish I know how to make.

The good news is that tonight, my chicken was a 9/10. As the hero of the dish, that’s a great sign. But the potatoes were a 7/10, and the vegetables and sauce utterly forgettable.

I’ve knocked various parts of the dish out of the park in the past, but never all elements together.

The quest continues.

title: Vancouver, a review
text:
I’m flying back from our three days in Vancouver, happy and full. It was a great trip.

Last time I was out here I thought the place was a complete dump, and I’m glad to say my perspective has improved. Visiting in the depths of winter, for many people, doesn’t give you the full picture.

The pros: it is in a beautiful area. The combination of the mountains, forest, and ocean integrated together is quite special, and certainly rare. By North American standards, architecturally it’s quite nice; more homogenous and static than Chicago (my favourite) but far nicer than the monotonous (insert pretty much any city here). However, it’s certainly not Budapest, Paris, or even Berlin (which I think is an underrated architectural gem).

So what? I’m not a highly observant person, so these things aren’t deal breakers.

The weather: there is a lot of Britain in British Columbia. Some of that is positive, it is very green, but it comes at a cost of highly variable weather. On any given day we had glorious sunshine, chilly winds, rain, and low hanging fog, sometimes all before midday. It’s a place where dressing in layers seems sensible. Coming from Denver where it have been 28/80+ degrees and sunny for the past four months, it was a rude reminder that not everyone is fortunate enough to ignore the weather report.

The food: we didn’t visit many places on our trip, but we did have sushi for lunch. I always doubt the seafood inland, so I really hoed into that. Plus the chicken we got from the butcher was elite. Vancouver has a well earned reputation for its dining scene, whereas Denver is the culinary equivalent to a regional airport.

Cons

The urban blight in Vancouver is dreadful. Hastings street is the epicentre, and it is something that needs to be seen to be believed. Thousands of people, zombified, shooting heroin and smoking meth. There is no hope, no light. Only darkness. A two kilometre strip of decay and despair, littered with used needles and cracked meth pipes. People passed out in the gutter; whether they’re alive or not isn’t clear. It’s dire, and a very, very hard problem to solve.

The city is expensive as well. Not necessarily for us; when you’re on USD everything is 30% off. But for those who are living there, things looked really tough. The butter was $10, a whole chicken was $40, petrol is $1.80 a litre etc. This is in a city where the median salary is under $70k. It looked like it would be really rough, and reminded me of the fortune we have to live in the world’s biggest economy and paid in the world’s reserve currency.

Conclusion: our friends live a great life. They have a beautiful home overlooking the city, and on a summer day where the sun is shining, you see the appeal. Whistler isn’t my favourite mountain, but it’s world famous for a reason, and is on your doorstep. Winter is wet, but it isn’t brutally cold like living in Toronto or Edmonton. There’s clearly an appeal.

Visiting was great, and I am looking forward to coming back. But I’d never live there, and I don’t envy those who do.

title: 3rd gear
text:
Sometimes, I’m not super productive. It clusters; the days I don’t do as much I hoped to in the office are the same days I don’t achieve my fitness, social, and/or extra-curricular goals.

It feels like I can’t get out of 3rd gear.

Tomorrow will be better.

title: Eating the frog
text:
Yesterday I mentioned how I was struggling to get out of 3rd gear. Today you’ll be glad to hear things went much better. Part of this was doing the hard things that you have been putting off. In my instance, it was getting back to the daily habit of weighing myself first thing.

From there, thing a snowballed, and it ended up being quite productive.

title: Chills, they’re multiplying
text:
Summer is coming to an end, slowly. The days are still glorious, but the mornings are starting to get a little chilly. It was 8 when we took our morning walk together, which isn’t cold, but it is cool.

After a few months of consistent 30+ days, it’s a welcome change.

title: Sleep pressure
text:
Our daughter doesn’t seem to need much sleep these days. While that is a blessing for her in the future (after all, minimizing sleep requirements maximizes life opportunity), for now, it’s a challenge. In the space of a week, she has gone from exhausted, with eyes barely open, at 7:45 to party girl at 9:30.

At this point, you need to call in the experts. We’re fortunate that we have a wonderful sleep consultant who gave us a few strategies, and we think in the coming weeks we’ll be a lot better prepared to face what may.

title: Preparing
text:
I am spending four days doing the single parenting thing this week while Ky is in Nashville. Fingers crossed.

This afternoon/evening was dedicated to prepping. Meals are planned for. Schedules set. Snacks baked and ready to deploy.

It will make a huge difference.

title: Day 1 down
text:
It was a tough, but magical day today. With Ky out, we both O and I missed her, but we still managed to have a lot of fun.

We called a friend, and congratulated him on his recent engagement. We went for a run, followed by a beer at a brewery (that was less of a “we” activity). We went to a first birthday party together. We had a slice of pizza. And we had lots and lots of fun.

Can’t wait to do it all again tomorrow.

title: A business idea, for anyone who wants it
text:
You can buy most things in America. In fact, visiting an American supermarket, and ESPECIALLY a Costco, makes one feel like Gorbachev when he first visited the US 40 years ago. There sure is a lot of stuff to buy.

Except for one thing. Hooded towels, extremely popular in Australian surfing culture, are completely foreign here. On multiple occasions, including today, many people have commented that they have never seen one before. I’ll include a photo below x

Maybe this is my side-hustle, but instead of Ripcurl, I slap a flag on it, or a political slogan, or an unlicensed NFL logo.

This really is the land of opportunity.

title: Rainy day
text:
It was POURING today, something that doesn’t happen very often. It caught me completely off guard

When you’re not used to it, and perhaps even when you are, it slows everything down. Traffic moves slower. Picking the right clothes takes longer. Things need to be dried or covered.

Autumn has arrived.

title: Gifted?
text:
I saw a post on X recently about how 99% of people who say that the equivalent of “I was a gifted kid but now I’m burnt out” is “I could have gone pro had it not been for The InjuryTM.”

It had me thinking. Perhaps I didn’t grow up thinking I was a genius, or perhaps things were just different 20 years ago in Australia, but I never really thought this way. However, I do notice in terms the discourse that there are MANY people who believe they’re super bright (but probably only top 30%) and are devastated that they haven’t won a Field’s Medal or something.

Overpromise, underdeliver is a sure fire for disappointment, and can be brutal when self-applied.

title: Obsidian League
text:
I have been really getting back into my Duolingo, and am now in the second highest league, the Obsidian league. For those who aren’t language-MAXXXING, DuoLingo gamifies the experience by allowing you to earn XP points, and each week you compete against others for who can earn the most. Finish in the top 5? Get promoted to the next league. Finish in the bottom 10? Down you go.

It’s a good motivation, and once you get to a certain level, everyone is fairly committed.

However, this week has me shook. After 4 days, the leading person, Karl P, has over 6000 XP. I am just over 1600, and 16th out of 20. For context, you could have WON the previous league with a score over 2k, and we’re only halfway through the week.

title: Granny
text:
Tonight was the AFL grand final, and spoiler alert, well done Brisbane.

I’m writing this at 2:12 in the morning, after what was a fun albeit lopsided game. Despite both teams entering into half time at 34 all, the first time two teams have been even Stevens since 1909, the Lions stormed home to win by a lot.

It was great to be able to hang out with a friend and see a few friendly faces at the pub. Plus it looked a glorious day in Melbourne, so that’s always a plus.

Until next time!

title: Crowd pleaser
text:
We went to ANOTHER 1st birthday party this afternoon. It’s not that I’m complaining, I actually quite enjoy them, and today’s edition was top notch.

Feeding a crowd is difficult, so picking the right dishes is crucial. These guys nailed it by going with the Texas style pulled pork.

Pork in the US is absurdly cheap, and I don’t know what they’re feeding these pigs, but they’re huge. Throw them in the smoker or over for six hours, let all that collagen melt down into tender strands, and then serve with dinner rolls, sauce, mac and cheese, and salad (optional).

Everyone goes home both happy and most importantly, not hungry.

title: Marginal improvement
text:
I have been slowly getting back into running recently, and today was the first time I felt a proper improvement. It was faster, less painful, and I feel recovered already.

Progress is progress.

title: MASSIVE WIN TODAY: The Sequel
text:
Many years ago, in a moment of distraction, I sent out a team-wide email that hadn’t been properly checked. For those that were there, it went down in Kiwibank folklore. For those who weren’t, a quick summary can be found HERE.

Well, today I want to add a second one to the list. It was late last night as I was desperately trying to finish up a report for one of our clients. After finishing the gig, I clicked send, without taking a second to double check. Fortunately, it was only an internal email, otherwise I may have been in deep ship!

title: Surprised!
text:
We had friends round tonight for a surprise party. They recently got engaged, and Ky and I are thrilled for them.

Sadly, we couldn’t make it to their Boston engagement party, but now that they’re back in the centennial state, we wanted to do something to celebrate.

It was the perfect example of casual dinner party, elevated. We had Aperol Spritzs, we had quite literally the best ragu I have ever made (the secret is pork belly) and we had a blast.

We’re lucky to have friends like them in our lives, it makes living in a foreign land feel like home.

title: Zimmer
text:
Ky and I are off on date night. After a recommendation from a friend, and a bit of luck, we are off to see Hans Zimmer at Ball Arena.

I’m looking forward to it!

title: Park time
text:
I was going to write about our date night last night w Herr Zimmer, but it’s late and I want to do it justice.

Instead, I thought the highlight of today was going to the park with Occi, and seeing her enjoy the playset. She is getting so, so much more confident and capable.

It’s great to see.

title: Zimmer
text:
Two nights ago we went to The World of Hans Zimmer, which was a real treat, and very affordable!

Hans Zimmer is like Michael Schumacher, Tiger Woods, or Pablo Picasso; people so famous within their domain that even if you couldn’t name a single other practitioner, you could name them.

The cool thing about Hans is how prolific he is. He’s not young by any definition, but he’s not ancient either. Even so, he has made so much music for so many movies.

Inception. Gladiator. Skyfall. Driving Ms Daisy. The Lion King.

A few things stuck out for me.

 1. He works really hard looking for authenticity. While writing the score for Sherlock, he spent months in Slovakia listening to Gypsy music, which he thought would work well with his Victorian England theme. For the Lion King, he spent time in South Africa to immerse himself in African music. He wanted his songs to sound authentic, and they are.
 2. Throughout the performance, he told little vignettes of his career. My favourite was about the Lion King. Hans was very excited to do this, not knowing that the movie would be one of the greatest ever made. At the time, he had a six year old daughter, who really wanted to go to a premier with him. Movies like Gladiator weren’t going to work, but an animation about a talking lion? Well, she could definitely come to that! But what came next was far more bittersweet. Hans had lost his dad when he was six years old, and in his words, writing the Lion King was “a requiem for my father”. It was beautiful.
 3. Would I go again? Yes, with a caveat. Ball Arena is a great venue for ice hockey, basketball, and Usher; I know because I have seen all three performed there. But these larger venues aren’t purpose built for orchestral music, and I think if I were to go again, I would to do it in a proper theatre.

Other than that, a wonderful evening!

title: 1st birthday
text:
It’s Occi’s first birthday party (we still have a week or so until she turns 1), and it should be a great day.

Our theme is Paris, and we have the ham and cheese croissants, macrons, berets, baguettes, and flags all lined up at the park. Our guests arrive in a little under an hour, and then it’s all go!

The calm before the storm

title: First Steps
text:
Today was the day!

After weeks of standing, crab walking along walls, and wild bear crawls, our darling daughter took her first steps.

They’re small, rare, and uncertain. But it’s the start of something wonderful.

I wonder where her steps will take her.

title: Blind
text:
I lost my contact lenses today. Dropped it on the floor and couldn’t find it for the life of me.

They were my last pair. Luckily I have glasses, but it’s been the first time in probably 20 years where I have been wearing glasses consistently.

Plus draconian laws mean I can’t just order more online without a prescription.

Bummer.

title: DenHac
text:
I recently joined DenHac, the US’ third oldest “HackerSpace”.

Sometimes called MakerSpaces, these are shared workshops were people can do woodwork, auto repair, fix electronics, code, solder, weld, and more, all in the company of like-minded creators.

I thought it was super cool. One of my goals in the next little while is to get back into basic woodworking, but buying all the tools is expensive, and I have nowhere to store them. For $45 a month, I have access to every tool I could possibly imagine, a workshop open 24/7 (that is only 7 minutes down the road) and a community of people that can teach me along the way.

I’m looking forward to it!

title: Date night
text:
A really wonderful evening was had. Simple, elegegant. Fun.

We simply went to ramen, had a tasty bowl, and shared a stunning fish collar cooked in a Korean sauce.

The highlight was being able to take the time to connect with one another, during a busy week, within a busy time of our lives.

title: Enough
text:
The past two days have been “Prime Day” aka Amazon’s biggest sale of the year. 24.1 billion (with a b) dollars were spent in the past 48 hours as people rushed to get their hands on whatever.

I was temped to look, but not enough to pull the trigger. After No Spend Septemb, I don’t feel the need.

title: Pre-approval
text:
I asked my boss today if it was ok if I could renew my visa, thus adding two more years to the clock.

He said yes.

Feels good.

title: Long week
text:
We have a big client on the books, and it’s a little make or break for us to do a good job for them.

With such a tight deadline, my colleague and I worked all of today, Saturday. What I thought we could knock off in a few hours took us about 9 hours, but the results are vert, VERY good. I’m stoked.

It’s nice that this overtime has been recognised as well, and not in the usual way. I can always get more money, but I can never get time back. So having Monday and Tuesday off is a great reward.

title: Steamboat
text:
It’s Occi’s birthday weekend, and we have decided to go to a super cute mountain town called Steamboat Springs. It is gorgeous.

The three of us have had the best time, and it has absolutely been worth the 4 hour drive and the inevitable turbo turd on the highway.

Cowgirl country

title: Hot Springs
text:
This afternoon, the family went down to the local hot springs for a little dip.

I am not the first person to say “let’s go to the Hot Springs!”, but every time I come back, I feel relaxed and rejuvenated, albeit a little dehydrated.

Little swock enjoyed it as well, although we couldn’t put her in the hottest pool in case she accidentally sous-vided herself.

Another top day.

title: 1st Birthday
text:
It was Octavia’s first birthday. Where has the time gone!

I’m tired and ready for bed, but all I can say is that it was a magical day. More tomorrow!

title: Little O turns 1
text:
If you’re not a grandparent, you probably find this stuff rather dull, but I wanted to capture a few thoughts on how the year has gone so far.

First off, it DOES go fast. When I see photos of her from the early days, it’s hard to believe they are of the same person! Back then, with all due respect, she was essentially a fragile potato. She slept. She slurped. And she pooped. As I remember telling my mate Lachy, it’s like looking after a real life tamagotchi (I stand by that!).

Now she is walking, talking (well, saying dada when she points to me, but also dogs and trees, so who knows) and developing her own little personality. It’s cool to see. Plus she’s huge!

Ky asked me my favourite thing about Octavia, and my answer was easy; I love that she’s healthy. A good friend of mine has had the hardest time with miscarriages and a child passing due to prematurity; a girl I went to school with lost her child due to SIDS. Even spending a few days in the hospital near the NICU made you realise that there by the grace of god go I.
