The best thing about Crested Butte is also the worst thing; it’s 4.5 hours from Denver. We drove up last night, had a vom-cano in the back with Ock, and got in just before 11. That isn’t much fun.

But getting up this morning and seeing this view more than makes up for it.

The Butte

title: Turns
text:
Ky and I spent the day, just the two of us, skiing at Crested Butte. Our good friends took care of O for the day; we will return the favour tomorrow.

It has been almost two years exactly since we were here, our last visit was for Ky’s Birthday. There was a lot more snow that time, but on the flip side, Ky had caught Covid and was also pregnant (we didn’t know!) so that was a bit of a challenge

This time around the snow was a bit light, but the weather was brilliant, the lines were empty, and the time together was special.

A really great day.

title: Rough Start
text:
Today was the annual (?) Alley Oop competition in Crested Butte, which is a cross country skiing race through the town. We were on baby sitting duty, so we went to check it out.

For those who don’t know, Cross Country Skiing, also know as Nordic Skiing, is done on flat-ish terrain, with a free heel and skis that can go up and down. A photo probably makes it easier to understand.

While watching, it reminded me of my first (and last!) Nordic ski race I competed in, 25 or so years ago.

In 2003, my family decamped to the Australian High Country for six months to live. We attended the local school, which had 26 students. Not in my year level; total.

Due to the small size and our proximity to the local ski fields, the Australian government felt it was necessary to a) provide all students with a heavily subsidized lift ticket and b) allow us to take every Friday off in 3rd term to go skiing. Honestly, it was the most educational experience we had while up there.

One week, it was the Interschools Competition, which was the annual state ski carnival. My sister ended up qualifying for nationals in the Moguls, the same event which caused me to wipe out and suffer a minor concussion. However, the most memorable event was the Nordic Skiing.

For some bizarre reason, whether you’re at the Olympics or the under-12s, everyone starts together. Imagine 50 odd kids, many of whom have done this activity once or twice, all crammed together waiting for the gun. Once it went off, it was pure chaos.

Like this, but much worse

I’m fairly sure 90% of us all fell over within the first 10 seconds. The smartest and luckiest were at the back, skated around, and probably went on to win it. I wouldn’t know, I think it took me 5 minutes to get my skis back on!

It’s a fond memory, and one I haven’t thought of for many, many years. I’m so glad my parents took me out to these things, even when we had no idea what was going on. Life really is all about getting involved.

title: Road trip
text:
It was a long drive back from Crested Butte, including our break, about 5 hours from door to door on windy roads.

Is it nice to have a short commute? Of course. Is it nice to spend 5 hours to chat about everything and nothing?

Absolutely.

title: Exponential
text:
I am part of a fantasy football league with one other guy, an old colleague from Kiwibank. After a good start, I have largely forgotten about it.

This all changed in Round 20, when I was automatically added to the FPL cup, a knockout competition. Each week you would be assigned a random opponent; score more and you progress to the next round, score less and you’re knocked out until there was a final winner.

Initially, I thought that there was no way this tournament could be successfully completed in half a season, after all, more than a quarter of a million people had entered, and there were only 18 rounds left in the season. It felt like way too many people in way too few rounds.

Turns out that the very human malady of underestimating exponentials had claimed another victim. Here is what it looks like over 18 rounds:

Round | Gameweek | Teams Remaining
——|———-|—————-
1 | MW20 | 262,144
2 | MW21 | 131,072
3 | MW22 | 65,536
4 | MW23 | 32,768
5 | MW24 | 16,384
6 | MW25 | 8,192
7 | MW26 | 4,096
8 | MW27 | 2,048
9 | MW28 | 1,024
10 | MW29 | 512
11 | MW30 | 256
12 | MW31 | 128
13 | MW32 | 64
14 | MW33 | 32
15 | MW34 | 16
16 | MW35 | 8
17 | MW36 | 4
18 | MW37 | 2 → 1 Champion

It’s wild to think that such a big number can be whittled down so quickly, but within 2 rounds, 75% of teams are already out.

Sadly, I was among that 75%, but it was fun while it lasted!

title: Prism
text:
Today I trialed out OpenAI’s new scientific writing tool, Prism. I wasn’t super impressed with where it is NOW, but I love where it could potentially go.

Writing academic and scientific papers is hard, and always will be. But one of the things that is unnecessarily difficult is incorporating LaTeX, which is essentially the way mathematical formulas are presented in text.

This is, or should I say, was, a real bugbear. Unless your equation is something like 1+1=2, then things get difficult and difficult to read. If I gave you the equation |sqrt((4*5)/(2^3)|^(1/(3-x)) you would have to read it like 10 times to finally get it. Whereas if you saw it expressed as

Which expressed graphically, looks like:

It feels trivial to be able to show these, BUT, they ‘re really difficult to present cleanly. When I was doing my Honours Thesis, I spent SO much time trying to figure this out and force it into Word without breaking everything. I managed it, eventually, but it was a poor use of time.

With Prism, I hope this is taken away, and more scientists, academics, and hacks (like me!) can spend their time on discovery, and less on formatting.

You can access Prism HERE.

title: Pub night
text:
Last week I started a group chat with a bunch of the dads. Ky is in a VERY active mum’s, I mean mom’s, group, so I wanted to do the same.

It’s pretty simple. Turn up to the Jefferson Park Pub (4.8 on Google) anytime after 8 on a Wednesday.

It was just me and another guy Jack, but we had a ripper two hours, and will be back again for round 2 next week.

For my Denver readers, Dad or not, feel free to join, it was a blast, and the more the merrier.

title: Keystone
text:
There hasn’t been much snow since last weekend, but that hasn’t stopped us coming up for another week.

We have come up with some friends, and think we are going to have a blast for the next few days.

To make it better, the drive was well under 2 hours, so we can spend our evening relaxed and at the lodge, not winding through mountain passes.

A short trip can often lure me into a disorganized trip; and that was the case this evening. There is always SO much to pack, and we left it to the last moment.

Fingers crossed we haven’t forgotten anything important!

title: Recreational Activity
text:
Today was a great day on the slopes.

Not because of the skiing, mind you. The slopes are in desperate need of some more snow, and there were definitely some sketchy moments navigating some icy stretches. When it wasn’t icy, the high of 8 degrees Celsius was softening things up. It was by no means the best day Ky and I had ever had skiing.

Regardless, we had a blast. It was very adult skiing, fully aware that we are partaking in a recreational activity. It was a day full of leisurely turns down groomed blues, of a 11am beer break, of avoiding all moguls and steeps. It was a day where we were out with some good friends, on a Friday, with the sun shining, and out on the slopes.

Good or bad snow, we still had a 10/10 day.

title: Planning the week
text:
With demands of our time ever increasing across the home and work front; My and I sat down this evening to plan out our week from go to whoa.

There is a bit of wiggle room, but the main thing is that we have a plan of who is doing what when. Calendars, itineraries, budgets, plans; all of these things can feel like handcuffs, but they’re actually liberating.

I’ll let you know how this first week goes, it will take a few weeks for us to really dial this one.

If you have a plan or schedule tip/trick/technique that works for you, please share it!

title: Under pressure
text:
There is a term in the baby world called “sleep pressure”, which is just a fancy term for tiredness. You can physically feel it when your eyelids get droopy, as well as a thousand other symptoms we are all familiar with.

Developing sleep pressure throughout the day is critical, because otherwise you end up like us right now, nearing 10pm, with a child who has no interest in going to bed.

And we didn’t even do anything wrong!

title: Buckle up
text:
February could be the biggest month yet in AI releases. That’s what the news is saying, but I’m not too sure.

Anthropic is out of the gate first with Opus 4.6, a slight improvement to 4.5. I say slight, but having trialled it, I can already detect an improvement. Easily my fave model at the moment.

OpenAI has responded with 5.3 Codex, their code editor. I don’t use it, so can’t really comment. Perhaps today I’ll have a play.

A surprise entry is Composer 1.5. This is Cursor’s own model, and already I love it. It’s focussed purely on code generation, and while not as “smart” as the flagship models like Opus or Gemini 3 Pro, it is about 15x faster. For a lot of use cases, that is exactly what you want, and it’s currently 50% off. I think I’m going to be using it a lot over these coming weeks.

There are rumours Google and XAi will feel the heat and release something as well to stay relevant; we will have to wait and see. Plus the technical world is going goo-goo over OpenClaw, a wrapper that allows AI agents to take over your computer, automating tasks such as email reply and messaging. I haven’t touched it, and won’t, but a lot of people seem excited by it.

The only team that is missing from this list is Meta, who after spending a fortune on talent have been very quiet. Will we see something new soon? So much money for no to zero ROI is going to start being reflected in the share price soon; if Zuck won’t act, his board might make him.

Interesting times.

title: Integrated
text:
We have all heard of a locking your keys in the car. With modern technology, it’s almost impossible to do now.

What IS possible is to lock your car in the garage, which is exactly what I did this evening. There isn’t a simple solution for that, but an eagle eyed toddler finding the spare is at least a good one!

Detective O hard at work!

title: It’s academic
text:
I just got home from a great dinner with one of my friends. Ok, the dinner itself wasn’t super memorable, but the company was.

I met Eric through UATX, aka the Incel Academy, which from the outside seems to be speedrunning its way to oblivion in spite of a $100m cash donation. He’s a professor at CU Boulder which is up the road, and we have been good friends since.

Eric is one of those guys who has a lot of friends and a real sense of adventure. Over the course of the evening he told me about the three times he has been apprehended by foreign militaries, his love of fly fishing and wine, and his recent paper exploring the intersection of space travel and blockchain markets.

Sadly he’s moving on to the University of Wyoming, so he’ll be a bit further away, but hopefully we will catch up again soon before he heads north. At the very least, we have a vague plan to go fly fishing in Montana in August; we shall see!

title: Hot lap
text:
The unseasonably warm weather continues, so Ky and I took the afternoon to walk around Sloan’s lake, one of our favourite parks. It was so nice to get back to running/jogging, and O loves being pushed in her stroller.

The only downside was that half of Denver had the same idea, so it was packed!

title: Voice Notes
text:
One of my goals this year has been trying to better connect with old friends, and have decided to go down the voice note route. With time zones and busy lives, it’s a lot easier.

I’m aiming for 5 a week, so hopefully you’ll get one soon. And don’t forget, you can always message (or voice note me!) at any time.

title: Starlink
text:
I don’t have a Starlink, but I like the company. The promise of reasonably fast internet, wherever you are in the world, for a few dollars a day, is a good one.

I would love it if governments embraced it more, rather than sticking with the boring old towers, so you can image my delight when I figured out the big brains down at Starlink HQ figured a way to turn it off for Russian forces while still allowing access to the Ukrainians. In day 1455 of the war (not a typo), this has led to their strongest counter-offence in over three years.

Like all right thinking people, I really hope the Ukrainians can emerge out of this morass victoriously, or at lease peacefully. You might not be Mr Musk’s biggest fan, but every little bit helps.

title: It begins
text:
After the worst snow season in living memory, the Colorado snow season is FINALLY getting into gear. It only took until mid February for things to get going.

There are reports that some mountains might be expecting 2ft of snow this week, which would be excellent, as we are once again up the mountain this weekend.

As I have mentioned before, we’re at the point where the joy of skiing is being out in nature with friends and family, home cooked meals, and glasses of red. It’s not getting waist deep in powder or hitting pristine turns our back.

BUT…

It is nice to do that every once in a while, and for all the people who make a crust out of the alpine industry, they must be more than relieved.

title: Hand. Foot. Mouth.
text:
Octavia has been monstrous the past few days. Refusing to eat, crying, screaming, and generally inconsolable.

We thought it might have been teething (it is!) but there must have been something else making it worse. Sadly, our suspicions turned out to be true.

Octavia has gone down with a case of Hand, Foot, and Mouth; which frankly I have never heard of, but is apparently quite common. There are a few symptoms, but the big one is painful blisters in the mouth and throat. No wonder she was so upset.

It means we are locked in for the weekend, and have sadly had to pull out of ski weekend with friends. It sucks, but right now I just hope she feels better soon.

There, is perhaps, a silver lining out of this, which everyone who has got sick knows. When you’re under the weather, you get to go on a mini-holiday. Not to Barbados, but rather a break from responsibilities. We’re a no screen time family, but this evening we cuddled up with a VERY fragile child and watched cat videos on TV, which is delightful. It means yoghurt and ice-cream for dessert. It means lots of hugs and time with soft toys.

Would we all rather be healthy and happy. Of course. But that doesn’t mean we can’t make the best of a rather crummy situation.

title: Back page. First.
text:
Ky and I have a subscription to the Economist. I usually read the print edition, and always start with the last page first.

For those not familiar with the newspaper, the final page of every edition is an obituary. Often it is someone famous; a prime minister, an author, a Nobel Laureate. But frequently it’s someone unknown, or least unknown to me.

This week, the obituary was on Virginia Oliver, a Maine woman who first went lobster fishing at 8 years old, then became a lobsterman (that’s what they’re called!) at the young age of 43. The only female lobsterman skipper for many decades, she continued running her operation and manning their ship, Virginia, until she turned 103. She passed at 105, after spending more than 60 years working the lobster trade.

Virginia certainly had a long, full, and interesting life. Living to over 100 is a marvel, and while genetics help, I have no doubt that continuing her work made an enormous difference. The fact that her four children (74, 76, 78 and 81) would come round for dinner every Saturday night wouldn’t have hurt either.

I try to read the paper every week, dipping in and out, but I always start with the obituary.

Vale Virginia.

title: Mile High Mags
text:
While O was having her nap this afternoon, I snuck off* to the Celtic; one of those bars which have every sport on, from the NBA finals all the way down to Bulgarian 3rd Division.

I chose the Celtic as they host the official Denver Newcastle United Supporters Group, and with the distinctive black and white strip, they were easy to spot.

There were four of them there, but by their eagerness to have a chat and buy me a Guinness, I got the impression they were still open to new members. One guy was from the Old Country, but the others were Americans who had somehow decided that a small, frequently relegated, until recently woefully managed football club in a dreary part of post-industrial Britain was going to be THEIR team.

Sadly they lost to a very good Manchester City side, but it was nice to get out and be part of a community, even if it is (for now) very small.

*Ky was at home when I left, it’s not like I abandoned our daughter!

title: Pantry
text:
We went to Ikea today to pick up a bookshelf. Little did our friends in Sweden know that this very reasonably priced piece of flatpacked furniture was not going to spend its life holding up books, but rather our pantry items.

For the past year we have been squirrelling cans, bags of rice, and sacks of potatoes in and out of tiny cupboard spaces. It meant we were always missing stuff, or buying ANOTHER tin of coconut milk when there were already 3 in the cupboard. The whole thing was just so tiresome.

Having a proper pantry is awesome. With doors installed, it looks the part, and being able to quickly view and select any item you need in an instant is frankly terrific.


I just wish we had done it earlier!

title: On rotation
text:
I love mixing things up in the kitchen. New recipes, ingredients, techniques etc. It’s always either something new, or trying to improve something I have done before.

Until recently. Every Monday, we have lamb koftas on the bbq. It’s not something I take too seriously, or try to improve. I know they’ll be good, easy to make, and quick to cook. I could probably think about way to make the flavour pop more or add more authentic spices, but for Monday, I think it’s fine as is.

It is very much in rotation.

title: Points Maxxxing
text:
I have never really been one for credit card maxing; largely because it’s not really a thing in Aus/NZ, and I only got my first credit card at the age of 29.

But now that we have been here a few years, and our credit score has finally built up, I thought, why not? There are a ton of cards out there that give you something for nothing, assuming you don’t overspend, and we don’t.

We haven’t been here long enough to get the real baller cards, but frankly, they seem more aligned with flexing than benefits. But a decent card with our local airline, United, should buy our family international flights each year for doing nothing beyond purchasing what we usually do.

Seems like a deal to me.

title: Soft Launch
text:
Like many people, I have often read a lot less of the Classics than I would have liked to. Some of that is due to lack of time, some of it is due to other priorities and distractions taking precedence.

But one reason is that a lot of the Western Traditions most famous works can be quite inaccessible. Some of that is due to language, and that is an issue. Even people ten years younger than me have noticeably different words and sayings, to the point where it can be completely indecipherable. Or, as Gen Z might put it:

“No cap, even the 10-years-younger NPCs got a whole diff firmware patch on their vocab. They be yappin in DLC slang and I’m just sittin there like “???” — full 404, no subtitles, OS not compatible. Deadass feels like I need Chat just to decode the timeline.”

But a much larger, less talked about issue is the lack of context. When the original author was writing, there was a huge amount of information that they, correctly, assumed everyone knew.

If I read a story set in modern day Iowa, a place I have never been, I know that its likely rural, conservative, with a big agricultural industry, pretty religious but not SUPER religious, economically flat or declining, ethnically homogenous and culinarily unadventurous. It’s all deeply coded into those four letters.

Now if instead the story was set in 18th Century Prussia, I have no idea. I am fairly sure Prussia is near modern day Poland, but beyond that, it is a bit of a black hole. Do they hate the Russians, or are they an offshoot of the Russians, or are the both? Would they be likely to invade Germany, or does the modern concept of Germany non-existent. Would said invasion include muskets and cannons, or horses and swords? A reader in 1826 would know all of this innately; 200 years on, it is a complete mystery.

I have tried to solve this issue with a little app I build called GreatWorks. It is in beta, but completely usable and, of course, completely free. There is only one text, Paradise Lost by Milton, but it is very dense, so a perfect place to start.

The basic idea is that the text is split into sections bookmarked with an overview and summary. The goal is not to give away spoilers, but to give you the relevant context you need to understand the text. If there are any lines or passages you don’t fully grasp, you can underline them, and either Enrich (where you will be an explanation of the passage) or Add to Chat, where you add it to your conversation with the text. I found the latter version tremendously helpful, but I would be interested to hear your thoughts.

I added a feature where unusual words were defined (they’re underlined, you just have to hover over them), but I don’t think it did a very good job; many obvious words were included and quite a few archaic ones excluded. It does work well occassionally.

Finally, you can choose your Reader level at the start. I haven’t tuned this as well as I might have liked; I personally found the Complete Noob level the most enjoyable, although maybe I have been overrating my literary chops for years.

I would love it if you could have a play around and give me feedback: good, bad, ugly. What I am going to do with it next is completely open, but I don’t see it as a business or side-hustle, but rather something interesting and helpful. Share with your friends and family, and let me know if there is a book or poem that you would love to see.

Details:

GreatBooks App: https://great-books-rho.vercel.app/

Source Code: https://github.com/hwetherall/GreatWorks

Note: This app is built for screens, not mobile. Sorry.

title: Did you know?
text:
That you can go skiing in Argentina? I didn’t until today, but it makes sense.

They have the mountains from the Andes, they have the latitude, they have Patagonia, and perhaps most importantly, they have a deeply European culture that would clearly love skiing.

Is it the best snow in the world? According to my research, probably not. It won’t be Mt Baw Baw, but it won’t be Courchevel or Niseko either. But what it could be is an incredible holiday destination that I had never considered.

My Year 8 Geography project was on Argentina, and since then, I think there has always been a mild interest in the nation that waxes and wanes.

Not this year.

But one day.

title: Depth v Breadth
text:
Ky and I were discussing drug legalisation the other day, and it was quite an interesting conversation.

Colorado has fully legalised marijuana, and I think decriminalised psilocybin, better known as magic mushrooms. This means that where we live, you can go to your nearest dispensary (which there are PLENTY of), pick up a joint, and blaze it on the sidewalk next to a police officer without a care in the world.

As someone who is not a marijuana user, but pro-legalisation, it has been interesting to see how this approach has panned out. Frankly, there isn’t much to write about. Occasionally I smell it, and I am sure some people use it while driving (very bad behaviour) but overall, it doesn’t feel like a net negative to my day to day life.

The most interesting part of our conversation hinged on the trade off between depth and breadth. When marijuana became legal, the depth of how bad things could get was greatly nullified. Buying a pharmaceutical grade product with your credit card in a licensed establishment is far better than cash deals in dark alleys with shady individuals. Your friendly neighberhood “budtender” isn’t going to try and push some meth or coke on you either. But that depth is countered by breadth; on the margin, there is going to be some people who wouldn’t have imbided in marijuana while illegal, but now that they can pick some up round the corner, they do. As with many things, the range of outcomes related to that decision is broad, and undeniably some are quite bad.

There is another, similar story going on right now with online sports betting and prediction markets in the US. 20 years ago, unless you were in Vegas, the average 20 something Denverite who wanted to put a bet on the upcoming game had two options: a gentleman’s bet with their friend, or get into some illegal gambling ring with some truly unsavoury characters. Today, it’s a 5 minute task downloading an app.

The depth has been massively lifted. For those who were always going to gamble, being able to do it via DraftKings or Bet365 is far better than getting in deep with Big Tony’s poker game. However, there are MILLIONS of (mostly young, male) Americans who are now regularly betting on games, who would never have been involved in illegal gaming in the past. The breadth is staggering.

It’s a trade off for policy-makers, governments, and frankly individuals, at all levels. As with many things, there are no solutions, only tradeoffs.

title: Tap the sign
text:
I recently made a meme, so without any further ado, Look upon my works, ye mighty and despair:

I think it will be in semi-frequent rotation in the coming months, and was inspired by the recent missile strikes in Iran.

A more fun way of saying “two things can be true at once”, it is a powerful reminder that balance and perspective are wise companions.

Is bombing a sovereign nation without support from Congress or, frankly, the American populace a good idea?

Probably not.

Is the world a better place now that innocent civilians have had their homes and lives destroyed.

Certainly not.

But a world where terrorist-supporting radicals have a nuclear weapon is a severe downgrade from the current status quo. This is truer for Iranians more than anyone; the average North Korean has a terrible life in many parts due to their nutty leader’s uranium enriched arsenal. Were Iran able to successfully test a warhead; there is a non-zero chance the Israelis would turn Tehran to glass moments later.

That is a terrible outcome.

There is one thing I can be certain of going forward, and that is that I don’t know what will happen next. Neither do you. A handful of people in a handful of countries have a slightly clearer vision, but checking my subscriber list, none of you are senior generals, admirals, or cabinet members.

As always, we shall see in due course. Stay safe.

title: Subscriber Bonus!
text:
I was successful in the recent Champions League Ballot, which was a bit of a thrill. Sadly, I won’t be able to make it.

If you, or someone you know, would like a single ticket to Newcastle v Barcelona next Tuesday, let me know. You have 22 hours until my ballot window closes, and they go back on the general market.

First come first served!

title: Change of direction
text:
The last six months or so Ky and I have been grinding French on DuoLingo. It is fair to say our heart hasn’t really been in it.

So last night, we decided to pivot to Portuguese. Why, or por que, you may ask?

Great question, and the answer is that for us, right now, it is far more useful.

O’s nanny is Brazilian, and my associate at work is as well. That is two people I speak to 4-5 times a week who would really appreciate it if I just learned a little bit of their native tongue. Plus in September we are going to Portugal for a friend’s wedding, so a touch of immersion.

I think I will put together a little project to help me get there, I’ll share it later this week.

title: Roll Back
text:
I am in SF for work until Friday, and usually I stay with my pal the “Cashew King”. However, this time he had family in town, so I had to make my own last minute arrangements.

San Francisco is an expensive city, and as I had blown most of my travel budget on flights, so I ended up staying at the Orange Village Hostel.

Despite my wife’s initial suggestion, and my constant fear, this isn’t a homeless shelter. In fact, they don’t even allow SF residents to check in, which feels slightly illegal, but it is in a part of town which the NYT might describe as “vibrant”.

Nonetheless, a four-person dorm is still a four-person dorm. It is safe, clean, and has free wifi, and the other fellow across from me seems like neither a murderer or a thief.

Is it inconvenient? Slightly. Is it affordable? Yes. Is it a great chance to step out and do something different. Absolutely.

Plus four years of boarding school toughened me up; in year 10 the Four Dorm was the prized allocation!

Cozy Intimate!

title: Such a nice place
text:
I have been in SF for work, and today I had the privilege of being on Stanford campus. It is clear why so many people want to work there.

It’s such a lovely place. We are in the tail end of winter, but it’s still perfect weather. The campus is clean, tidy, and well maintained. It doesn’t have the classic beauty of a Harvard or Cambridge, but the sandy buildings have a charm.

If you’re at Stanford, all things considered, you’re probably doing pretty well. You’re likely young, you’re at a top school, you’re surrounded by friends and opportunities. People just felt alive and optimistic; it was nice to see.

Sadly I didn’t make it to the gift store, but perhaps next time.

title: Higher and higher
text:
My fact of the week came courtesy of an article I read on the flight over to SF, about the changing state of our northern neighbour. I’m not referring to Canada, but rather Wyoming.t

The Cowboy State (technically the Equality State) is to my knowledge the least populous state in the union. What it lacks in people it makes up for in the beautiful plains to the east and the towering mountains in the west. If you have seen the TV show Yellowstone, you know what I’m talking about.

If you had to guess what the primary industry in Wyoming is, a sensible answer could be tourism, or agriculture. In terms of export value, perhaps. But in terms of “income generated” for the individuals who “reside” in Wyoming, it’s actually dividends. Let me explain.

Wyoming has a few things that are highly desirable for wealthy people. It is beautiful and close to rich people activities, like skiing and fly fishing. It’s safe and private. And it doesn’t have any state income tax; in fact I don’t think they have many taxes at all. So if those things appeal to you and you’re a big dog, why not “reside” in Wyoming.

Which leads me to the fact of the day. The top 1% of households in Australia earn ~550k. The top 1% of American households earn 700k. And the top 1% of households in Teton County, Wyoming, earn 35m a year, or 466x the median state income.

You can see the appeal

title: Snowman
text:
On Friday it bucketed down with snow.

By Monday it will almost all be gone.

In that small window, someone made an enormous snow ball.

O and I were impressed!

title: All the gear
text:
There is a guy on YouTube I listen to while at the gym who give very solid workout advice. For those interested, check out Mike Israetel, founder of Renaissance Periodization and a sports and exercise scientist. He’s funny, knowledgeable, and very, very strong. He’s also super open about his own use of anabolic steroids to grow his muscles, while being very clear that he doesn’t recommend it for the vast majority of his listeners (me included).

Today I was doing the dishes and tuning in to his comment about 8 time Mr Olympia, Ronnie Coleman. For those of you who’s knowledge of bodybuilders starts and ends at Arnold Schwarzenegger, here is a photo:

The thing I took away from the show was that that at the very, very top, there is often a significant about of luck that is required to win, and you need to be good in areas that you originally didn’t think super important.

For Ronnie and elite body builders, it isn’t enough to be ultra disciplined with insane genetics and a fastidious training and diet plan. At his level, the big decider was how well your body could handle the “gear”, aka steroids.

For Ronnie, apparently he had this god-tier ability to maximize the main effects (muscle growth, rapid recovery etc.) while minimizing the side-effects (mood destabilization, health risks etc.). Apparently he could take almost super-human doses of this stuff for months and be, just, fine.

I was thinking about who else fits in this category. Keith Richards, guitar player for the Rolling Stones, lived a life that should have put him in the grave by by 27. Heroin, cocaine, tobacco, alcohol. Massive amounts, night after night, year after year. He turns 83 this year and I think still tours. How? He’s obviously rich enough to afford great healthcare, but honestly, its genetics.

Lebron James has played almost 1900 NBA games, and missed under 200 due to injury in that entire time. Add in his time with Team USA and the All Stars, and he has missed under 10% of games due to injury. For the first 15 years, that number was under 5%. The average league wide is a tad under 20%. Again, there is training and healthcare, but having elite recovery skills and durability is the different between a great player and one of the GOATs.

Finally, Warren Buffet. When he hit retirement age, his personal net worth was $19 billion. Not too shabby! But instead of retiring, he stuck with Berkshire Hathaway as CEO, and now, at 95, he has grown that to a tad under $150b. Yes, inflation. Yes, he’s good at what he does. But the number one reason that he is so loaded is that he lived for a really, really long time.

As the kids say, some people are just build different.

title: Fish pond
text:
There is a small lake at one of the larger parks near us, and sometimes I see people fishing there. I never see anyone catch anything, and I wonder if they’d have the same outcome throwing their lure into a bucket.

But walking past today, I saw a number of carp (?) jumping out of the water. So they’re definitely in there!

title: Bug
text:
I have been building this new tool for work, and the initial progress has been promising. Without going into too much detail, it pulls together a number of tools to do long running research; imagine you wanted to find out all about Brazil, and in particular the food scene in the lower half of the country, and how it has changed over time. Rather than doing a cursory search of 3-4 websites, this tool is iterative, and goes through 20k web searches, picking the best results, rejecting the bad ones, and iterating its search behavior in real time. It’s a really cool project, and the outputs are excellent.

However, the reason I am writing this at midnight is that after 3 hours of deep research, I was ALMOST done, and then the whole system crashed, and all that research disappeared.

Why? How?

Simple. In the applications 30k+ lines of code, there was a single line which said tier=0, which was meant to identify the target company as distinct from the rival competitors we were analysing. However, another part of the code base outlined the options as either 1, 2, or 3. If it saw a 0, then it would break the system.

A small issue, a big impact.

Fortunately, as my AI co-developer told me, “Expensive lesson, but at least it’s a cheap fix.”

title: A puzzle
text:
Imagine I have two children.

What are the odds that they’re both boys?

What are the odds if I tell you at least one is a boy?

And do the odds change if I tell you the boy’s name?

First person to return the correct answer gets a prize!

title: Something to do
text:
Today we woke to see our world blanketed in snow.

This is a rarity, and possibly the third time it has happened all winter. The season has been dreadful for snowsports lovers, i.e us.

As I have mentioned many times, I’m a fan of snow days. They don’t cause any severe downsides (we don’t drive much, our house is insulated, and our car is garaged), and it is a novelty and something nice to look at. Octavia LOVES these days.

With that said, today’s cold weather threw a few small, icicle sized spanners into the works:

 * While riding my Lime bike to the gym this morning, my hands were so frozen that I had to abort 3/4 of the way through, lest I be maimed with permanent frost bite. It took me about 15 minutes for them to feel normal again (note to self: buy gloves)
 * Some people like humid weather, others hate it. Same for cloudy weather, same for hot weather. But EVERYONE haters windy weather (with perhaps the exception of sailors and kite flyers). Last night’s gust pulled off my BBQ cover and now the Webber is 3 inches under snow. Great.
 * There is a much shorter list of things to do with a rambunctious toddler when the weather hits sub-zero. Costco trips in the afternoon are poor substitutes to visiting the playground.

I had a moronic geography teacher back in Year 9 who insisted that there was no such thing as good weather or bad weather, just consistent and inconsistent weather. He was also well known as Mr Dope-away. But maybe he was onto something…

title: Pre-Doping
text:
I have just ticked over halfway in my 8 week gym program, and starting to feel and see the benefits. All great stuff.

In my quest to become a certified Swole-dier, I was looking this morning at potentially adding a pre-workout supplement, and found something that had surprised me.

Back in 2010, I used to take this pre workout called Black Powder. It was AWESOME. I remember taking a scoop or two before ergs or rowing races and remember the tingles, hand shakes, and performance improvement vividly.

Turns out that it had a secret ingredient, 1-3 dimethylamylamine, aka DMAA. For those not up to date with their chemistrs, this is, as ChatGPT described it, “legal over the counter crack, lite”.

No wonder it worked!

Unsurprisingly, it has since been banned from pretty much all countries as a supplement, and WADA added it to their doping list.

A crazy time. For now, I think I’ll be sticking with a banana.

title: Choose wisely
text: There is a quote I like that goes “What a privilege it is to go to bed tired after spending the day pursuing a challenge of your own choosing”. It’s wordy, but packs a punch.

title: Over the coals
text:
As the clock struck noon, Newcastle had just scored to level things against Barcelona at the Nou Camp, and I was really starting to dream. I had been thinking about possible flight options, and was the step before firing up Skyscanner with card in hand.

Fast forward 45 minutes, and Newcastle limp off 7-2 losers. It turns out there are levels to this game.

A disappointing afternoon turned into a wonderful evening though, as a pal and I had a dad-daughter BBQ at Sloan’s Lake, easily Denver’s most picturesque park.

We fired up the public charcoal grill, cooked up a feast, looked over the mountains and played on the playground. We all had a blast, and can’t wait to go again soon.

Lord of the flame

title: Mix Up
text:
15 odd years ago, I decided to join my local football club, the Albert Park Falcons. The pride of VAFA D Grade, they wore the iconic red and blue stripes with pride. Why iconic? Read on and you’ll find out.

Back in the Wild West days of the internet, simple things like navigation were surprisingly hard. So on my first day of training, I looked on the home computer where training would be, threw my boots into a supermarket shopping bag, and hopped on my bike.

10 minutes later, I was there, and like usual, I was late. I quickly dumped my bike, jumped the fence, and joined in the warmup, which was kicking practice. I managed to get a few rather rusty kicks away before one of the coaches pulled me aside, and asked me who I was. I told him that I’d found out about the club online, was keen to join, and thought I’d come along to training.

It was then, to my horror that I realised my mistake.

The Albert Park Falcons and the Port Melbourne borough have grounds 3 minutes apart, and wear the same “iconic” strip. However, that is wear the similarities end. The Falcons are an amateur club playing in the lowest level of the VAFA, and according to Wikipedia, have never won a senior premiership. The Port Melbourne Boroughs are a professional football team playing in the VFL, and have won more premierships than any other club in the competitions history.

Turns out you can’t just jump the fence and join professional teams, but it still makes for a funny story.

Port Melbourne Albert Park

title: The week
text:
It’s Friday night, which is as good a time as ever to reflect on the week that has passed.

What went well:

 * The Dad-Daughter BBQ I organized was a smashing, albeit lightly attended, event on Wednesday
 * I made it to the gym 4x this week
 * O and I spent a ton of time together 1v1, which is always a treat
 * My boss seems very happy with how everything is going, so we survive another week!
 * The weather has been outstanding.

What wasn’t so great:

 * O has picked up something and today wasn’t herself, and then spewed up
 * My calendar sync didn’t work and I missed two social calls back to NZ, which I feel terrible about
 * It’s always a little quiet when Ky is away (she was away for two nights in Florida). I am glad she is back
 * I have a tendency to stay up a little too late when it’s just me.
 * My diet hasn’t been dialed in, and while I have seen some good gains in the gym, the scales went marginally the wrong way
 * The snow season is well and truly over; something that I’m reminded of every time I see my skis in the garage.

Overall, a great week!

title: FOTZ
text:
As of yesterday, our family are FOTZ, or Friends of the Zoo. Essentially, we got the season pass.

Being a member is a grand deal. It means we can pop in for two hours, see a few of the highlights, and then leave without any regrets. Want to see more Asian animals, and less reptiles? I mean, who in their right mind wants to skip the reptile house, but sure, we can do that.

Ky and O have been two days in a row, and today I joined them. It was packed, as all zoos are on warm Saturdays, and the highlight was definitely the stingray enclosure, where we could touch and even feed the rays.

A great morning.

title: Land of the Free
text:
One of the nice things about Western Civilization, and America in particular, is people are free to be who they want to be.

Case in point: today at the park I saw a furry scampering around. I know at least one of my readers is a furry expert, but for the uninitiated, furries are people who dress up as, imitate, and often identify as an animal other than human. They call this alter-ego, and I am dead serious, their fur-sona.

It’s not something I would probably do, but this young fellow walking around on all fours, with cat mask and tail firmly on, wasn’t hurting anyone. Good for them, I’m glad they’re enjoying their life!

For those more interested in this topic, I HIGHLY recommend my friends podcast episode on the topic: https://open.spotify.com/episode/0hk3qgUrp08bM4R4y6wUYo?si=qZ8YHLExQQCymGoRErMURA

title: Keepwatch
text:
With seven rounds left of the Premier League, there is still plenty to plenty to play for.

At the top of the table, two teams are left in the title race, although Arsenal are sitting very pretty. It really is theirs to lose, but they do have that reputation.

At the other end, 4-5 teams are desperately trying to avoid that last relegation spot (sorry Burnley and Wolves, the die is cast for you). Tottenham, West Ham, Nottingham Forrest and perhaps even Leeds are in the mix. It’s tight.

Finally, there is always a clattering for those super lucrative European places. Even clubs as low as 12th have a slim chance of making it.

It is so much to keep track of, so over the weekend, I build a tool that does it for me.

Keepwatch uses a combination of maths and LLMs to show users the probabilities of their favourite club’s outcome come the end of season. By simulating the last seven rounds 10k times using live odds, you can clearly see what chances you have of becoming champion, qualifying for Europe, or being relegated.

Where it gets interesting is you can use Chat or Kyle (which is inverted Chat, named after the famous inverted wingback, Kyle Walker) to test scenarios. For example, what happens if one of your star players gets injured? The LLM calculates the impact, updates the probabilities, and reruns the simulation. You can then see how certain events change outcomes; some in a big way.

As games are not zero sum, an injury to a player on another team can actually be a huge impact on your own, even if you don’t play them.

My personal favourite feature is the Deep Dive, where it takes one club and one scenario, runs the simulation, and then pinpoints the 2-5 games that matter MOST. It then searches the web to identify risks, opportunities, and things to watch. In a two minute read, it gives you everything you need to know for the final two months of the season. The ones I would recommend are Man City, Tottenham, and Newcastle to demo, but all of them provide interesting results.

If you’re not embarrassed by your first deployment, you have released too late, or so the saying goes. There are certainly bugs and corner cases and errors that exist, but I think it is halfway decent start. I hope you find it useful, and I would really love your feedback.

Unsurprisingly this application does work on Mobile, but isn’t optimized for it. Still to a PC if you can.

The links you need:

Keepwatch: https://road-to-europe.vercel.app/

Source code: https://github.com/hwetherall/road-to-europe

title: Prisoners of Geography
text:
My sister and her husband just got back from their holiday to Patagonia. I spoke with her today and it sounded like she has a great time.

One of the places that they visited was a Chilean port city called Valparaiso. In the 19th century, it was one of the wealthiest cities in the world, due to it being a major port as ships navigated from Asia and the west coast of America to Europe. All the large shipping companies set up offices there, and the wealth generated was turned into stunning building and architecture.

That all changed in 1914, when the Panama Canal was built. Now there was no need to navigate around the base of South America, you could just squeeze right through the middle.

Over the coming 11 decades, those shipping companies left, the wealth evaporated, and while the building are still beautiful, they have fallen into quite a state of disrepair. Similar stories abound in places like St Louis, Detroit, Liverpool, Geelong.

title: Novelty
text:
When Ky is away, I like to try and find new parks to take O to.

It’s something new for both of us, and a great way to spend the time.

title: Free Calendar
text:
For the past two-three years, I have had a Calendly subscription. Today, I cancelled it.

For the first year or two, it was really handy for work. I often had to organise meetings with clients and customers, and an automated Calendar Booking tool well and truly paid for itself.

But I haven’t used it for a year, and I’m staring at the prospect of pouring $150 down the drain.

It isn’t goodbye for ever, but it is certainly goodbye for now.

title: The Pig
text:
Easter is nearly upon us, and today while at the department store we decided to pick up a few goodies for our nanny. Two things surprised us.

In the Commonwealth, there would be dozens of different chocolate egg options. Bougie options. Budget options. White, milk, dark, Swiss style, Dubai style, salted, caramelised, chillied, hazlenutted. Maybe other stores are different, but the Sloan’s Lake Target was seriously lacking.

On the flip side, they had a bunch of Percy Pigs, the only lolly I actually quite enjoy. They’re usually only available at M&S in the UK, so that was a pleasant surprise.

title: Season Launch
text:
I have decided to pull on the boots for one last season of Australian Rules Football. At 34, the costs of playing full contact sports increases with every passing day.

Part of my motivation is that it will be a fun way to stay fit and make friends. But another part of it is leaving the game on a high.

I haven’t had a lot of luck in this sport. At high school we got battered, winning one game in our last two years (although our year 10 team was DOMINANT). At senior level, when I played in the reserves we missed out on finals, and when I played in the club level, we won the grand final, but I wasn’t selected for the grand final. As for college football, in the UK, we lost our varsity game to Cambridge, and the national championship was canned due to Covid. In Australia, we lost to a very good UWA in the final to come second at UniGames.

So a lot of Ls, a silver medal or two, and honestly, a lot of fun, good times, and happy memories. As mentioned in a previous post, the Denver Bulldogs are part of the Big Three (along with the Austin Crows and NY Magpies), and so if we’re going to go out on a high, this is the way to do it.

title: BBQ
text:
We went round for a BBQ at our friends’ place, and it was a touch of back home.

Big yard, snags sizzling, dogs yapping, kids playing, sunscreen lathered and drinks cold.

A perfect way to end the weekend.

title: Vacation
text:
So begins my four day break, starting this Tuesday.

One of my closest friends is in town, and with many, many hours separating us, I wanted to make the most of the time we have together.

It has been a while since I have had a staycation, but I can’t wait for it.

title: Exploration
text:
Some of my oldest and dearest friends have come to visit us in Denver, and so far it has been an absolute blast.

Our day started in the mountains, where we hiked up to Lost Lake, got some snaps, and then drove into the scenic town of Nederlands.

Our drive back included two compulsory stops; a trip to Starbucks (drive through, of course) and a visit to the range to let off a few rounds of ammunition.

Lachy and I don’t have a huge amount of experience with handguns; in fact, we don’t have any at all.

Fortunately, a tiny old lady in her 70s was next to us at the range, and she kindly helped us to both load, cock, and then unleash the full power of our pistol. Between shots, she told us that over the weekend she had put up some shelving, and due to her age her hands were still a bit sore. Because of her fragile, she said she could only use her smaller pistol today at the range!

Only in America.

title: Cultural experiences
text:
For years, I never really knew what to do or show people when they came to visit Denver.

Now I know.

Today’s adventures included a trip to Bass Pro (an enormous outdoor and recreation store) which had a free gun range, hundreds of taxidermied animals, a bass filled aquarium, and a staff member called Doug who knew almost everything there is to know about bow hunting. After that, we visited my personal favourite Mexican restaurant, a dispensary, and the REI before heading to Aurora to test drive the CyberTruck and its full self-driving capabilities. Back home, delicious dinner, cold beer. Bed.

Another great day.

title: Return of the Ronnie
text:
Yesterday we had a welcome surprise: our Roomba, fresh from his recent repair, had returned.

I have never returned something to be fixed under warranty before, and I didn’t know what to expect. But it’s working, mopping, and tidying our kitchen as we speak.

So that’s nice.

title: ATX
text:
We are currently an hour out of Austin, Texas; enjoying the Easter Break. How we got here is quite the question.

Many many years ago, I had the good fortune to be part of an org called 180 Degrees Consulting, which selected college students, gave them a sprinkling of training, and sent them off to do pro-bono consulting for NFPs and charities. It was, and still is, a great idea: students get hands on experience, cash strapped orgs get some help, and consultancies get a sneak peak at potential talent. In my case, they clearly didn’t like what they saw, but that’s another story.

In my second year with 180DC, I was invited to be part of their global summit, which was hosted in Lima, Peru. This WAS one of the great weeks of my life.

On it, I met a young Stanford woman called Ashleigh, who was on our team tasked with assisting an empanada company that hired Peruvians with Special Needs.

Many years later, when Ky and I first moved to Denver, we were introduced by Ashleigh to one of her best friends from college and her husband. Over the ensuing years, Sydney and Jason have become fast friends, particularly when we became parents a month or two apart from one another.

This is a long way of explaining why this weekend we are on a huge ranch (with taxidermied bear rugs!) over the Easter break with Ashleigh and her husband, our Denver Pal’s (and their daughter) and the three of us.

All because I signed up for a student club on a whim 11 years ago.

title: It’s different down here
text:
Regular readers know I am currently in the great state of Texas, and by golly, they do things differently down here.

Take, for example, the nearby coffee shop, which is “Christ centred”. All of their options had biblical names, from the Red Sea to the John 4:10. I got the Bean Revival; it was actually quite good!

Once we got our dose of (holy) bean water, we picked up what felt like a metric half ton of BBQ for lunch, which was quite the treat.

In the afternoon, we loaded into the ATVs and explored the ranch, where we saw a ton of deer and gazelles, bouncing around the plains. But it was the next door neighbors, with their pet giraffes, that really stole the show.

I’m a big fan of the Lone Star state; it certainly has character!

title: Another Classic
text:
In the late 80s, fans of Australian Rules football were enthralled by two powerhouses: the Hawthorn Hawks and the Geelong Cats. Hawthorn had the better team overall, but the Cats had Gary Ablett, widely considered one of the greatest to have ever played the game. You don’t get the nickname “God” if you’re making up the numbers.

Almost 30 years later, when I was a teenager, the rivalry kicked off again. Geelong and Hawthorn played a number of grand finals and regular season games that went down as instant classics. Both teams were stacked with talent and characters, not least Gary Ablett’s son, Gary Ablett junior. As someone with a Hawks supporting father while going to school in Geelong, it was hard to get away from all the hype.

With games drawing enormous crowds, the AFL decided to make it an annual tradition to always be played on Easter Monday, a public holiday in Australia. While I haven’t followed the league closely for a while, I checked this morning to see the score.

Hawthorn 92 – Geelong 91.

Another classic.

title: Reset
text:
After a pretty full on past week, where we hosted friends in Denver, then lived it up in Austin, I needed to give my digestive system a break.

I find a 24 hour fast is a great place to start. It sounds intimidating, but as long as you drink plenty of water, stay occupied, and realise that those hunger pains will subside in a few minutes time, it’s totally manageable.

title: The other list
text:
There is a guy on YouTube who does short form interviews with billionaires and other high net worth individuals on the street. I’m unsurprised he has grown his channel to 20 million subs.

One particular big dog gave a piece of advice I have been mulling over for the past few days. He looked around a crowded London street and asked “how many people here do you think has a to-do list, everyone?”

Probably not everyone, but yeah, a good number of people keep a to do list either online, on paper, or if they’re foolish, in their head.

But how many people have a “not to do list”, of things they’re NOT going to do. I haven’t thought of ever creating one, but it makes sense. There are things that we know steal our time, energy, attention, capacity, and joy. Putting them on a not to do list would be probably just as helpful as “do the laundry”.

To stoke a bit of crowd participation, reply to this email with your suggestion on what should be on my “not to do” list and I’ll commit to it for 72 hours and write about my experience.

title: Margins
text:
After work, the three of us went on a hunt for some Bubble Ice Tea. I’m not one to ever go out and buy one, but I will always have a cheeky slurp of Ky’s when she gets one. After initially being quite dubious of their consistency, I have a tender spot in my heart for the little bubbles now.

I don’t think I would enjoy running a Bubble Ice Tea parlour, but I sure would enjoy being an investor in one, should the opportunity arrive. The margins on those things must be incredible, and while the one in Denver was quiet, there have been quite a few in Melbourne, particularly near RMIT, which had literal lines round the block. Someone is doing very, very nicely out of that.

title: The S Curve
text:
Fans of innovation will be familiar with the S Curve. For my more visual readers, it looks something like this.

The best example is the phone. It took decades to get from the brick phone to the Nokia to the iPhone. Then, in the space of 7-10 years it went from cool to incredible. And now, it’s flat. Each year Apple brings out a new phone that is slightly faster, slightly thinner, slightly better photos, translation options now for ancient Assyrian etc. The days of week long build ups to the new launch are over.

It’s going to be tough for Apple. The glory days of the iPad + iPod nano + iPhone all simultaneously crushing it are over. The Apple Watch was a banger, but the headset has been a disaster. Their laptops still continue to be world class and wildly expensive, so the future remains bright. Ish.

PS I was inspired to write this after updating my iOS software from 18 -> 26, and seeing almost zero improvements, and a noticeable but small lag.

title: Bass Couch
text:
Last night, I went to somewhere I haven’t been in years: a nightclub. Let me explain.

I met a law professor many moons ago during my time as a UATX Fellow, better known on this platform as the Incel Academy, and we have been fast friends since day one. What I admire about Eric is his breadth of interests; academically, he’s working on research from space governance to blockchain regulation. Outside of the classroom, he’s an avid skier, wine collector, outdoor explorer, and EDM connoisseur. So when one of his favourite acts was in town last night, he decided to invite me along.

The Bass Couch is a side section with the Black Box nightclub. It’s a tiny little alcove with a couch, enough space to stretch out and boogie, and close enough to see the DJ work his magic. For older patrons (I’m 34, a decade younger than Professor Alston), it’s nice to be able to enjoy your favourite acts that come on at 12, while not having to be on your feet from 9pm.

Last night’s fare was Drum and Base, something I’m not particularly enmeshed in, but I’m always up to try new things. The sound system was clearly optimised for pumping heaving bass waves into the crowd, which was a real eclectic mix. At least on person in their 60s was out there getting their groove on.

I’m really glad I went, and also glad that I could still be home in bed by 1:30. Even with the Bass Couch, that’s late enough for me these days.

title: Back to the Bar
text:
I ran out of body wash the other day, and while doing my research on what a suitable replacement could be, decided that it was probably best to invest in bar, rather than liquid. Obviously if you’re buying soap for a communal gym shower, then the bar is a bit unpleasant, but that is far from my particular use case.

I don’t know if I fully believe that a bar of soap lasts between 2-10x more than the liquid, but I’m open to believing it. I will use it for the first time tomorrow, and keep a track of its outcome. For $9, even if it lasts the same amount of time as the body wash, I think I am still saving money.

This feels like a rather mundane topic, so I’ll spice it up with a little throwback to a business idea I had 10+ years ago, inspired by a Melbourne Company called Free Water. The idea was simple; distribute free water at festivals and uni events, paid for by sponsors. So you’d get a free bottle (cost < $1), be pleasantly surprised it is free, ask why, and then rehydrate while reading a little bit about the sponsor. I remember being interested in what the sponsor was doing, rather than feeling forced into it in the way YouTube or TV adds invade your space.

My idea grew off of this. The basic pitch was distributing hand soap to communities with low access to hygiene in places like Sub-saharan Africa and South Asia. My mother has been a lifetime advocate for the power of handwashing, but if you only have water and $2 to your name, it’s a lower priority. Soap is borderline free to make in large batches, but distributing them without the power of markets is really difficult. Having a third party, a sponsor, come in and do the paying solved that issue.

I didn’t give it the world of thought, but if I remember correctly, it felt quite clever. For advertisers/sponsors, you were getting your clients to hold your advertisment, in their hands, a few times a day, with at least a speck of gratitude. That feels more valuable than a 30 second ad on TV where people are looking the other way. Plus it has a feel good factor, which large corporate wouldn’t love to be able to get a bit of marketing upside out of their “giving back” programs? For the recipients, hand hygiene directly helps solve one of the most pressing issues in the developing world, a problem that we have had a working, affordable solution for decades.

Maybe I should run this through the Innovera System and see how much legs this idea has, 10 years on.

title: The list
text:
A few messages, I wrote about trialling a 72 hour Not To Do list, crowdsourced from readers. You can read it HERE.

So far, the list of Not To Do’s are:

 * Not to speak to my sister
 * No wearing headphones while others are in the room
 * No doom scrolling
 * No small talk
 * No second helpings

It starts at 7am Tuesday and concludes 7am Friday. Any additional requests will be added, within reason.

title: Lid
text:
I have been thinking of growing my hair out a bit longer. The downside is that during the growing phase, it can get a bit untidy. I was looking a bit wooly, so off to the shearing shed I went.

Except this time, it was at a friend’s place. One of Ky’s good buddies is an (unlicensed) hairdresser, and she gave both of us a quick trim, and dinner! Not a bad way to spend your Wednesday evening.

title: Scramble
text:
This afternoon I got off work a little early and headed into the mountains with my buddy Eric, formerly of Bass Couch fame. He is an avid scrambler, and not of the egg variety.

All scrambling is hiking, but not all hiking is scrambling. The big difference is, to borrow Eric’s academic vernacular, the degrees of freedom allowed. In hiking, most of the time you walk from point A to point B, usually following a path or trail. In scrambling, you get out of your car, head into the wilderness, and hope that in a few hours you will find your way back.

It was great fun. Part of it was scaling rocks and uneven terrain, dodging poison ivy and prickly cacti. Part of the fun was all the finds along the way; I came home with two souvenirs, a deer leg and a small antler. Occi was understandably thrilled with the finds. But most of the fun was talking smack with my buddy. There wasn’t much chatting for the first hour as it was essentially all uphill, with limited oxygen to go around. But the next two hours exploring the ridge and hacking our way down was far less respiratorily demanding, and we were able to chat about everything from free markets in Nicaragua to time-boxing one’s life throughout their 20s and 30s.

I came back to Denver with a few scrapes, a few stings, a touch of sunburn, some bones, and a heart full of joy. I’ll be up there again.

title: Affordable luxury
text:
For my entire life, I’ve worn either Bonds underwear (if I’m lucky) or random/generic brand (if I’m not). This isn’t necessarily an unwise or misfortunate life path, those 4 for $20 deals really are a steal!

However, the 50% off sale at Tommy John had led me down a new road. A pair of boxers isn’t exactly what you would describe as “luxury”, but there is certainly an increase in feel and comfort. Maybe not enough to justify at full price, but if I’m going to wear something everyday, a small upgrade goes a long way.

title: Messi
text:
On Saturday, a buddy and I went down to Mile High Stadium to see the Colorado Rapids take on the absurdly named Club Internacional de Fútbol Miami, better known as Inter Miami.

Usually the Rapid’s play at the historic Dick’s Sporting Goods Arena (“the Anfield of the Rockies”), but this was no ordinary game. The reason myself, friend, and 75k other fans packed into the city’s NFL stadium is due to one man: Messi.

Lionel Messi has been, for my entire life, on of the two best players in the world. In any city in the world, from Albania to Zambia, you’ll still almost certainly see someone walking around with an FC Barcelona shirt on, with a 10 and a Messi on the back.

At 38, his prime years are behind him, and he’s decided to see out the twilight of his career playing in Miami. This has resulted in what amounts to a travelling circus when the team is on the road, as thousands of fans clamour to see the GOAT* in person.

My buddy and I had a great time. We saw Messi score two goals, including the curled winner that brought the house down, but also some scintillating play from the Rapids; 2-3 losers on the day, but a brave efforts nonetheless.

The Rapids, clearly keen to cash in on their newest fans, offered everyone attending two free tickets to their next game at Dick’s, so maybe the family will go check them out as they take on Real Salt Lake City or AC San Antonio.

Great seats, great day!

Overall, a sparkling way to spend a Saturday afternoon.

*Real ballers recognise CR7

title: Fast
text:
I’m currently 2/3 of the way through a 36 hour fast, and going strong. Around 6pm, I didn’t think I would make it, and was enduring waves of hunger and headaches. Why was I doing this when I had half a fridge full of food!

But our bodies are built to fast, and after your body transitions into a fasted state, the hunger dissipates, a sense of calm descends, and a feeling of power that it really is mind over matter.

That said, I am very much looking forward to breaking the fast at 12pm tomorrow!

title: 99% invisible
text:
The quietest place in Manhattan is, unsurprisingly, Central Park.

It makes sense, the only place where there aren’t cars, but it’s not just that. I learned today that the park is actually sunken, and slightly walled, so all of the sound from the city hits the walls and deflects up, while the visitors in the park are lower, and can enjoy some much needed peace.

title: Lunch break
text:
The past two days I have been fortunate to have lunch with friends. Not over zoom, not over business. Honest to god, hour blocked in the calendar, food, drinks, and a good catch up.

As someone who works from home, it’s nice to get out of the house, be social, have someone else do the cooking, and enjoy the sun. I can’t do it everyday, but I certainly think I’ll try to incorporate it into my schedule more often.

title: Neighbours
text:
Our neighbours are moving to Portland this weekend. We’re going to miss them.

Sophia and Jackson are the dream couple to live next to. They have a delightful dog called Edie, who O adores, and regularly come round with baked goods and gifts. They help take out our rubbish if we forget, and vice versa. Plus they’re always up for a chat.

They’re expecting their first child in October, so it’s understandable why they’re heading back to their native Oregon. This evening we had them round for dinner, and while we may never see them again (I hope we do!) it’s been a great time.

title: ANZAC Day Pt 1
text:
The 25 of April is ANZAC Day in Australia and New Zealand. It is our equivalent to Remembrance Day or Memorial Day.

In 1915, the Australian and New Zealand Army Corp landed at Gallipoli, a rugged cliff faced area in modern day Turkey. The Ottoman Empire had allied with Germany in World War 1, and heavily mined the Dardanelles, a narrow strait between the Mediterranean and the Black Sea. As anyone with a petroleum powered car knows, if a rival controls an important waterway, you’re going to have a bad time.

Winston Churchill, long before he won a Nobel Peace Prize or became Prime Minister, was the Lord Admiral of the British Navy. His mission was to conduct a beach landing in the straits, take control of the waterway, and allow Allied ships to pass through. Sadly, it was his greatest failure.

There is an urban legend that a Turkish soldier swam out and moved some signals, so that when the allied boats landed, instead of arriving at a gentle beach head, they landed at the wrong place, a few miles away. This wrong place was hilly, steep, and surrounded by Turkish machine gunners. If you have seen the intro of Saving Private Ryan, you’ll have a fair idea of what it was like. Of the 16k men who were part of the mission on April 25th, two thousand of them were dead within the first 24 hours.

After eight months of complete and utter failure, where the Allied forces were unable to get more than a few hundred feet up the cliffs, the decision was made to evacuate. Amazingly, the evacuation was considered one of the most effective military manoeuvres by either side in the entire conflict, with almost zero casualties sustained.

Tomorrow I’ll finish off the rest of the story, and how we commemorated the day today in Denver.

title: ANZAC pt2
text:
After explaining the history of ANZAC Day yesterday, today I wanted to share our experience of the service we attended.

Traditionally, an Anzac Day service starts at dawn. I haven’t been to many that have though; when I was a child I remember going to the War Memorial in Melbourne and remembering it being very cold and not knowing the words to God Save the Queen.

At school, we were probably the ONLY school in Australia not to take Anzac Day off, but I do remember the ceremony very clearly. The headmaster would read out the names of every student and teacher who had died in conflict, starting at the Boer War, working through the two world wars, Vietnam, Korea. East Timor, Iraq and Afghanistan. The whole exercise takes around 20 minutes. It was a profoundly depressing, but also moving, part of the ceremony.

Our experience in Denver was very different. Firstly, it was the first ceremony I have been to that emphasised the Australian AND New Zealand contribution; after all, it isn’t AAC or NZAC day. Back in Aus, and I’m sure it was the same in NZ, the other country was a bit of an after thought.

Even more unique was the addition of a THIRD nation into the ANZAC Day ceremony, the good ol’ US of A. Not only was the Stars and Stripes hanging next to the two Australian and New Zealand Flags, but we all had to got to sing the US anthem as well. Just like my experience as a kid 25+ years ago, I didn’t know all the words.

I’m glad we went. After the service we had a chance to catch up with a few members of the diaspora and enjoy a cold one. Sadly we left before the two-up began, but there will always be next year.

Lest we forget.

title: Christmas Gifting
text:
I came across an interesting story the other day of how Dave Ramsey celebrates Christmas with his employees.

For those who don’t know the name, Dave Ramsey is best known for providing information and help on personal finance. He has had his share of criticism, most because his techniques and suggestions are aimed at, and work tremendously well for, the bottom 50% of earners. Refusing to go into debt for anything bar housing, paying off said debt as fast as possible, and focussing on low cost index funds and bonds aren’t glamorous, high return, or sophisticated. They won’t make you fabulously wealthy, but if you stick with them, you’ll almost always, after some time, be comfortable. For the millions of Americans (and Brits, and Australians, and New Zealanders) who feel like they’re drowning in debt, this is a wonderful alternative.

The other thing that Mr Ramsey is well known for is his Christian faith. Not really my cup of tea, but I respect that he really puts his money where his mouth is. According to the story, each Christmas his organisation buys $10 million in bad debt, which on secondary markets you can pick up for as little as 2.5c on the dollar. These are people’s blown out credit cards, delinquent car notes, overdue medical bills etc. The banks have given up on them, so selling those debts for pennies is better than nothing.

Once they have the debt in their possession, Dave then asks his employees to call the holders of the debt, tell them it has been cleared, and wish them a merry Christmas. Apparently the staff LOVE it.

Initially, I thought I’d prefer to have the cash myself, but on second thoughts, I think the whole idea is super lovely. The fact that the whole office does it together, the gratitude you get throwing people financial life rafts, the “spirit of Christmas” vibe. I think it’s really neat.

What I liked most was the EFFICIENCY of his gifting. We have all heard stories oh how for every $100 you give to charity X, only $7 or so ends up digging the well in Africa. Meanwhile, this method allow you to buy $10m of debt for $250k, essentially 40x your contribution.

Clever stuff.

title: High Score
text:
It wasn’t planned, but it certainly feels like Australia Week. A few days ago I did a deep dive on ANZAC Day. Today, I want to indulge in the most common Australian conversation: complaining about fuel prices.

Right now, the average fuel price in Aus is $1.98 a liter, or $7.5 a gallon. That is ~$5.37 USD, so definitely higher than average.

The reason I bring this up is that today we refuelled our car from practically zero, and hit the $50+ marker for the first time.

And you know what? So what. Life is too short to get worried about things outside of our control, and fuel prices are one of them.

title: Price’s Law
text:
There is a famous phenomenon that emerges across a whole host of domains called Price’s Law. Put simply, the square root of all inputs provide 50% of the outputs.

This can be seen everywhere; a small number of artists on Spotify make up most of the listens, a small number of Premier League players score most of the goals, a small number of universities produce most of the breakthroughs. A handful of stars produce most of the light in the universe, a handful of rivers hold most of the water. The list goes on, and on, and on.

In small groups, it is hard to recognise Price’s Law. In a legal firm of 25, it makes sense that the 5 partners generate most of the money and output; the other 20 associates, paralegals, and HR just support them. But when it scales to global outcomes, like music or literature, it becomes brutal.

Take Spotify. There are almost 10 million artists on the platform.

 * Of those, 81% have less than 1000 monthly streams.
 * Around 200k have 10k+ streams, earning them “professional” status and activating royalties (you don’t get paid at all before then, something I just learned).
 * Under 1% of all artists have over 100k streams per month.
 * Taylor Swift has over 120 MILLION streams per month.

A single person, Taylor Swift, probably has more streams per month than the bottom 40% of artists on the platform. Price’s Law says that 50% of all streams are driven by the square root of 10 million, which is a bit over 3000 artists, or 0.03% of the platform.

Price’s Law isn’t just a fun quirk to bring up at trivia night. I have been thinking how it might impact market based economies, where a small number of hyper industrious, innovative, intelligent, and capable people can produce the majority of economic output, leaving a huge majority of people fighting over the scraps. I think this idea, combined with Tomas Piketty’s famous r > g statement, deserves some further thought.

A great potential effort post.

title: Shattered Shield
text:
I have been a fan of OtterBox for a while, the “military grade” phone case manufacture. It’s been coming my iPhone safe and unblemished for over three years. It always had my back.

Until it didn’t. After daily droppings on concrete, eventually the case gave up, and I have little option but to “raw dog” my phone.

There is something elegant about the feel of raw metal and glass, but it isn’t a long term solution.

The replacement case arrives tomorrow.

title: End of the Lime
text:
After seven years of operations, the city of Denver has decided not to renew Lime’s license to operate their scooters. I for one am going to miss them.

I think micro-mobility is great, and Lime is my second favourite provider (Neutron Mobility, being the GOAT for their $60 unlimited rides a month offering).

There is no denying there are issues with the scooters; people fall off them drunk, they clog up the footpath, lime green isn’t everyone’s favourite colour. But being able to scoot from a to b for only a few dollars is such a great option to have.

Denver may be a little tidier without them. But it will also be a little less fun.

Lime, we are going to miss you.

title: Training #1
text:
The new season with the Denver Bulldogs has kicked off, and I made it down for my first training this afternoon.

It’s been a while since I laid boot to leather, and sadly it isn’t like riding a bike. About 40% of my kicks went in “unconventional” directions, but it was still good to get back out there.

Looking forward to round 2.

title: The curse of the canvas
text:
I have inherited a canvas Barbour jacket from my late grandfather. It wasn’t formally in the will for me to have it, but it fits, and it’s in my house, so I guess it is mine for now.

It’s a lovely colour and very well made, but there is a major issue; it smells very much like canvas.

Personally, I quite like the scent; it reminds me of my childhood and pony club. But I can understand that the earthy notes can be a bit much.

The short term solution is to leave it outside until it cleans itself up. As for a long term solution, I have no idea. I’d hate to give it up, but we can’t have our whole wardrobe smelling like a barn.

Here is hoping ChatGPT will have the answer.

title: Mogged
text:
I wandered past our local pub this evening and saw a young guy shuffling some cards in front of what I assumed was his date. The weather wasn’t kind so it kept strolling, but if I had to guess, he was mid or pre magic magic truck.

Bold strategy.

Many, many, many years ago, I wasted an immeasurable amount of time practicing illusions. It’s a niche interest, so unsurprisingly I thought I was pretty good, pretty quick. As with all false illusions, this one crumbled fast.
